CodeFormatExample
4733-digit47 × 10³ = 47 kΩ

R47R-notation0.47 Ω
01AEIA-96100 × 1 = 100 Ω
68CEIA-96499 × 100 = 49.9 kΩ

Section 1D Electrical & Electronics

Wire & Ampacity

AWG sizing, metric equivalents, current capacity, and DC resistance. With voltage drop calculator.
AWG Reference TableTAB. 1D-1
AWG mm² ⌀ (mm) Chassis (A) Power (A) Ω / 1000 ft Ω / km
Chassis wiring assumes free-air, single conductor. Power transmission assumes 700 circular mils per amp, conservative for long runs and bundled conductors. Derate for ambient temperature and bundling.
Voltage Drop CalculatorFIG. 1D-1
Voltage Drop—
% of Source—
Voltage at Load—
Power Dissipated in Wire—
NEC Ampacity (Table 310.16 · Copper)TAB. 1D-2
Allowable ampacity for not more than three current-carrying conductors in raceway, cable, or earth. Base condition: 30°C / 86°F ambient. Apply correction factors below for other conditions.
AWG / kcmil 60°C (TW, UF) 75°C (THW, THWN) 90°C (THHN, XHHW-2)
NEC 240.4(D): branch-circuit overcurrent protection limits 14 AWG to 15A, 12 AWG to 20A, 10 AWG to 30A regardless of the column value. Higher-temp columns are used for derating, not for raising the breaker.
Ampacity Correction CalculatorTOOL 1D-2
Base Ampacity (Table 310.16)—
Ambient Correction Factor—
Bundling Adjustment Factor—
Corrected Ampacity—
240.4(D) Overcurrent Limit—
This is a working reference, not an inspection-grade calculation. Confirm against the latest NEC and your AHJ before doing actual electrical work.
Section 2A Mechanical

Tap & Drill

Drill sizes for tapping at 75% thread engagement, standard practice. Imperial and metric.
Thread Pitch / TPI Tap Drill Decimal in Metric mm Clearance
Clearance drill = close fit for screw passage. For free fit, go one step up.
Counterbore for Socket-Head Cap Screws (SHCS)TAB. 2A-2
ScrewCB Dia (in)CB Depth (in)Clear Dia (in)Notes
Counterbore depth = head height + 0.005-0.020" recess for the screw head to sit flush or slightly below. Clear dia is for the screw shank pass-through.
Counterbore for Button-Head Cap Screws (BHCS)TAB. 2A-3
ScrewCB Dia (in)CB Depth (in)Clear Dia (in)
Button heads have a domed top. Counterbore can recess the flange or leave the dome proud — depends on the design.
Countersink for Flat-Head ScrewsTAB. 2A-4
Screw82° CS Dia (in)90° CS Dia (in)100° CS Dia (in)Notes
82° is the inch standard (UNC / UNF), 90° is metric DIN 7991. 100° appears on some aerospace fasteners.
Reamer Prep SizesTAB. 2A-5
Reamer SizeHand Reamer DrillMachine Reamer Drill
Leave material for the reamer: hand reaming wants 0.003-0.005" undersize; machine reaming on rigid setups wants 0.005-0.015" depending on diameter. Hard materials want less stock.
Dowel Pin Press-Fit HolesTAB. 2A-6
Dowel DiaPress-Fit HoleSlip-Fit HoleNotes
Press-fit hole is undersized for permanent or semi-permanent retention. Slip-fit hole is on the same nominal size for jig and fixture alignment.
Knurling ReferenceTAB. 2A-6
Knurl TypePatternUse
Straight (axial)Parallel to shaft axisEasy push-fit grip; retains rotation
Diagonal (helical)Single helix, ~30° from axisOne-direction grip; tool handles
Diamond (crossed)Two opposing helixesMost common; gripping surfaces, knobs
Concave (relief-cut)Knurl ground into a relief grooveHidden detail under a thumb pad
Pitch (TPI)Diametral PitchCrest-to-CrestBest For
Coarse (12-14 TPI)P12, P14~1.8-2.1 mmLarger knobs, coarse gripping
Medium (16-21 TPI)P16, P20~1.2-1.6 mmGeneral-purpose; instrument knobs
Fine (25-35 TPI)P25, P30~0.75-1.0 mmSmall parts, precision instruments
Extra fine (40+ TPI)P40< 0.65 mmMicro adjustments, vernier knobs
Bump (forming) knurling pushes material; cut (CNC) knurling removes material. For bump knurling, set the stock diameter to (nominal − pitch / 2) to allow the metal to flow up into the pattern; the finished diameter ends up at the nominal. Cut knurling does not change the diameter. Use coolant for bump knurling on steel; aluminum and brass knurl dry with no issue. The depth of knurl on a finished part is typically half the pitch.
Special Thread Forms (ACME, Buttress, Square)TAB. 2A-7
Thread FormProfile AngleUse
UN / ISO (standard fastening)60° symmetricGeneral fasteners, bolts, machine screws
ACME (general purpose lead screw)29° symmetricLead screws, vises, jack screws; trapezoidal teeth
Stub ACME29° (shallower depth)Where full-depth ACME is too deep; high-load lead screws
Buttress (asymmetric)7° / 45°Unidirectional high-load applications; presses, vises with one-way force
Square0° (perpendicular flanks)Highest efficiency; jacks, valve stems; hard to manufacture
Whitworth (BSW)55° symmetric, roundedLegacy British thread; still found in older machinery
Trapezoidal (ISO 2904)30° symmetricEuropean equivalent of ACME; "Tr" designation
Power-transmission threads (ACME, buttress, square) trade self-locking for efficiency. Square threads are the most efficient (~85-95% with good lubrication) but are difficult to manufacture and weak at the root. ACME is the common compromise: ~50-70% efficient, robust, easier to cut. Buttress threads handle massive one-direction force (the 7° flank carries load; the 45° flank only provides clearance), used in artillery breech blocks, hydraulic press platens, and bottle caps (yes, plastic bottle threads are buttress-derived).
Section 2B Mechanical

Feeds & Speeds

Starting points for milling and drilling. Always verify on scrap before committing to the part.
InputsFIG. 2B-1
Starting ParametersTAB. 2B-1
SFM—
Spindle Speed—
Chip Load / Flute—
Feed Rate—
Recommended DOC—
SFM Reference (Mill / Drill)TAB. 2B-2
MaterialHSS SFMCarbide SFMNotes
G-code Reference (Common Codes)TAB. 2B-3
CodeFunctionGroup / Notes
G00Rapid positioning (non-cutting move)Motion · modal; max federate
G01Linear interpolation (cutting move)Motion · modal; uses F feedrate
G02 / G03Circular interpolation CW / CCWMotion · modal; needs I/J center offsets or R radius
G04Dwell (pause)Non-modal; specify time with P or X
G17 / G18 / G19Select XY / XZ / YZ planePlane · modal; affects arc interpretation
G20 / G21Inch / millimeter unitsUnits · modal; set at start of program
G28Return to machine homeNon-modal
G40 / G41 / G42Cutter compensation off / left / rightComp · modal; required with D tool offset
G43 / G49Tool length offset on / offComp · modal; specify H register
G54 - G59Work coordinate systems 1-6WCS · modal; G55 = "second vise" etc.
G80Cancel canned cycleCycle · modal
G81Simple drill cycleCycle · modal; rapid down to R, feed to Z, rapid up
G82Drill cycle with dwellCycle; counterbore / spot face
G83Peck drilling cycleCycle; deep holes; Q is peck depth
G84Tapping cycleCycle; synchronized spindle + feed
G90 / G91Absolute / incremental positioningPosition · modal
G98 / G99Return to initial / R-plane after canned cycleReturn · modal
CodeFunction
M00Program stop (operator restart)
M01Optional stop (only if switch enabled)
M02 / M30End of program (M30 rewinds)
M03 / M04 / M05Spindle on CW / CCW / off
M06Tool change (with T preselect)
M07 / M08 / M09Mist coolant / flood coolant / coolant off
M19Spindle orientation (for tool change)
M97 / M98 / M99Local subroutine / external subroutine / return
G-codes specify geometry and motion; M-codes specify auxiliary functions. Modal codes stay active until cancelled by another code in the same group; non-modal codes act once. The major Fanuc dialect is the de facto standard, but Mazak, Haas, Heidenhain, and Siemens all have small variations. Always check the controller manual for non-standard codes (G15 polar mode, G68 coordinate rotation, etc.). The first line of a program typically sets: G17 G20/G21 G40 G49 G80 G90 — plane, units, no comp, no offsets, no cycle, absolute.

Section 3C Software

HTTP Status Codes

Standard HTTP response codes with categories and brief descriptions.
Common HTTP HeadersTAB. 3C-2
HeaderDirectionUse
AcceptRequestMedia types client prefers (e.g., application/json)
Accept-EncodingRequestCompression algorithms (gzip, br, zstd)
Accept-LanguageRequestPreferred languages (en-US, en;q=0.9)
AuthorizationRequestCredentials; format depends on scheme (Basic, Bearer, etc.)
Cache-ControlBothCaching directives (no-cache, max-age=3600, private)
Content-EncodingResponseCompression applied (gzip, br, deflate)
Content-LengthBothBody size in bytes
Content-TypeBothMedia type (application/json; charset=utf-8)
CookieRequestCookies set by server (name=value; other=value)
Set-CookieResponseServer sets a cookie (with optional Path, Domain, Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite)
ETagResponseResource version identifier (for conditional GET)
If-None-MatchRequestSend ETag; server returns 304 if unchanged
Last-ModifiedResponseResource last modified time
If-Modified-SinceRequestConditional GET based on time
HostRequestDomain being requested (required in HTTP/1.1)
LocationResponseRedirect target (with 3xx); resource location (with 201)
RefererRequestURL of the page that linked here (note: misspelled in spec)
User-AgentRequestClient identification string
OriginRequestOrigin (scheme+host+port) of the request; used by CORS
Access-Control-Allow-OriginResponseCORS: which origins can read this resource (* or specific)
Access-Control-Allow-MethodsResponseCORS: methods allowed in CORS request
Strict-Transport-SecurityResponseHSTS: enforce HTTPS for this domain (max-age=31536000)
Content-Security-PolicyResponseCSP: restrict resource loading; XSS mitigation
X-Frame-OptionsResponseClickjacking protection (DENY, SAMEORIGIN); modern equivalent is CSP frame-ancestors
Retry-AfterResponseHow long to wait before retry (seconds or HTTP date); with 429 / 503
X-Request-IDBothCustom: trace identifier for log correlation
HTTP Authentication PatternsTAB. 3C-3
SchemeHeader FormatUse
BasicAuthorization: Basic base64(user:pass)Simple; only safe over HTTPS; legacy and internal APIs
Bearer (token)Authorization: Bearer <token>Opaque tokens, OAuth access tokens; most common modern API auth
Bearer (JWT)Authorization: Bearer <jwt>JSON Web Token; header.payload.signature; self-contained claims
API key (header)X-API-Key: <key>Service-to-service; no standard header name (varies by API)
API key (query)?api_key=<key>Discouraged: appears in logs, referer headers, browser history
OAuth 2.0 (Authorization Code)Browser redirect flow: client → auth server → user grants → code → token; for web apps
OAuth 2.0 (PKCE)Auth Code + PKCE; for native apps and SPAs without client secret
OAuth 2.0 (Client Credentials)Service-to-service; client_id + client_secret → token; no user involved
OAuth 2.0 (Device Code)For TVs and devices without browser; show code, user enters on phone
DigestChallenge-response with nonce; mostly retired; replaced by Bearer + HTTPS
HMAC signatureAWS Signature v4 and similar; signed request body; high security
mTLS (mutual TLS)Client certificate; service-to-service in zero-trust networks
JWT structure: three Base64URL-encoded parts joined with dots. Header (algorithm, type), Payload (claims like sub, iss, aud, exp), Signature (HMAC or RSA/ECDSA of header.payload). Verify the signature server-side before trusting any claim. Don't store secrets in JWT payloads (Base64 is not encryption). For new web app auth, prefer OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code + PKCE flow with refresh tokens; for service-to-service, Client Credentials or mTLS. API keys in URLs leak through every layer (logs, proxies, analytics); always use headers.
API Pagination PatternsTAB. 3C-4
PatternRequest ShapeProsCons
Offset / Limit?offset=40&limit=20Simple; jump to any page; total count obviousSlow for deep pages (DB scans); items shift if data changes mid-pagination
Page / Per-Page?page=3&per_page=20Human-friendly; familiar to usersSame as offset; just sugar over it
Cursor (opaque)?cursor=eyJpZCI6MTIzfQStable through inserts/deletes; fast on indexed columns; consistent orderingCan't jump to arbitrary page; cursor encoding tied to API version
Cursor (keyset / seek)?after=2026-06-04T12:00&limit=20Same as opaque but more transparent; SQL-friendly (WHERE id > X)Requires sortable, unique key; exposes ordering field
Time-window / since-until?since=1717520400&until=1717606800Natural for time-series; logs, eventsNeed handling for events at the same timestamp
Response PatternExampleNotes
Link header (RFC 5988)Link: <url?page=2>; rel="next", <url?page=5>; rel="last"GitHub-style; relations: next, prev, first, last
Embedded metadata{ "data": [...], "next_cursor": "abc", "has_more": true }JSON-API and most modern REST APIs
HATEOAS hypermedia{ "data": [...], "_links": { "next": {"href": "..."} } }JSON-API, HAL formats; client follows links rather than constructs URLs
X- headersX-Total-Count: 1547
X-Page: 3
X-Per-Page: 20		Common in older / custom APIs; not standardized
For new APIs with potentially large or frequently changing data, prefer cursor-based pagination: stable through writes, faster on indexed columns, no skipped/duplicated items if data shifts between requests. Use offset/limit only for small, infrequently changing collections (admin tables, paginated reports). For "infinite scroll" UIs, cursor is the only correct choice. The Link header is the cleanest cross-API convention but JSON metadata is more discoverable for client developers; doing both is common (GitHub does).
API Rate Limiting PatternsTAB. 3C-5
AlgorithmBehaviorUse
Fixed windowN requests per clock window (e.g., per minute)Simple; allows burst at window boundary (potentially 2N in adjacent windows)
Sliding windowRolling count over the last N secondsSmoother; harder to implement; usually implemented with bucketed counters
Token bucketTokens refill at fixed rate; each request consumes oneAllows controlled bursts up to bucket size; standard for most APIs
Leaky bucketConstant outflow rate; excess requests dropped or queuedStrict; smooth output; rarely user-facing
Concurrency limitMax N parallel requests per clientFor long-running endpoints (uploads, exports); orthogonal to rate
Response HeaderMeaning
X-RateLimit-LimitTotal requests allowed in window
X-RateLimit-RemainingRequests remaining in current window
X-RateLimit-ResetUnix epoch (or seconds) when window resets
X-RateLimit-UsedRequests used in current window (some APIs)
RateLimit-Limit / Remaining / ResetIETF draft (without X- prefix); same semantics
Retry-After: <seconds>How long to wait; with 429 (or 503)
Retry-After: <HTTP-date>Same, as RFC 7231 date format
When rate-limited, the API returns 429 Too Many Requests. Always include Retry-After on a 429; clients with exponential backoff will be wrong otherwise. Standard implementation: token bucket per API key with Redis (INCR + EXPIRE) or a dedicated rate-limit service (Envoy, Kong, AWS API Gateway throttling). For client code, respect headers — read X-RateLimit-Remaining and slow down before hitting the limit, not after. Sliding-window log algorithms are accurate but memory-intensive; sliding-window counter (a hybrid with fixed buckets) is the modern compromise.
Section 3D Software

Regex Cheatsheet & Tester

Reference card for regular expression syntax, plus a live tester using JavaScript's RegExp engine.
Live TesterTOOL 3D-1
enter a pattern
Regex Flavor DifferencesTAB. 3D-2
FeaturePCRE / PerlJavaScriptPythonPOSIX BRE / sed
Lookbehind (?<=...)Yes (variable since 10.30)Yes (since ES2018)Yes (fixed-width before 3.7; variable since)No
Lookahead (?=...)YesYesYesNo
Named groups(?<name>...) or (?P<name>...)(?<name>...)(?P<name>...)No
Backreferences\1 or \k<name>\1 or \k<name>\1 or (?P=name)\1 (BRE only in some)
Possessive quantifiers *+ ++YesNo (use atomic groups)3.11+ yesNo
Atomic groups (?>...)YesNo (workaround with lookahead)3.11+ yesNo
Unicode property \p{L}YesYes (with u flag)Yes (with re.UNICODE or regex module)No
Case-insensitive (?i) inlineYesNo (flag only)YesNo
Dotall (. matches newline)(?s) or s flags flag (ES2018)re.DOTALL or (?s)No
Multiline (^$ per line)m flagm flagre.MULTILINE or (?m)n/a (line-oriented)
Anchors \A \Z (start/end of string)YesNo (use ^$ without m flag)YesNo
Word boundary \bYes (ASCII unless Unicode mode)Yes (ASCII)Yes (Unicode-aware in regex module)Yes
Recursive patternsYes ((?R), (?0))NoOnly with regex module, not stdlib reNo
Conditionals (?(1)yes|no)YesNoYesNo
PCRE (Perl-Compatible Regular Expressions) is the most feature-rich and the de facto standard for power users; most Unix tools (grep -P, ripgrep, ag) use PCRE. JavaScript was catching up steadily through ES2018 / ES2020. Python's stdlib re is solid but lacks recursive patterns; the third-party regex package adds them. POSIX BRE (basic regular expressions, used by old grep and sed) is the most limited; ERE (extended, egrep, grep -E) is a notch up. For portable scripts target PCRE2 or POSIX ERE; for embedded patterns in code use the host language's flavor and document.

Epoch & ISO 8601

Convert between Unix epoch (seconds or milliseconds), ISO 8601 strings, and human-readable dates.
Now (epoch)—
Now (ISO)—
InputTOOL 3E-1
Accepts:
• 10-digit numbers as epoch seconds
• 13-digit numbers as epoch milliseconds
• ISO 8601: 2026-06-03T14:30:00Z
• Date strings: June 3 2026
ConversionTAB. 3E-1
Epoch (s)—
Epoch (ms)—
ISO 8601 UTC—
ISO 8601 Local—
RFC 2822 / HTTP—
Relative—
strftime Format SpecifiersTAB. 3E-2
SpecifierMeaningExample
%Y4-digit year2026
%y2-digit year26
%mMonth, zero-padded01-12
%BFull month nameJanuary
%bAbbreviated monthJan
%dDay of month, zero-padded01-31
%eDay of month, space-padded" 1" - "31"
%jDay of year001-366
%AFull weekday nameThursday
%aAbbreviated weekdayThu
%wWeekday as number0 (Sunday) - 6 (Saturday)
%uWeekday ISO1 (Monday) - 7 (Sunday)
%VISO week number01-53
%HHour 24-hour00-23
%IHour 12-hour01-12
%pAM / PMAM, PM
%MMinute00-59
%SSecond00-59 (60 for leap second)
%fMicrosecond (Python)000000-999999
%ZTimezone nameUTC, EST, PDT
%zTimezone offset+0000, -0500
%sUnix epoch seconds1717520400
Common PatternFormat StringOutput
ISO 8601 date%Y-%m-%d2026-06-04
ISO 8601 datetime%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S2026-06-04T14:30:00
ISO 8601 with TZ%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z2026-06-04T14:30:00-0400
RFC 2822 (email)%a, %d %b %Y %H:%M:%S %zThu, 04 Jun 2026 14:30:00 -0400
Log timestamp%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S2026-06-04 14:30:00
Filename-safe%Y%m%d_%H%M%S20260604_143000
Pretty (US)%B %-d, %YJune 4, 2026
Pretty (EU)%-d %B %Y4 June 2026
strftime / strptime is standard across C, Python, Ruby, Go, Rust (via chrono), most shell date commands. JavaScript's Date.toLocaleString uses Intl options instead, but libraries like date-fns and Luxon use strftime-compatible tokens. The %-d form (no padding) is a GNU extension; standard POSIX is %d with zero-padding only. For Go, use Go's reference time (2006-01-02 15:04:05) instead of strftime; it's a different system entirely.
Cron Schedule ReferenceTAB. 3E-3
┌────────── minute (0 - 59) │ ┌──────── hour (0 - 23) │ │ ┌────── day of month (1 - 31) │ │ │ ┌──── month (1 - 12) │ │ │ │ ┌── day of week (0 - 6, Sunday = 0 or 7) │ │ │ │ │ * * * * * command to execute
ScheduleCron Expression
Every minute* * * * *
Every 5 minutes*/5 * * * *
Every 15 minutes*/15 * * * *
Every hour, at :000 * * * *
Every hour, at :3030 * * * *
Every day at midnight0 0 * * *
Every day at 9 AM0 9 * * *
Weekdays at 9 AM0 9 * * 1-5
Weekends at noon0 12 * * 0,6
First of every month0 0 1 * *
Last day of month0 0 28-31 * * (gated in command)
Every Sunday at 2 AM0 2 * * 0
Twice daily (8 AM, 8 PM)0 8,20 * * *
Every 2 hours from 9 AM to 5 PM0 9-17/2 * * *
January 1 at midnight0 0 1 1 *
Reboot (cron's @reboot)@reboot
Yearly (shortcut)@yearly or @annually
Monthly@monthly
Weekly@weekly
Daily@daily or @midnight
Hourly@hourly
Cron uses system local time, not UTC; daylight-saving transitions can run a job twice or skip it entirely. For accuracy across timezones, schedule in UTC by setting CRON_TZ=UTC at the top of crontab (Linux Vixie cron). Day-of-month and day-of-week are OR'd, not AND'd: 0 0 1 * 1 means "midnight on the 1st OR on Mondays," not "the first Monday." Use crontab.guru to verify expressions. For sub-minute scheduling, cron is the wrong tool; use systemd timers, schedulers like APScheduler, or a job queue.

Section 1E Electrical & Electronics

Electrical Formulas

Enter any two values, the other two compute. Clear a field to re-solve.
V—

Reactance & ResonanceTOOL 1E-2
XL (inductive)—
XC (capacitive)—
Resonance fr—
Q (if R = 1Ω)—
Decibel ConverterTOOL 1E-3
dB (power, 10·log)—
dB (voltage, 20·log)—
dBm (re 1 mW)—
dBV (re 1 V)—
dBu (re 0.775 V)—
dB → power ratio (10^(dB/10))—
dB → voltage ratio (10^(dB/20))—
Time Constants & DividerTOOL 1E-4
RC τ (63.2% charge)—
RC 5τ (~99% settled)—
RL τ (L/R)—
Voltage Divider
Vout (unloaded)—
Vout (with load)—
Current through divider—
Battery & Power BudgetTOOL 1E-5
Efficiency accounts for DC-DC losses, depth-of-discharge limits, and the fact that you rarely get 100% of label capacity. 80% is a reasonable default for LiPo+boost regulator; drop to 60-70% for cold operation or aged cells.
Total Energy (Wh)—
Usable Energy—
Average Power—
Runtime—
In days · weeks—
Most real designs care about duty cycle. If your device spends 99% of the time in deep sleep at 50 µA and 1% awake at 100 mA, the effective average is (0.99 × 0.05) + (0.01 × 100) ≈ 1.05 mA, not 100 mA.
Crystal & Clock LoadingTOOL 1E-6
Crystal datasheet gives a specified CL (load capacitance). For symmetric Pierce oscillators with two equal loading caps C1 = C2: CL = (C1·C2)/(C1+C2) + Cstray. Solve for C: C1 = C2 = 2·(CL − Cstray). Typical stray is 2-5 pF (pin + trace + IC).
Required C1 = C2—
Standard E12 closest—
Effective CL (with E12)—
Frequency Pull (ppm)—
Gain Margin (rough)—
Frequency pull estimate uses Δf/f ≈ −C0·ΔCL / (2·(C0 + CL)²) with C0 ≈ 1 pF typical. For tight-tolerance clocks (RTC, USB host), use trimmable caps or match to your specific crystal lot.
LED Current-Limit ResistorTOOL 1E-7
Total LED Drop—
Voltage Across Resistor—
Resistor (exact)—
Resistor (E12 next-up)—
Power Dissipated by R—
Recommended SMD Size—
Assessment—
R = (Vs − N × Vf) / If. Pick the next E12 value up (higher R) to stay safely under rated current. SMD package sizing: 0603 handles 100 mW, 0805 handles 125 mW, 1206 handles 250 mW, 1210 handles 500 mW. For a series string the supply must exceed N × Vf with at least 20-30% headroom or the LEDs run dim and react badly to supply variation. For PWM-dimmed LEDs, the average current matters; for constant brightness, use a constant-current driver (e.g., LM334, LM3914, AL8807) instead of a resistor above about 50 mA.
Section 1F Electrical & Electronics

RF Tools

Wavelength and frequency, antenna lengths, VSWR converter, free-space path loss, ISM and amateur band reference.
Frequency ↔ WavelengthTOOL 1F-1
λ (full wave)—
λ/2—
λ/4—
5λ/8—
Antenna LengthTOOL 1F-2
Quarter-wave whip and half-wave dipole with 0.95 end-effect correction. For a real build, plan to trim a touch longer and tune down.
1/4 wave whip (×0.95)—
1/2 wave dipole (×0.95)—
Full wave loop—
J-pole radiator (3/4 λ)—
VSWR · Return Loss · ΓTOOL 1F-3
Enter any one value; the others compute.
VSWR—
Return Loss—
|Γ|—
Mismatch Loss—
% Power Reflected—
Free-Space Path LossTOOL 1F-4
FSPL—
Friis equation20·log(d) + 20·log(f) + 32.45
Smith Chart HelperTOOL 1F-5
Positive X = inductive, negative X = capacitive. Output point plotted on the normalized chart; the dashed yellow ring is the constant-VSWR locus through your point.
Normalized z—
Γ—
|Γ|—
∠Γ—
VSWR—
Return Loss—
|Z|—
L-Network Matching SynthesisTOOL 1F-6
L-network matches a real source impedance to a complex load via one series + one shunt element. Two configurations: low-pass (series L, shunt C) and high-pass (series C, shunt L). Series element goes on the lower-resistance side.
Q factor—
Bandwidth (3 dB)—
Topology—
Low-Pass L-Network
Series L—
Shunt C—
High-Pass L-Network
Series C—
Shunt L—
Complex loads are handled by absorbing XL into the series element. Component values shown assume an ideal lossless network. Real-world: derate Q for component loss (typical inductor Q at HF ≈ 50-150, ceramic capacitor Q ≈ 200-1000).
Band Reference (ISM, License-Free, Amateur)TAB. 1F-1
BandFrequencyWavelengthRegionCommon Use
Verify against your regional regulator (FCC in US, ETSI / national agencies in EU, etc.) before deploying. Power and duty-cycle limits vary.
Section 2C Mechanical

Laser Cutter Settings

Machine reference for the xTool family on the Mountain Maryland bench, plus starting parameters by material and operation.
Machine ReferenceTAB. 2C-1
Material Operation Machine Power % Speed (mm/s) Passes Notes
All values are conservative starting points based on community references and public datasheets. Test on scrap. Focus height, air assist, lens, and material moisture content all affect results. Treat anything published here as a v0 baseline to refine against your machine.
Section 1G Electrical & Electronics

Breadboard & Stripboard

Layout conventions for solderless breadboards and Vero-style stripboards, plus the pitch reference you reach for whenever a footprint isn't where you expected.
Standard Solderless Breadboard TopologyFIG. 1G-1
Standard half-board layout. Power rails run horizontally; component columns run vertically (5 ties on each side of the center channel). The center channel is for DIP packages straddling rows.
Stripboard (Vero) TopologyFIG. 1G-2
Continuous copper tracks along each row with holes at 0.1″ pitch. Cut tracks between holes with a spot-face cutter to isolate sections. Components mount on the non-copper side.
Connector & Footprint Pitch ReferenceTAB. 1G-1
PitchImperialCommon Use
Section 2D Mechanical

3D Printer Settings

FDM filament parameters and resin exposure starting points. Treat as v0 baselines; calibrate against your specific printer, hotend, lens, and resin batch.
FDM Filament ReferenceTAB. 2D-1
Difficulty colors: ■ Easy · ■ Medium · ■ Hard · ■ Expert (enclosure + hardened nozzle)
Resin (MSLA / DLP / SLA) ReferenceTAB. 2D-2
Exposure values typical for 8K monochrome LCD printers at 50 µm layer height. Adjust ±0.5s for thinner/thicker layers and printer-specific LCD power.
Cantilever Snap Fit CalculatorTOOL 2D-3
Max Strain at Base—
Bending Stress—
Strain vs Yield Limit—
Deflection Force—
Assessment—
For a rectangular cantilever: ε = (3 × h × y) / (2 × L²), σ = E × ε. Pick a cantilever where strain stays well below the material's yield strain (typically <50% of yield strain for repeated use, <75% for single use). Tapered cantilevers (smaller cross-section at the free end) reduce stress concentration at the base; rule of thumb is to taper the cantilever to half its base thickness at the tip. For latch faces, use a 30°-45° lead-in angle for assembly and a 70°-90° retention angle to resist removal.
Living Hinge Design ReferenceTAB. 2D-3
MaterialHinge Thickness (mm)Cycle LifeNotes
Polypropylene (PP)0.25-0.50millions of cyclesThe canonical living hinge material; integral with injection-molded part
Polyethylene (PE)0.30-0.60millions of cyclesLess common than PP; bottle caps, flip lids
Nylon (PA6, PA12)0.40-0.80hundreds of thousandsHigher strength; less flexible; needs longer hinge area
TPU0.50-1.0+very highElastomer; effectively infinite hinge life; rubber-like feel
PLA / PETG (FDM 3D print)0.40-0.60 (single layer)10-100 cyclesPossible but limited; print hinge as a single line, parallel to layers
ABSnot recommendedlowCracks at the hinge; use mechanical hinge instead
A traditional injection-molded living hinge is 0.25-0.5 mm of polypropylene with a 1-2 mm radius at the entrance, mechanically pre-flexed before first use to align the polymer chains. For FDM-printed living hinges, orient the print so the hinge fold is parallel to the layers (the hinge bends along a single line), and use a single-perimeter wall at the hinge with no infill. PLA and PETG hinges survive tens of cycles, not millions; for repeated opening use a mechanical hinge or print in TPU. For SLS or MJF (laser-sintered nylon), the part can include a thin (0.4-0.8 mm) living hinge that performs nearly as well as injection-molded PP.
Section 2E Mechanical

GD&T Quick Reference

Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing symbols per ASME Y14.5, grouped by type. Each card shows the symbol, name, characteristic family, and whether a datum is required.
Material Condition & Other ModifiersTAB. 2E-1
Feature Control Frame AnatomyFIG. 2E-1
| ⌖ | Ø 0.25 | Ⓜ | A | B | C |
⌖ · characteristic (here: position)
Ø 0.25 · tolerance value (diameter zone, 0.25 units)
Ⓜ · material condition modifier (MMC)
A B C · primary, secondary, tertiary datum references
Drawing Sheet Sizes & Title BlockTAB. 2E-2
StandardSizeDimensions (mm)Dimensions (in)
ANSIA216 × 2798.5 × 11
ANSIB279 × 43211 × 17
ANSIC432 × 55917 × 22
ANSID559 × 86422 × 34
ANSIE864 × 111834 × 44
ISO 216A4210 × 2978.27 × 11.69
ISO 216A3297 × 42011.69 × 16.54
ISO 216A2420 × 59416.54 × 23.39
ISO 216A1594 × 84123.39 × 33.11
ISO 216A0841 × 118933.11 × 46.81
Title block (ASME Y14.1) standard elements: drawing number, title, scale, units, projection (1st-angle ISO or 3rd-angle ANSI), tolerance defaults (e.g., "Unless Otherwise Specified: ±0.1 mm linear, ±0.5° angular"), drafter name and date, revision history, sheet number / total sheets, owner / company name, material, finish. Show projection method with the standard double-cone or trihedral symbol to remove ambiguity for international suppliers. Standard scales: 1:1, 2:1, 5:1, 10:1, and 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50, 1:100. For mechanical drawings 1:1, 2:1, and 1:2 are most common.
Section 3I Software

Bill of Materials Calculator

Editable parts list with running cost. Add rows, set tax rate and shipping, watch the grand total update live. Export the working list as CSV. Data is session-only; refreshing the page clears it.
Part / Description Qty Unit Cost Line Total Notes
Subtotal$0.00
Tax$0.00
Shipping$0.00
Grand Total$0.00
Section 3J Software

Keyboard Shortcuts

Working reference for the apps you reach for the most. Defaults shown; verify against your installed version's settings.
Section 3K Software

Markdown Reference

CommonMark and GitHub Flavored Markdown (GFM) syntax reference. Covers inline formatting, block elements, links, images, tables, fenced code blocks, task lists, and the extensions most renderers support. For HTML entities see 3F; for code-block syntax highlighting languages, see your renderer's documentation.
Inline FormattingTAB. 3K-1
MarkdownRenders AsNotes
**bold**boldTwo asterisks or two underscores
*italic*italicSingle asterisk or underscore
***bold italic***bold italicThree asterisks or underscores
~~strikethrough~~strikethroughGFM extension; double tildes
`inline code`inline codeBackticks; use double backticks if content has backticks
==highlight==highlight (varies)Extension; not in CommonMark; Obsidian, some renderers
[link](url)linkInline link; brackets for text, parens for URL
[link][ref]linkReference link; [ref]: url defined elsewhere
![alt](url)imageSame syntax as link with leading !
<https://example.com>autolinkAngle brackets auto-create link
Block ElementsTAB. 3K-2
MarkdownRenders As
# H1
# H2 ... # H6		Headings, levels 1-6 (use hash + space)
- item
* item
+ item		Unordered list; any of the three markers
1. item
2. item		Ordered list; numbers auto-renumber
- [ ] todo
- [x] done		Task list (GFM extension); rendered as checkbox
> quoted text
> second line		Blockquote; nest with multiple >
---
***
___		Horizontal rule; three or more of same character
code block (4 spaces)
or
```python
code
```		Fenced code block; backticks with optional language
[^1] inline footnote
[^1]: footnote text		Footnote (GFM extension); appears at bottom of document
Tables (GFM)TAB. 3K-3
| Column A | Column B | Column C | |----------|:--------:|---------:| | left | center | right | | data | data | data |
Pipes separate cells; the second row defines column alignment with colons: :-- left, :-: center, --: right. Pipes at line start and end are optional. Cell content cannot contain raw pipes (escape with \|) or line breaks (use <br>). For complex tables, fall back to inline HTML.
Links, References, and EscapesTAB. 3K-4
PatternExampleNotes
Inline link with title[text](url "title")Title appears as tooltip on hover
Reference link[text][1]
[1]: https://example.com		Useful for long URLs; definitions anywhere in document
Anchor / heading link[goto](#section-name)Heading text lowercased, spaces to hyphens, punctuation stripped
Image with link[![alt](img.png)](url)Nest image inside link syntax
Hard line breakend with 2 spaces
or backslash \		Soft break (single newline) merges to space; need explicit hard break
Escape character\* \_ \` \[ \]Backslash escapes Markdown special characters
HTML pass-through<div>raw HTML</div>Raw HTML allowed; block-level needs blank line before/after
GitHub auto-links bare URLs, issue numbers (#123), and user mentions (@username) inside GitHub. Other renderers vary. Code blocks with a language hint (```python) trigger syntax highlighting on most platforms. For docs and READMEs, two-space hard breaks and reference-style links keep the source readable. For GFM admonitions (alerts), use the blockquote-with-tag pattern: > [!NOTE], > [!WARNING], > [!TIP], > [!IMPORTANT], > [!CAUTION].
Section 3L Software

Git Command Reference

Day-to-day Git commands grouped by task. Covers status & history, branching & merging, undoing changes, remote operations, stash, and the .gitignore pattern syntax. For shell variable expansion see 3M; for SSH key management see 3N.
Status & HistoryTAB. 3L-1
CommandUse
git statusShow staged, unstaged, untracked files
git status -sShort format; one-letter prefix per file
git logFull commit history (most recent first)
git log --onelineOne-line summary per commit
git log --graph --oneline --allASCII graph of all branches; the classic view
git log -p <file>Full diffs for commits touching this file
git log --since="2 weeks ago"Filter by time
git log --author="name"Filter by author
git show <commit>Show diff and metadata for a single commit
git diffUnstaged changes
git diff --stagedStaged changes (vs the last commit)
git diff <branch1> <branch2>Compare two branches
git blame <file>Per-line author and commit attribution
Branching & MergingTAB. 3L-2
CommandUse
git branchList local branches; * marks current
git branch -aInclude remote-tracking branches
git branch <name>Create branch; doesn't switch
git switch <branch>Switch branches (modern; replaces checkout for branches)
git switch -c <name>Create and switch in one step
git checkout <branch>Older equivalent of switch
git merge <branch>Merge into current branch
git merge --no-ff <branch>Force merge commit even if fast-forward possible
git rebase <branch>Replay current branch's commits on top of <branch>
git rebase -i HEAD~5Interactive rebase last 5 commits (squash, reorder, reword)
git branch -d <name>Delete local branch (safe; refuses if unmerged)
git branch -D <name>Force-delete local branch
git push origin --delete <branch>Delete remote branch
Staging, Committing, UndoingTAB. 3L-3
CommandUse
git add <file>Stage specific file
git add .Stage all changes in current directory
git add -pStage interactively by hunk
git commit -m "msg"Commit staged changes
git commit -am "msg"Stage all tracked + commit (skips untracked)
git commit --amendEdit the last commit (message and/or staged changes)
git reset <file>Unstage a file (keeps changes in working tree)
git reset HEAD~1Undo last commit; keep changes unstaged (soft default mode)
git reset --soft HEAD~1Undo commit; keep changes staged
git reset --hard HEAD~1Undo commit AND throw away changes (destructive!)
git restore <file>Discard unstaged changes (modern; replaces checkout for files)
git revert <commit>Create new commit that undoes a previous one (safe for shared history)
git clean -fdRemove untracked files and directories
Remote Operations & StashTAB. 3L-4
CommandUse
git remote -vList configured remotes with URLs
git remote add origin <url>Add new remote
git remote set-url origin <url>Change remote URL
git clone <url>Clone repository (full history)
git clone --depth 1 <url>Shallow clone (latest commit only)
git fetchDownload remote changes; doesn't merge
git pullFetch + merge (or rebase if configured)
git pull --rebaseRebase local commits onto remote instead of merging
git pushPush current branch to its upstream
git push -u origin <branch>Set upstream and push (first time on new branch)
git push --force-with-leaseForce push that's safe if remote hasn't changed
git stashSave uncommitted changes; clean working tree
git stash popApply most recent stash and remove it
git stash listList all stashes
git stash drop <n>Discard a specific stash
git cherry-pick <commit>Apply commit from another branch to current
.gitignore Pattern SyntaxTAB. 3L-5
PatternMatches
*.logAll .log files anywhere in repo
build/Any directory named build anywhere
/buildOnly top-level build (leading slash anchors at root)
**/tempAny temp directory at any depth
!important.logNegation; include important.log even if *.log excludes it
doc/*.txtDirect children of doc; doesn't match doc/sub/*.txt
doc/**/*.txtAny depth under doc
?.txtSingle-character wildcard; matches a.txt not ab.txt
[abc].txtCharacter class; matches a.txt, b.txt, c.txt
# commentHash starts a comment line
Patterns are evaluated from top to bottom; later rules override earlier ones. Common stack: language-specific ignore (Python, Node, Rust), then OS-specific (.DS_Store, Thumbs.db), then editor (.vscode/, .idea/), then project-specific. github.com/github/gitignore has well-maintained templates. Once a file is tracked, adding it to .gitignore doesn't untrack it; use git rm --cached <file> to stop tracking while keeping the local copy.
Section 3M Software

Shell & Linux Reference

Bash syntax, common Linux commands by purpose, glob and pattern expansion, ANSI terminal escape codes, and errno reference. For Git see 3L; for cron and date formats see 3E.
Bash Variable & Parameter ExpansionTAB. 3M-1
ExpansionResult
$VAR or ${VAR}Value of VAR; braces required when followed by letters
${VAR:-default}Value of VAR, or default if unset/empty
${VAR:=default}Same as above, but also assigns default to VAR
${VAR:?msg}Error with msg if VAR unset/empty
${VAR:+other}Use other if VAR is set, else empty
${VAR:offset:length}Substring of VAR (zero-indexed)
${#VAR}Length of VAR
${VAR#prefix}Remove shortest matching prefix
${VAR##prefix}Remove longest matching prefix (greedy)
${VAR%suffix}Remove shortest matching suffix
${VAR%%suffix}Remove longest matching suffix
${VAR/pattern/repl}Replace first occurrence
${VAR//pattern/repl}Replace all occurrences
${VAR^^}Uppercase
${VAR,,}Lowercase
$(command)Command substitution (preferred over backticks)
$((arith))Arithmetic expansion; integer only
Common Linux Commands by PurposeTAB. 3M-2
PurposeCommon Commands
Navigationcd, pwd, pushd / popd, ls, ll, tree
File operationscp, mv, rm, mkdir, rmdir, touch, ln (-s for symlink)
Permissionschmod, chown, chgrp, umask, stat
Viewing filescat, less, more, head, tail (-f follow), tac
Text processinggrep, sed, awk, cut, sort, uniq, wc, tr
Find & searchfind, locate, which, whereis, type, ripgrep (rg), fd
Archive & compresstar, gzip, gunzip, zip, unzip, xz, zstd
Process managementps, top, htop, kill, killall, jobs, bg, fg, nohup, screen, tmux
Disk usagedf, du, ncdu, lsblk, fdisk, mount, umount
Networkingping, traceroute, dig, nslookup, curl, wget, ss, netstat, ip, ifconfig
Permissions & userssu, sudo, passwd, who, w, id, last
System infouname, hostname, uptime, free, vmstat, dmesg, journalctl
Package management (Debian/Ubuntu)apt, apt-get, dpkg
Package management (Red Hat)dnf, yum, rpm
Package management (macOS)brew, port
Redirection & PipesTAB. 3M-3
OperatorMeaning
cmd > fileRedirect stdout to file (overwrite)
cmd >> fileAppend stdout to file
cmd 2> fileRedirect stderr to file
cmd 2>&1Redirect stderr to stdout (often > file 2>&1)
cmd &> fileBash shortcut for both stdout and stderr
cmd < fileUse file as stdin
cmd <<EOF
here-doc
EOF		Here-document; multi-line stdin
cmd1 | cmd2Pipe stdout of cmd1 to stdin of cmd2
cmd1 |& cmd2Pipe both stdout and stderr
cmd &Run in background
cmd1 && cmd2Run cmd2 only if cmd1 succeeds (exit 0)
cmd1 || cmd2Run cmd2 only if cmd1 fails
cmd1 ; cmd2Run sequentially regardless of result
Glob & Shell Pattern ExpansionTAB. 3M-4
PatternMatches
*Any string (not including /); doesn't match leading dot by default
?Single character
[abc]Any single character from set
[a-z]Character range
[!abc]Any character not in set
{a,b,c}Brace expansion; expands to a b c
{1..10}Numeric range expansion
**Recursive glob (with shopt -s globstar); any depth
~Home directory; ~user for another user's home
Globs are not regex. * in a glob is .* in regex; ? in a glob is . in regex. Use shopt -s nullglob to make unmatched globs expand to nothing instead of literal pattern (useful in scripts). Use shopt -s dotglob to include hidden files. Test glob expansion safely with echo before running destructive commands like rm.
ANSI Escape Codes (Terminal Colors & Cursor)TAB. 3M-5
CodeEffect
\033[0mReset all formatting
\033[1mBold / bright
\033[3mItalic (not all terminals)
\033[4mUnderline
\033[7mReverse video (swap fg/bg)
\033[30m - \033[37mForeground: black, red, green, yellow, blue, magenta, cyan, white
\033[40m - \033[47mBackground: same colors
\033[90m - \033[97mBright foreground colors
\033[38;5;N m256-color foreground (N = 0-255)
\033[38;2;R;G;B mTrue color foreground (RGB)
\033[HMove cursor to top-left
\033[N;MmMove cursor to row N, col M
\033[2JClear screen
\033[KClear from cursor to end of line
\033[?25l / \033[?25hHide / show cursor
In Bash, use echo -e or printf to interpret escape sequences. The \033 escape is octal for ESC (0x1B); \e works in echo -e. Always reset (\033[0m) at the end of colored output to prevent bleeding into the next prompt. To detect color support: tput colors returns 8 / 16 / 256 depending on terminal. NO_COLOR environment variable should disable color output (https://no-color.org convention).
Common errno ValuesTAB. 3M-6
errnoSymbolMeaning
1EPERMOperation not permitted
2ENOENTNo such file or directory
3ESRCHNo such process
4EINTRInterrupted system call
5EIOInput/output error
9EBADFBad file descriptor
11EAGAIN / EWOULDBLOCKResource temporarily unavailable (would block on non-blocking I/O)
12ENOMEMCannot allocate memory
13EACCESPermission denied
14EFAULTBad address
17EEXISTFile exists
20ENOTDIRNot a directory
21EISDIRIs a directory
22EINVALInvalid argument
24EMFILEToo many open files
28ENOSPCNo space left on device
32EPIPEBroken pipe
98EADDRINUSEAddress already in use
104ECONNRESETConnection reset by peer
110ETIMEDOUTConnection timed out
111ECONNREFUSEDConnection refused
errno values are positive in Linux; system calls return -1 and set errno to the specific error. The standard C library function strerror(errno) converts to a human-readable string; the shell utility errno (or perl -E 'say $!=N') does the same. Many higher-level tools wrap these (e.g., Python's FileNotFoundError is errno ENOENT/2). Exit codes 1-125 are conventional for failure (most programs use 1 for generic error), 126 = not executable, 127 = command not found, 128+N = killed by signal N (so 130 = Ctrl+C, signal 2).
Logging Level ConventionsTAB. 3M-7
LevelUse ForExample
TRACEHyper-detailed; function entry/exit; loop iterations; usually off in productiontrace("entering parse(), input=%s", input)
DEBUGDevelopment detail; variable values, query strings, decision branchesdebug("query: %s with %d params", sql, params.length)
INFONormal operation; significant lifecycle events; useful in production logsinfo("server started on port %d", port)
WARNUnexpected but recoverable; deprecated API use; degraded but functionalwarn("disk space below 20%%; consider cleanup")
ERROROperation failed but app continues; exceptions caught and handled; logged for diagnosiserror("failed to send email to %s: %s", addr, err)
FATAL / CRITICALApp must terminate; unrecoverable; pager-worthy in productionfatal("database connection lost; shutting down")
Framework / SystemLevel Hierarchy
Python loggingDEBUG (10) · INFO (20) · WARNING (30) · ERROR (40) · CRITICAL (50)
Java log4j / SLF4JTRACE · DEBUG · INFO · WARN · ERROR · FATAL (log4j only; SLF4J drops FATAL)
JavaScript consoleconsole.debug · console.log · console.info · console.warn · console.error
Go log/slogDebug · Info · Warn · Error (slog standard since Go 1.21)
Rust logtrace! · debug! · info! · warn! · error!
syslog (RFC 5424)0 emerg · 1 alert · 2 crit · 3 err · 4 warning · 5 notice · 6 info · 7 debug
systemd-journaldSame as syslog (0-7); set SYSLOG_FACILITY per service
PracticeNotes
Structured logs (JSON)Key-value fields, not free-text; greppable and machine-parsable; required for log aggregation (Loki, Elastic, Datadog)
Include context fieldsrequest_id, user_id, trace_id; bind to each log entry across the request lifecycle
Never log secretsAPI keys, passwords, tokens, full credit card numbers; sanitize before logging
Sample high-volume logsFor hot paths, log 1 in N events rather than all; preserves visibility without flooding
Set production level to INFODEBUG floods storage; can be enabled per-request via header (X-Debug, ?debug=1)
Rotate by size and timelogrotate on Linux; cloud platforms rotate automatically
ERROR ≠ exceptionCatching an expected error (404, validation failure) is INFO or WARN; uncaught is ERROR
FATAL implies exitIf you don't terminate after FATAL, use ERROR instead; pages people unnecessarily otherwise
The right level is a contract with your future self and your ops team. INFO should be useful enough to follow a request through normal operation; ERROR should fire on things a human needs to look at; WARN on things to watch (rising disk usage, slow queries, deprecated endpoint hits). When in doubt, log structured JSON with timestamp, level, message, and context fields (request_id, span_id for OpenTelemetry tracing). The OTLP / OpenTelemetry standard unifies logs, metrics, and traces under one model; if you're starting fresh, design for it from the start.
Section 3N Software

Networking Reference

Well-known port numbers, CIDR / subnet mask reference, IPv4 special-purpose ranges, and common file format magic numbers for byte-level file identification. For HTTP status codes see 3C; for shell network commands see 3M.
Well-Known & Common Port NumbersTAB. 3N-1
PortProtocolService
20 / 21TCPFTP (data / control)
22TCPSSH, SCP, SFTP
23TCPTelnet (insecure; legacy)
25TCPSMTP (email send, server-to-server)
53TCP / UDPDNS
67 / 68UDPDHCP (server / client)
69UDPTFTP (trivial FTP; embedded firmware updates)
80TCPHTTP
110TCPPOP3 (email retrieval)
123UDPNTP (time sync)
143TCPIMAP (email retrieval)
161 / 162UDPSNMP (network management)
389 / 636TCPLDAP / LDAPS
443TCPHTTPS
465TCPSMTPS (legacy implicit TLS)
514UDPSyslog
587TCPSMTP submission (with STARTTLS)
993TCPIMAPS (IMAP over TLS)
995TCPPOP3S (POP3 over TLS)
1194UDPOpenVPN
1433TCPMicrosoft SQL Server
1883TCPMQTT (IoT messaging)
3306TCPMySQL / MariaDB
3389TCPRDP (Remote Desktop)
5432TCPPostgreSQL
5683UDPCoAP (constrained-device IoT)
6379TCPRedis
8080 / 8443TCPHTTP / HTTPS alternate (dev servers, proxies)
8883TCPMQTT over TLS
27017TCPMongoDB
Ports 0-1023 are "well-known" (IANA reserved, typically need root to bind on Unix); 1024-49151 are registered; 49152-65535 are ephemeral / dynamic. Linux ephemeral range is typically 32768-60999 (see /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range); macOS uses 49152-65535. For embedded development, MQTT (1883) and CoAP (5683) are the dominant IoT messaging protocols; both have TLS variants (8883 for MQTTS, port varies for CoAP+DTLS).
CIDR / Subnet Mask ReferenceTAB. 3N-2
PrefixSubnet MaskTotal AddressesUsable Hosts
/32255.255.255.25511 (host)
/31255.255.255.25422 (point-to-point, RFC 3021)
/30255.255.255.25242
/29255.255.255.24886
/28255.255.255.2401614
/27255.255.255.2243230
/26255.255.255.1926462
/25255.255.255.128128126
/24255.255.255.0256254
/23255.255.254.0512510
/22255.255.252.01,0241,022
/21255.255.248.02,0482,046
/20255.255.240.04,0964,094
/19255.255.224.08,1928,190
/18255.255.192.016,38416,382
/17255.255.128.032,76832,766
/16255.255.0.065,53665,534
/8255.0.0.016,777,21616,777,214
/00.0.0.0All IPv4Default route
RangeUse
10.0.0.0/8Private (RFC 1918); largest private space
172.16.0.0/12Private (RFC 1918); 172.16.0.0 - 172.31.255.255
192.168.0.0/16Private (RFC 1918); most common home / SOHO range
169.254.0.0/16Link-local (auto-assigned when DHCP fails)
127.0.0.0/8Loopback (localhost is 127.0.0.1)
224.0.0.0/4Multicast (224.0.0.0 - 239.255.255.255)
255.255.255.255Limited broadcast
100.64.0.0/10Carrier-grade NAT (CGNAT); ISPs
Usable hosts = 2^(32 − prefix) − 2 (subtracts network address and broadcast address). The /31 exception (RFC 3021) allows both addresses on point-to-point links since there's no broadcast need. CIDR notation lets you express any prefix length; subnet masks were the older fixed-class form (A, B, C). For VLSM (variable-length subnet masking), pick prefix sizes that fit your subnets; subdivide a /24 into smaller /27s for separate VLANs without wasting addresses.
Common File Format Magic NumbersTAB. 3N-3
FormatMagic Bytes (hex)ASCII / Notes
PNG89 50 4E 47 0D 0A 1A 0A.PNG\r\n\x1A\n — 8 byte signature
JPEG (JFIF)FF D8 FF E0SOI marker + JFIF
JPEG (Exif)FF D8 FF E1SOI marker + APP1 Exif
GIF87a / GIF89a47 49 46 38 [37/39] 61"GIF87a" or "GIF89a"
WebP52 49 46 46 ?? ?? ?? ?? 57 45 42 50"RIFF" + size + "WEBP"
BMP42 4D"BM"
TIFF (little-endian)49 49 2A 00"II*\0"
TIFF (big-endian)4D 4D 00 2A"MM\0*"
PDF25 50 44 46 2D"%PDF-"
ZIP / .docx / .xlsx / .pptx / .jar50 4B 03 04"PK\x03\x04" — Office docs are ZIP archives
gzip1F 8BTwo bytes
bzip242 5A 68"BZh"
tar (POSIX)at offset 257: 75 73 74 61 72"ustar" magic 257 bytes in
7-Zip37 7A BC AF 27 1C"7z\xBC\xAF\x27\x1C"
ELF (Linux executable)7F 45 4C 46"\x7FELF"
Mach-O (macOS executable)FE ED FA CE / CF FA ED FE32-bit / 64-bit big or little-endian variants
PE / EXE (Windows)4D 5A"MZ" — DOS stub header
Class file (Java)CA FE BA BE"cafebabe"
WASM00 61 73 6D"\0asm" + 4-byte version
SQLite3 database53 51 4C 69 74 65 20 66"SQLite f" (16-byte header total)
MP3 (ID3v2)49 44 33"ID3" — tag, not the audio itself
MP3 (raw frame)FF FB / FF F3 / FF F2MPEG-1 / 2 / 2.5 sync byte + version
MP4 / M4Aat offset 4: 66 74 79 70"ftyp" box; preceded by 4-byte size
FLAC66 4C 61 43"fLaC"
Ogg4F 67 67 53"OggS"
The Unix file command uses these magic numbers (and many more) to identify file types; hexdump -C file | head shows the first bytes. The standard magic database is /usr/share/file/magic or /etc/magic. Note: PDF and other formats allow leading whitespace / BOM before the magic, so strict offset-0 checks are not always reliable. Files based on ZIP (DOCX, XLSX, ODF, JAR, EPUB) all share the same 50 4B 03 04 magic; you have to look inside the archive for the manifest to tell them apart.
Section 3O Software

Config File Formats

Side-by-side syntax for JSON, YAML, and TOML; the same data structure represented in each, when each is the right choice, and JSON Schema basics. For Markdown see 3K; for HTML and URL encoding see 3F.
JSON vs YAML vs TOMLTAB. 3O-1
PropertyJSONYAMLTOML
CommentsNoYes (#)Yes (#)
Indentation significantNoYesNo
Strings: quotes requiredYes (double)Optional (mostly)For most; " or '
Multi-line stringsNo (escape \n)Yes (| literal, > folded)Yes (""" or ''')
Dates / timestampsNo (use ISO 8601 string)Yes (native)Yes (RFC 3339 native)
NumbersInteger or floatInteger, float, hex, octInteger, float, hex, oct, bin, infinity
Booleanstrue / falseMany (true/false, yes/no, on/off — Norway problem)true / false only
Nullnullnull, ~, or emptyNo native null
Trailing commasNo (strict)n/aAllowed in arrays
Schema validationJSON Schema (mature)JSON Schema (via conversion)Less common
Use CaseBest Fit
REST API request / responseJSON
CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, GitLab)YAML
Kubernetes manifestsYAML
Rust Cargo.toml / Python pyproject.tomlTOML
Configuration files for hand editingTOML or YAML
Programmatic config (machine read/write)JSON
Long-form documentation embedded in configYAML (multi-line strings, comments)
Same Data, Three FormatsFIG. 3O-1
JSON { "name": "almanac", "version": "0.1.0", "author": "mike", "tags": ["maker", "tools"], "build": { "minify": true, "output": "dist/" } }
YAML name: almanac version: 0.1.0 author: mike tags: - maker - tools build: minify: true output: dist/
TOML name = "almanac" version = "0.1.0" author = "mike" tags = ["maker", "tools"] [build] minify = true output = "dist/"
YAML Gotchas (The Norway Problem and Friends)TAB. 3O-2
GotchaCauseFix
Norway: "NO" becomes boolean falseYAML 1.1 truthy/falsy includes yes/no/on/offQuote the string: country: "NO"
Version 1.0 becomes 1 (integer)Unquoted numeric strings parsed as numbersQuote: version: "1.0"
Hex IP becomes number0x... is parsed as numberQuote IP and hex strings
Tabs vs spaces errorYAML requires spaces, not tabs, for indentEditor: show invisibles; convert tabs to spaces
Long string truncatedIndentation inside multi-line block scalarUse explicit indent indicator: |2 for 2-space
Anchors / aliases unexpected expansion&name and *name reuse valuesAvoid unless intentional; some parsers disable for security
Unicode characters break parserOlder parsers ASCII-onlySet encoding explicitly, use modern parser (PyYAML 6+, ruamel.yaml)
YAML 1.2 fixes most of these (notably the Norway problem) but many tools still use 1.1 parsers (PyYAML default is 1.1 until version 6). When in doubt, quote strings. If your config has hostnames, version numbers, or country codes, you almost certainly want quotes around them. TOML avoids these issues entirely by being strict; YAML's "human-friendly" syntax is the source of the gotchas.
JSON Schema BasicsTAB. 3O-3
KeywordPurpose
"type"Specify the JSON type: "string", "number", "integer", "boolean", "array", "object", "null"
"properties"Define object properties and their schemas
"required"Array of property names that must be present
"additionalProperties"Allow / disallow / schema for properties not in "properties"
"enum"Restrict to one of a fixed set of values
"const"Must equal exactly this value
"minimum" / "maximum"Numeric range (inclusive)
"minLength" / "maxLength"String length range
"pattern"Regex string must match
"format"Named formats: "email", "uri", "date-time", "uuid", "ipv4", "ipv6"
"items"Schema for each array element
"minItems" / "maxItems"Array length range
"uniqueItems"If true, array must not have duplicates
"$ref"Reference another schema by URI; enables reuse
"allOf" / "anyOf" / "oneOf"Combine schemas; intersection, union, exactly-one
"not"Negate a sub-schema
Validators: ajv (JavaScript, very fast), jsonschema (Python, canonical), justify (Rust). For OpenAPI / Swagger specifications, JSON Schema is the underlying constraint language. The current draft is 2020-12; many tools still target draft-07 (the most widely supported). For TOML and YAML, convert to JSON first and validate against the same schema; most validators have helpers for this.
Section 3P Software

License & Versioning Reference

Open source license comparison (permissive vs copyleft), Semantic Versioning rules, and pre-release / build metadata conventions. For Git tagging see 3L; for repository conventions and READMEs see 3K.
Open Source License ComparisonTAB. 3P-1
LicenseTypeSource must be shared?Patent grant?
MITPermissiveNoImplicit / weak
BSD 2-ClausePermissiveNoImplicit / weak
BSD 3-ClausePermissive (+ no-endorsement)NoImplicit / weak
Apache 2.0Permissive (+ patent grant + NOTICE)NoYes (explicit)
Unlicense / 0BSD / CC0Public domain dedicationNoNone
LGPL-2.1 / LGPL-3.0Weak copyleft (library link allowed)Only for the LGPL library itselfYes (LGPL 3.0)
GPL-2.0Strong copyleftYes (entire derivative)Implicit
GPL-3.0Strong copyleft (+ patent grant + anti-tivoization)Yes (entire derivative)Yes (explicit)
AGPL-3.0Network copyleft (SaaS-closing)Yes (including network use)Yes (explicit)
MPL 2.0 (Mozilla)File-level copyleft (hybrid)Yes for modified MPL files onlyYes
CC BY-SA / CC BY-NCCreative Commons (content, not code)Varies by variantNot for code
ConcernUse
Maximum reach, no constraintsMIT, BSD-2, Apache 2.0 (Apache adds patent protection)
Library that closed-source apps can linkLGPL-2.1, LGPL-3.0, MPL 2.0, Apache 2.0
Force open source on derivativesGPL-2.0, GPL-3.0
Close SaaS loophole (force source on hosted services)AGPL-3.0
Patent troll protectionApache 2.0, GPL-3.0, MPL 2.0 (explicit patent grants)
Public domain / no copyrightUnlicense, CC0, 0BSD (Unlicense most common)
Documentation, art, written contentCC BY 4.0 (permissive), CC BY-SA (share-alike)
License compatibility: code can be combined if the resulting license is at least as restrictive as each contributor. MIT/BSD/Apache can be relicensed into GPL but not the reverse. AGPL is "infectious" for network services; many companies forbid AGPL dependencies. For dual licensing (GPL + commercial), see Qt and MongoDB's approach. Always include the full license text in a LICENSE file at repo root; reference it from README. Mike's projects default to GPL-3.0 with plain-text reference (no hyperlink) as a standing convention.
Semantic Versioning (SemVer 2.0)TAB. 3P-2
ComponentWhen to Increment
MAJOR (X.0.0)Backwards-incompatible API changes
MINOR (0.X.0)Backwards-compatible new functionality
PATCH (0.0.X)Backwards-compatible bug fixes only
VersionMeaning
1.2.3Standard release; stable API
0.x.yInitial development; anything can change at any time
1.0.0-alphaPre-release; lower precedence than 1.0.0
1.0.0-alpha.1Pre-release with identifier suffix
1.0.0-beta.2Beta pre-release; alpha < beta < rc < release
1.0.0-rc.1Release candidate
1.0.0+20240601Build metadata (ignored for precedence)
1.0.0-rc.1+build.123Pre-release + build metadata combined
npm / Cargo RangeMeaning
^1.2.3Compatible: >= 1.2.3 and < 2.0.0 (npm); same minor in Cargo
~1.2.3Tilde: >= 1.2.3 and < 1.3.0 (patch updates only)
1.2.xWildcard: any patch in 1.2; same as ~1.2.0
>=1.2.3 <2.0.0Explicit range
*Any version
latestMost recent stable; bypasses range solver
SemVer is a promise, not a guarantee; library authors break it accidentally and intentionally. For dependency resolution, use lockfiles (package-lock.json, Cargo.lock, poetry.lock, Pipfile.lock) to pin exact versions for reproducible builds. The 0.x.y phase historically meant "still in design"; many libraries stay there long after they're production-ready (rust-cli "clap" was 0.x for years). When you cut 1.0.0, you commit to maintaining backwards compatibility per the rules above.
Section 1H Electrical & Electronics

Thermal Calculator

Junction temperature estimation for semiconductors and heatsink sizing. Compute Tj = Ta + P · Σθ, or solve backward for the heatsink θsa you need.
Junction TemperatureTOOL 1H-1
Set θcs to 0 and θsa to your package's θja for "no heatsink" cases. Standard junction-to-ambient datasheet values use this convention.
0°C 50°C 100°C 150°C 200°C
Total Thermal Resistance—
Temperature Rise (ΔT)—
Junction Temperature—
Margin to Tj max—
Status—
Heatsink Sizing (Reverse)TOOL 1H-2
Given power, ambient, max junction temp, θjc, and θcs, find the largest acceptable θsa. Lower θsa = bigger / better heatsink.
Required Σθ (max)—
Required θsa (max)—
Heatsink Category—
Typical θja ReferenceTAB. 1H-1
Packageθja (°C/W)Notes
Heatsink θsa CategoriesTAB. 1H-2
Heatsink TypeNatural ConvectionForced Air (fan)
Forced air typically improves θsa by 2-4× over natural convection. Real performance depends on airflow direction, fin orientation, mounting torque, and surface finish (anodized fins radiate better than bare aluminum at high T).
LDO & Linear Regulator Thermal CalculatorTOOL 1H-3
Dropout Voltage—
Power Dissipated—
Efficiency—
Junction Temperature—
Margin to 125°C—
Assessment—
P = (Vin − Vout) × Iout + Vin × Iq. The first term dominates for any meaningful load. LDOs become spectacularly inefficient at high dropout: 12V → 3.3V at 500mA wastes 4.35W as heat, which a SOT-223 cannot dissipate. Practical rule: keep P × θJA below 80°C above ambient for reasonable lifetime. For dropout above 5V or current above 500mA, use a switching regulator instead; the only reasons to suffer an LDO at high dropout are low noise (linear regulators don't switch) or tiny board area (no inductor needed).
Common Regulator Package θJA ReferenceTAB. 1H-2
PackageθJA (no pour)θJA (1 in² pour)θJA (heatsink)Typical Current
SOT-23220-260 °C/W160-200 °C/W—< 150 mA
SOT-23-5 / SC-70180-220 °C/W150-180 °C/W—< 250 mA
SOT-89100-140 °C/W80-100 °C/W—< 500 mA
SOT-22390-110 °C/W50-70 °C/W35-50 °C/W~1 A
TO-252 / DPAK70-90 °C/W25-45 °C/W15-25 °C/W~2-3 A
TO-263 / D2PAK55-70 °C/W20-30 °C/W10-15 °C/W~3-5 A
TO-220 (standing)50-65 °C/W—3-5 °C/W~5-10 A
TO-24735-45 °C/W—2-4 °C/W~10+ A
QFN (with thermal pad)—30-50 °C/W—Varies by part
θJA values are nominal; real performance varies with copper weight (2 oz vs 1 oz makes a measurable difference), via count under the tab, surrounding component heat, and airflow. Datasheet values typically assume specific JEDEC test boards (JESD51 standards). For tighter calculations use θJC (junction-to-case) plus board and ambient thermal paths, but for most design work the table θJA with a 1 in² copper pour is close enough.
Section 1I Electrical & Electronics

I²C Bus Planner

Plan your I²C chain. Add each device with its 7-bit address, see conflicts immediately, reference common sensor addresses to identify what's pinging back on a scan.
Add a DeviceTOOL 1I-1
Accepts 0x76, 76 (interpreted as hex), or 0b1110110 (binary). Standard 7-bit range: 0x08 to 0x77. Addresses 0x00-0x07 and 0x78-0x7F are reserved.
Devices On BusTAB. 1I-1
Address Space Map (7-bit, 0x00 - 0x7F)FIG. 1I-1
Each cell = one 7-bit address. Yellow = in use; red = conflict (multiple devices); gray = reserved by the spec.
Common I²C Device AddressesTAB. 1I-2
Address(es)Devices / Sensors
Many devices have 2-8 selectable addresses via ADDR pins. The first column shows the configurable range.
Section 1J Electrical & Electronics

Op-Amp Configurations

Standard op-amp topologies with formulas, schematics, and live calculations. Pick a topology, plug in resistor and capacitor values, see gain or cutoff frequency immediately.
Topology ReferenceTAB. 1J-1
TopologyTransfer FunctionInput ZNotes
All formulas assume ideal op-amps: infinite gain, infinite input impedance, zero output impedance, infinite bandwidth. Real devices have GBW (gain-bandwidth product) limits, slew rate caps, and non-zero input bias currents. Pick devices with ≥10× the bandwidth you need.
Section 1K Electrical & Electronics

MOSFET / BJT Selection Helper

Given operating parameters, compute required device ratings and search a reference of common parts. Selection guidance with reasonable safety margins; verify against datasheets for your actual application.
Selection CalculatorTOOL 1K-1
Required Vds/ce (1.5× derate)—
Required I rating (1.5×)—
Max Rds(on) for loss target—
Conduction Power Loss (typical)—
Gate Drive Requirement—
Recommended Package—
Notes—
Common MOSFETsTAB. 1K-1
Common BJTsTAB. 1K-2
Section 1L Electrical & Electronics

PCB Layout Tools

Trace impedance (microstrip, stripline, differential pair), current capacity (IPC-2221), via current and thermal via calculators, decoupling capacitor strategy, and crystal load capacitance. Defaults assume FR-4 (εr = 4.5). For stackup standards and fab DRC see 1P; for SMD pad sizing see 1Q; for high-speed bus rules see 1R.
Microstrip ImpedanceTOOL 1L-1
Z0 (single-ended)—
εeff (effective)—
Propagation Delay—
Wavelength @ 1 GHz—
Wadell / Hammerstad formula. Accurate within a few percent for 0.1 ≤ w/h ≤ 20. For 50 Ω on standard 6-mil FR-4: ≈ 11 mil. Add ~5% margin for manufacturing variation.
Current Capacity (IPC-2221)TOOL 1L-2
Min Trace Width—
Cross-Sectional Area—
Current Density—
IPC-2221 empirical formula: I = k · ΔT0.44 · A0.725. k = 0.048 (external) or 0.024 (internal). ΔT = 10°C is conservative; 20°C is common for power traces. Add vias and pour on critical power paths.
Common Substrate Dielectric ConstantsTAB. 1L-1
MaterialεrLoss TanUse Case
Differential Pair Impedance (Edge-Coupled Microstrip)TOOL 1L-3
Single-Ended Z₀—
Coupling Factor—
Differential Zdiff—
Common-Mode Zcm—
Match Quality—
Edge-coupled microstrip uses the IPC-2141 / Hammerstad approximation: Zdiff = 2 × Z₀ × (1 − 0.48 × e−0.96(S/H)). Tight coupling (S ≤ H) gives the strongest differential mode but is sensitive to dimensional variation. USB 2.0 targets 90Ω diff (±10%); HDMI, USB 3.x, gigabit Ethernet, and LVDS target 100Ω. For accurate manufacturing, ask your fab for their controlled-impedance trace dimensions; the math here is a starting point.
Stripline Impedance (Symmetric Inner Layer)TOOL 1L-4
Single-Ended Z₀ (Stripline)—
Differential Zdiff—
Velocity (% of c)—
Propagation Delay—
Symmetric stripline: trace centered between two reference planes. Uses Wheeler/IPC formula: Z₀ = (60/√εr) × ln(4B / (0.67π(0.8W + T))). Stripline has slower propagation than microstrip (full dielectric, no air) but better noise immunity and natural EMI shielding. Use stripline for high-speed clocks and noise-sensitive signals. Inner-layer routing on a 4-layer board with planes on layers 2 and 3 is the canonical stripline configuration.
Via Current CapacityTOOL 1L-5
Annular Cross-Section—
Effective Width (π × D)—
Current per Via—
Total Current (parallel)—
Assessment—
Via current is modeled as a flat conductor of width π×D and thickness equal to plating. IPC-2152 external-trace formula: I = 0.048 × ΔT^0.44 × A^0.725 (A in mil²). Rule of thumb: a 0.3 mm via with standard 1 oz plating handles roughly 1 A at 10°C rise. Stitch multiple vias for power: 4-6 parallel vias for a 5A buck regulator output is typical. Plated through-holes carry less than the equivalent trace area because heat dissipation through the hole is poorer; use the temperature-rise slider to be conservative.
Thermal Via ArrayTOOL 1L-6
θ per Via—
θ Array (parallel)—
Heat Flow Capacity—
ΔT @ given Power—
Recommendation—
Models each via as a copper cylinder: θ = L / (k × A), where k_copper = 400 W/m·K and A is the annular copper cross-section. Parallel vias divide the thermal resistance: θ_array = θ_single / N. Typical 3×3 array of 0.3mm vias under a SOT-23 or QFN gives ~6-10 K/W from die to inner plane, enough to dissipate 2-3W with reasonable margin. Filling vias with thermally-conductive paste or using filled-and-plated vias improves this further. For high-power applications (>5W) use solid copper coins or thermal pads pressed to the case.
Decoupling Capacitor StrategyTAB. 1L-2
Cap ValueRoleEffective RangePlacement
10-100 µF (bulk)Low-frequency reservoir; supplies droop from large load stepsDC to ~100 kHzOne per power rail at point-of-entry; tantalum, electrolytic, or polymer
1-10 µF (mid)Mid-frequency reservoir; supplies fast transients before bulk reacts~10 kHz to ~10 MHzOne per major IC or section; X7R/X5R ceramic
100 nF (the classic)High-frequency local store for digital edge currents~100 kHz to ~100 MHzOne per power pin, <3mm from pin, direct to plane
10 nFHigher-frequency for fast logic~1 MHz to ~500 MHzOne per pair of digital pins on fast MCUs; FPGA Vccint
1 nFVery high frequency; RF and SerDes~10 MHz to GHzOne per SerDes lane or RF stage; 0402 minimum
100 pFRF / clock decoupling~100 MHz to multi-GHzAt GHz oscillators, PA outputs; 0201 sometimes used
Use a hierarchy: one bulk cap at the power entry, one mid-value per board section, one 100nF per IC power pin. Place each cap on the IC side of the board with the shortest possible loop to ground through the plane (via to plane right next to the cap pad). The "100nF + 10nF + 1nF in parallel on one rail" trick mostly does not help below GHz; the 100nF's parasitic inductance dominates, and adding smaller caps creates anti-resonant peaks. Better to make the 100nF placement perfect than to stack more caps with bad placement.
Crystal Load CapacitanceREF. 1L-8
See also
For full crystal load capacitance math (C1 = C2 = 2 × (CL − Cstray), nearest E12 value, frequency pull estimate, gain margin), see 1E Electrical Formulas → TOOL 1E-6 Crystal & Clock Loading. For PCB layout: keep oscillator caps within 3 mm of the MCU pins, surround with continuous ground pour, tie the pour to the MCU ground pin closest to the oscillator, and do not route other signals under the crystal.
Crosstalk Estimator (Microstrip, Parallel Trace)TOOL 1L-9
Edge Rate dV/dt—
Near-End (NEXT) Coupling—
Near-End Crosstalk—
Far-End (FEXT) Coupling—
Far-End Crosstalk—
Critical Length (NEXT saturates)—
Assessment—
Approximation from Howard Johnson / IPC heuristics: KNEXT ≈ 0.25 × (1 / (1 + (S/H)²)) — near-end crosstalk saturates after the signal travels two trip-times down the parallel run (the "critical length"). KFEXT ≈ (1.1 × tpd × length / tr) × KNEXT for microstrip — far-end crosstalk scales with run length and inversely with rise time. Rule of thumb: keep S ≥ 3 × H for < 1% NEXT, S ≥ 5 × H for < 0.5%. Stripline cancels FEXT almost completely (signal and return-current share dielectric symmetrically); microstrip does not, which is why differential pairs and clocks belong on inner layers for fast designs.
Section 1M Electrical & Electronics

Filter Designer

First-order RC and second-order LC passive filters. Set the cutoff and one component value; the other is computed. Magnitude response sketch shown for context.
Filter SetupTOOL 1M-1
Enter cutoff and one component; the other is computed. For LC band-pass / band-reject filters Q = (1/R)·√(L/C); set R to control bandwidth.
Computed Component—
Standard E12 nearest—
Actual cutoff with E12—
Roll-off slope—
Time constant τ—
Q factor (LC only)—
E12 / E24 Standard Component ValuesTAB. 1M-1
SeriesToleranceValues (×10ⁿ)
E6±20%1.0 1.5 2.2 3.3 4.7 6.8
E12±10%1.0 1.2 1.5 1.8 2.2 2.7 3.3 3.9 4.7 5.6 6.8 8.2
E24±5%1.0 1.1 1.2 1.3 1.5 1.6 1.8 2.0 2.2 2.4 2.7 3.0 3.3 3.6 3.9 4.3 4.7 5.1 5.6 6.2 6.8 7.5 8.2 9.1
E48±2%48 per decade
E96±1%96 per decade
Section 1N Electrical & Electronics

Battery Reference

A reference for common rechargeable and primary chemistries (Li-ion, LiPo, LiFePO4, NiMH, NiCd, alkaline, lead-acid) covering nominal voltage, energy density, cycle life, self-discharge, and safe operating range. Includes capacity math (mAh to Wh, series and parallel pack design), runtime estimation, C-rate, and charging fundamentals. For battery management circuits and protection, see 1E Electrical Formulas and 1H Thermal Calculator.
Chemistry ComparisonFIG. 1N-1
Nominal V is the working voltage; full charge and end-of-discharge differ. Energy density is per kilogram (Wh/kg) for comparative sizing. Cycle life is to 80% original capacity at 1C discharge. Self-discharge is per month at room temperature. Operating range matters most for outdoor and unconditioned-space applications.
Capacity CalculatorTOOL 1N-1
Cell Energy—
Pack Configuration—
Pack Nominal Voltage—
Pack Capacity—
Pack Energy—
Estimated Runtime—
Discharge Rate (C)—
Series adds voltage (S×V), parallel adds capacity (P×mAh). Real-world runtime is 80-90% of theoretical due to internal resistance and load profile. Cells in parallel must be matched (same chemistry, capacity, age, voltage) or they will fight each other and one will overcharge.
C-Rate ReferenceTAB. 1N-1
C-RateTime to Full DischargeUse
0.1C10 hoursLong-duration loads (DC backup, low-power sensors)
0.2C5 hoursStandard charging rate for many chemistries
0.5C2 hoursModerate; typical for portable electronics
1C1 hourSpecification benchmark; capacity ratings use 1C
2C30 minutesTools, vapes, e-bikes (cells must support it)
5C12 minutesRC vehicles, drones; high-discharge cells only
10C+6 minutes or lessRacing drones, motor starts; specialty cells
A 3500 mAh cell at 1C delivers 3.5 A for one hour. At 2C it delivers 7 A for 30 minutes. Manufacturers spec maximum continuous and burst C-rates separately; exceeding continuous voids life and causes heating. For Li-ion, sustained discharge above 1-2C significantly reduces cycle life.
Common Cell SizesTAB. 1N-2
CellDiameter × Length (mm)Typical CapacityChemistryUse
1865018 × 652500-3500 mAhLi-ionLaptops, tools, flashlights, e-bikes, Tesla Model S
2170021 × 704000-5000 mAhLi-ionTools, e-bikes, Tesla Model 3
2665026 × 655000-6000 mAhLi-ion / LiFePO4Flashlights, energy storage
1450014 × 50700-900 mAhLi-ionAA-sized; 3.7V; not interchangeable with alkaline
1044010 × 44300-400 mAhLi-ionAAA-sized; 3.7V; not interchangeable with alkaline
AA14.5 × 50.52000-3000 mAhAlkaline / NiMHUbiquitous; 1.5V (alkaline) or 1.2V (NiMH)
AAA10.5 × 44.5800-1200 mAhAlkaline / NiMHSmall electronics; same V as AA
C26 × 506000-8000 mAhAlkaline / NiMHLarger devices, lanterns
D34 × 61.510000-12000 mAhAlkaline / NiMHLong-runtime devices
9V (PP3)26 × 17 × 48500-600 mAhAlkaline / NiMHSmoke alarms, multimeters, guitar pedals
CR203220 × 3.2220 mAhLi-MnO2 (primary)Coin cell; RTCs, key fobs, motherboards
CR123A17 × 34.51500 mAhLi-MnO2 (primary)Cameras, flashlights, smoke alarms
LiPo pouchvaries (flat)100-10000+ mAhLiPoPhones, drones, custom packs
Charging FundamentalsTAB. 1N-3
ChemistryCharge ProfileCharge VoltageFloat / EndNotes
Li-ion / LiPoCC then CV4.20 V/cellterminate at 0.05C0.5C standard, 1C max for most cells. Never overcharge.
LiFePO4CC then CV3.65 V/cellterminate at 0.05CInherently safer than Li-ion; longer cycle life.
NiMHCC, delta-V cutoffvaries-dV or temperature1.4-1.5 V at top; smart chargers detect -dV.
NiCdCC, delta-V cutoffvaries-dV or temperatureMemory effect; full discharge before recharge.
Lead-acid (flooded)3-stage: bulk / abs / float14.4 V (12V batt)13.2-13.8 V floatFloat can be maintained continuously.
Lead-acid (AGM)3-stage14.4-14.7 V13.5-13.8 V floatLower self-discharge than flooded; spill-proof.
Alkaline (primary)do not charge——Some "rechargeable alkalines" exist but cycle life is poor.
CC = constant current, CV = constant voltage. Li-ion charges to a voltage limit then holds until current tapers; this is the universal safe profile. Lead-acid has three stages: high current bulk, taper to absorption, then maintenance float. NiMH and NiCd use delta-V detection (the voltage briefly dips at full charge); good chargers also use temperature cutoff as a safety backup.
Section 1O Electrical & Electronics

Schematic Component Symbols

Standard schematic symbols for passive components, diodes, transistors, op-amps, logic gates, sources, grounds, and switches. ANSI/US conventions shown by default; IEC variants noted where they differ. For physical pinouts (which pin is which on a real package) see 1C Pinouts; for op-amp circuit topologies see 1J; for RF schematics see 1F.
Passive ComponentsFIG. 1O-1
Resistors are shown US-style (zigzag) by default; IEC-style (rectangle) is equally valid and is the default in European schematics. Polarized capacitors mark the positive terminal with a "+" and use a curved or filled plate for the negative side. Inductor symbols vary widely (humps, loops, even just a coiled line) but all read as "inductor" in context.
DiodesFIG. 1O-2
The triangle points in the direction of conventional current flow (anode to cathode). The bar at the tip of the triangle is the cathode (negative). Variants modify the cathode bar: Zener bends, Schottky brackets. Optoelectronic variants (LED, photodiode) add arrows for light: outward arrows emit, inward arrows receive.
TransistorsFIG. 1O-3
BJT arrow on emitter; NPN points out (N-channel pushes current out the emitter), PNP points in. MOSFETs: enhancement-mode shows broken channel (three short segments); depletion-mode shows solid channel. Body-diode arrow indicates channel type: arrow into device = N-channel, arrow out = P-channel. JFETs have continuous channel and gate arrow.
Op-Amps & Logic GatesFIG. 1O-4
Op-amp triangle: inverting input ("−") on top or bottom by convention varies; what matters is the labeling. Power pins (V+, V−) often omitted from schematics for clarity. Logic gates use distinctive ANSI shapes; IEC uses uniform rectangles with labels ("&", "≥1", "=1", etc.). The bubble at the output indicates inversion (NOT, NAND, NOR, XNOR).
Sources & GroundsFIG. 1O-5
Voltage sources show "+" and "−" or a longer line for positive. AC source uses sine wave inside circle. Battery: long line is positive, short line is negative; multiple cells = multiple line pairs. Ground symbols: earth ground (descending lines) for actual earth connection; chassis or signal ground (triangle) for circuit reference. Use distinct symbols for analog, digital, and earth grounds when the circuit has multiple reference planes.
Switches, Connectors & MiscFIG. 1O-6
SPST = single-pole single-throw (one input, one output, on/off). SPDT = single-pole double-throw (one input, two outputs, A or B). Push-button defaults to normally-open (NO) unless explicitly drawn as normally-closed (NC). Transformer dots indicate phasing: same-polarity instantaneous voltages on dotted terminals. Crystal symbol is rectangular body between two short capacitor-like plates.
Section 1P Electrical & Electronics

PCB Stackup & Fabrication

Standard PCB stackups (2, 4, 6, and 8 layer) with FR-4 dielectric, copper weight reference, drill size and aspect-ratio rules, and a side-by-side comparison of fab DRC minimums for common prototyping vendors (JLCPCB, OSH Park, PCBWay, Eurocircuits). Use this to decide layer count before routing and to confirm your design clears the chosen fab's tolerances. For trace impedance math see 1L; for SMD pad sizes see 1Q.
Standard PCB StackupsTAB. 1P-1
Layer CountTypical UseLayer Assignment (top → bottom)Total Thickness
2-layerHobby, simple digital, low-power, prototypesL1 signal/power · core 1.5mm · L2 signal/ground1.6 mm
4-layer (signal-ground-power-signal)Standard for MCU + RF, USB, mixed-signalL1 signal · prepreg · L2 GND · core · L3 PWR · prepreg · L4 signal1.6 mm
4-layer (signal-ground-signal-signal)Dense routing with one solid reference planeL1 signal · prepreg · L2 GND · core · L3 signal · prepreg · L4 signal/PWR1.6 mm
6-layerBGA breakout, high-speed digital, low EMIL1 sig · GND · L3 sig · L4 sig · PWR · L6 sig1.6 mm
8-layerFPGA boards, multi-GHz, multi-rail powerL1 sig · GND · L3 sig · PWR · GND · L6 sig · GND · L8 sig1.6 mm typical
Flex / rigid-flexWearables, cameras, folding mechanismsPolyimide core, ½ oz copper, coverlay both sides0.1-0.3 mm flex
Rules of thumb for layer count: 2 layers for anything DC and below ~10 MHz; 4 layers as soon as you have controlled-impedance signals or more than one supply rail; 6 layers for BGAs above ~256 balls or for boards with both high-speed and high-power; 8+ for FPGAs or multi-rail high-speed systems. Adjacency matters: signals should always have an unbroken reference plane directly below (GND ideally). Never sandwich signals between two power planes of different voltages.
Copper Weight ReferenceTAB. 1P-2
WeightThickness (µm)Thickness (mil)Typical Use
½ oz/ft²17.80.7Inner layers, fine-pitch signal traces, controlled-impedance inner stripline
1 oz/ft² (standard)351.4Outer layers default; signal traces and modest power up to ~3A on a 1mm trace
2 oz/ft² (heavy)702.8Power rails up to ~10A; thicker copper requires wider traces and gaps
3 oz/ft²1054.2Motor controllers, switching power; specialty fab and higher cost
4-6 oz/ft²140-2105.5-8.4Heavy industrial power, EV battery management, welder front ends
"Outer-2-inner-1" notation means 2 oz/ft² on outer layers, 1 oz/ft² on inner layers; very common for power boards. Heavier copper requires wider trace/space minimums because etching is less precise: at 2 oz copper, expect minimum trace/space to roughly double from the bare-copper minimum. Cost goes up roughly linearly with copper weight at most fabs; specialty heavy-copper houses (Bittele, etc.) offer 10+ oz weights.
Standard Drill Sizes & Aspect RatioTAB. 1P-3
Drill (mm)Drill (mil)UseMin Pad Diameter (mm)
0.156Microvias (HDI fabs only; laser-drilled blind vias)0.30
0.208Small signal vias (HDI capable fabs)0.40
0.2510Signal via, dense BGA breakout0.50
0.3012Standard signal/power via; the workhorse0.60
0.4016Power vias, larger TH pads0.70
0.5020Through-hole component leads (small resistors, headers)0.80
0.8031.5TH leads, 0.1" headers1.20
1.0-1.239-47TH headers, larger leads, edge mounts1.6-2.0
