Aspect ratio (board thickness / drill diameter) determines plating reliability. Standard fab tier supports 8:1 aspect ratio: a 0.30mm drill works through 2.4mm of board. For a 1.6mm board, drills down to 0.20mm are workable; below that requires HDI capable fabs. Annular ring (pad radius minus drill radius) is typically 0.10-0.15mm for standard tier and is what limits how small the pad around a given drill can be.
Fab DRC Comparison (Prototyping Vendors)TAB. 1P-4
SpecJLCPCB (standard)OSH Park (4-layer)PCBWay (standard)Eurocircuits (Standard pool)
Min trace/space5 mil / 5 mil5 mil / 5 mil5 mil / 5 mil5 mil / 5 mil (PE), 4/4 (RF)
Min drill (mech.)0.20 mm0.25 mm0.20 mm0.20 mm
Min annular ring0.13 mm0.15 mm (6 mil)0.125 mm0.125 mm
Min hole-to-copper0.20 mm0.20 mm0.15 mm0.15 mm
Min edge clearance0.20 mm0.25 mm0.20 mm0.20 mm
Min solder mask sliver0.10 mm0.10 mm0.10 mm0.10 mm
Min silkscreen text0.8 mm (0.15 mm stroke)1.0 mm0.8 mm1.0 mm
Max copper weight (std.)2 oz outer1 oz2 oz outer1 oz / 35 µm
Controlled impedanceExtra costCalculator-onlyStandard offerStandard offer
Min board size10 × 10 mmnone stated10 × 10 mm10 × 10 mm
Aspect ratio (PTH)8:17:18:1 (10:1 advanced)8:1
Values are nominal minimums for standard production tiers as of 2024-2025; check current capability pages before committing to a tight design. "Advanced" or "high-precision" tiers at each vendor go significantly tighter (3 mil trace/space, 4 mil annular ring, smaller drills) at higher cost and lead time. For first-prototype work the standard tier at any of these vendors is usually fine; the cheapest path is to design to OSH Park or JLCPCB standard tier and then verify against the vendor's published rules.
Solder Paste Powder Sizes & SelectionTAB. 1P-5
TypeParticle Size (µm)Smallest ApertureUse
T325-450.30 mm (12 mil)Most common; up to 0.5mm pitch; standard 0603/0402 reflow
T420-380.20 mm (8 mil)Fine-pitch QFN, 0402, smaller; better release through stencil
T515-250.15 mm (6 mil)Very fine pitch BGA (0.4 mm), 0201, microvia paste-in-hole
T65-150.10 mm (4 mil)Specialty; 01005 chips, 0.3 mm BGA; expensive, short shelf life
AlloyCompositionLiquidusNotes
SAC305Sn 96.5 / Ag 3.0 / Cu 0.5217-220 °CThe default lead-free alloy. Strong, expensive (silver content)
SAC0307Sn 99 / Ag 0.3 / Cu 0.7217-227 °CLow-silver lead-free; cheaper, more brittle joints
Sn-Pb eutecticSn 63 / Pb 37183 °CLower temp, easier rework, banned for consumer EU goods (RoHS)
Bi-Sn (low-temp)Sn 42 / Bi 58138 °CHeat-sensitive components, connectors with plastic shells
Sn-CuSn 99.3 / Cu 0.7227 °CCheapest lead-free; wave soldering common
No-clean fluxes are standard for most assembly; water-soluble fluxes leave conductive residues that must be washed off (and require specialized wash equipment). Solder paste shelf life is 6-12 months refrigerated, 4-8 hours at room temperature. Stir before use; never re-refrigerate after opening. For hobby reflow with hot air or a toaster oven, T3 SAC305 in a 100g tub is the standard choice.
Stencil Thickness & Area RatioTAB. 1P-6
Stencil ThicknessBest ForMin Aperture (Area Ratio > 0.66)
0.20 mm (8 mil)0805 and larger; THT pads; rugged industrial~0.65 mm square
0.15 mm (6 mil)Common general-purpose; 0603, SOIC, QFP, basic QFN~0.50 mm square
0.13 mm (5 mil)Mixed fine and coarse; 0402, 0.5mm QFN~0.42 mm square
0.10 mm (4 mil)Fine-pitch; 0.4mm BGA, 0201, smaller QFN~0.33 mm square
0.08 mm (3 mil)Ultra-fine; 0.3mm BGA, WLCSP~0.26 mm square
Area ratio = aperture-opening area / aperture-wall area. For laser-cut stainless stencils, paste releases reliably when ratio > 0.66 (some sources say 0.50 for electroformed). Below this, paste sticks to the aperture walls instead of the pad and you get insufficient paste. For a 0.15 mm stencil with a 0.5 mm square aperture: area = 0.25 mm², walls = 4 × 0.5 × 0.15 = 0.30 mm², ratio = 0.83. Stencils with mixed coarse/fine apertures often use step-down (thinner where fine, thicker elsewhere) or split into two stencils.
Reflow Profile ReferenceTAB. 1P-7
PhaseSnPb (Sn63/Pb37)SAC305 (lead-free)Bi-Sn (low-temp)
Preheat ramp1-3 °C/s to 100°C1-3 °C/s to 150°C1-2 °C/s to 80°C
Soak (flux activation)100-150°C, 60-90s150-200°C, 60-120s80-100°C, 60-90s
Ramp to peak2-3 °C/s1.5-3 °C/s1.5-2 °C/s
Peak temperature210-230°C235-250°C155-175°C
Time above liquidus (TAL)45-90s (Liq = 183°C)45-90s (Liq = 217°C)45-60s (Liq = 138°C)
Cool-down rate2-4 °C/s2-4 °C/s2-4 °C/s
Total cycle time3-5 minutes4-6 minutes3-4 minutes
Always check the paste datasheet first; manufacturers tune fluxes for specific profiles. Soak phase activates the flux and equalizes board temperature; skipping it causes tombstoning on small parts. Time above liquidus (TAL) must be long enough for full wetting but short enough to limit intermetallic growth. For hot-plate or toaster-oven reflow, a thermocouple on the board with a profile-tracking app (PhasePlot, ReflowKit) is much more reliable than timer guesses. Bi-Sn low-temp paste is excellent for boards with heat-sensitive components but is brittle and not recommended for thermal-cycling applications.
Section 1Q Electrical & Electronics

SMD Land Patterns

Reference pad sizes for common surface-mount packages following IPC-7351 nominal (Density Level B) conventions. For each package: pad dimensions, pitch, and notes on via-in-pad and fine-pitch handling. These are starting points; always cross-check against the part datasheet's recommended pattern, which takes precedence.
Chip Resistors & Capacitors (2-terminal)TAB. 1Q-1
PackageBody L × W (mm)Pad L × W (mm)Pad Gap (mm)Hand Solder?
0201 (0603 metric)0.60 × 0.300.30 × 0.300.30No (microscope only)
0402 (1005 metric)1.00 × 0.500.55 × 0.500.50Tweezers + flux, with patience
0603 (1608 metric)1.60 × 0.800.85 × 0.950.85Yes, the everyday default for hand work
0805 (2012 metric)2.00 × 1.251.15 × 1.300.90Yes, easy
1206 (3216 metric)3.20 × 1.601.65 × 1.601.80Yes, easy
1210 (3225 metric)3.20 × 2.501.65 × 2.501.80Yes
2010 (5025 metric)5.00 × 2.502.70 × 2.502.00Yes (often used for high-power resistors)
2512 (6432 metric)6.30 × 3.202.70 × 3.203.20Yes (1W+ chip resistors)
"0603" in US Imperial nomenclature is "1608" in metric; they refer to the same part. The metric designation gives the body length and width in tenths of a millimeter. For hand assembly, 0603 is the sweet spot: small enough for dense layouts, large enough to handle with tweezers and standard 0.5-0.8mm iron tips. Anything smaller than 0402 is reflow-only for most makers.
SOT & SOIC PackagesTAB. 1Q-2
PackagePitch (mm)Pad L × W (mm)Pin CountNotes
SOT-230.951.20 × 0.603Single transistor / small regulator; ubiquitous
SOT-23-50.951.20 × 0.605Op-amps, single-channel logic; same footprint as SOT-23
SOT-23-60.951.20 × 0.606USB protection ICs, single MOSFETs with body diode
SOT-891.501.65 × 0.95 + thermal pad3Small power regulator / transistor with tab
SOT-2232.301.65 × 1.95 + tab41-1.5A linear regulators; tab is heat sink
SOIC-8 (narrow)1.271.95 × 0.608Op-amps, small MCUs, EEPROMs
SOIC-16 (narrow)1.271.95 × 0.6016Logic, ADCs, drivers
SOIC-16 (wide)1.272.05 × 0.6016Wider body for higher voltage isolation
TSSOP-140.651.45 × 0.4014Smaller alternative to SOIC; harder to hand-solder
TSSOP-280.651.45 × 0.4028Common for ICs needing more I/O than SOIC offers
SSOP-200.651.45 × 0.4020Very similar to TSSOP; slightly different body height
SOIC packages have 1.27mm (50 mil) pitch and are the easiest fine-pitch packages to hand solder. TSSOP/SSOP at 0.65mm pitch require either fine-tip soldering or drag-soldering with plenty of flux. For first-time use of a 0.65mm package, lay down a thicker line of flux, tack two diagonally-opposite pins, then drag-solder each side with the iron pulling solder away from the lead row.
QFN / DFN PackagesTAB. 1Q-3
PackagePitch (mm)Body (mm)Pad L × W (mm)Notes
DFN-6 (1×1)0.501.0 × 1.00.30 × 0.20Tiny single-channel parts (ADC, ESD)
DFN-8 (2×2)0.502.0 × 2.00.35 × 0.25Small op-amps, LDOs
QFN-16 (3×3)0.503.0 × 3.00.35 × 0.25Small MCUs (e.g. ATtiny), low-pin-count ICs
QFN-20 (4×4)0.504.0 × 4.00.55 × 0.30Wireless modules, power management ICs
QFN-32 (5×5)0.505.0 × 5.00.60 × 0.30ESP32 modules, mid-range MCUs
QFN-48 (7×7)0.507.0 × 7.00.80 × 0.30STM32 mid-range, sensor hubs
QFN-64 (9×9)0.509.0 × 9.00.85 × 0.30Higher-end MCUs, audio codecs, USB hubs
QFN-100 (12×12)0.4012 × 120.65 × 0.20FPGAs (low-end), application processors
QFN central thermal pad is almost always present and must be soldered for both thermal and (often) electrical reasons (it's usually internally bonded to ground). Use a 4 × 4 array of 0.3mm thermal vias under the pad to escape heat to inner layers. Hand-soldering QFNs is possible but tedious: tack two pins, reflow the body with a hot-air station, then touch-up edge fillets. Solder paste + stencil + hot-air or hotplate is the saner workflow for QFN.
BGA PackagesTAB. 1Q-4
Pitch (mm)Ball Diameter (mm)Pad Diameter (mm)Min Trace Escape (mil)Notes
1.270.750.655 milOlder "PGA-like" BGA; easy to route
1.000.500.505 milCommon for memory, FPGAs (older parts)
0.800.400.404 milApplication processors, mid-range FPGAs
0.650.300.304 mil + microviaMobile SoCs; needs HDI
0.500.250.253 mil + microviaWLCSP (chip-scale); HDI required
0.400.200.203 mil + microviaVery dense WLCSP; specialty fabs only
BGA escape routing: for pitch ≥ 1.0mm, you can route through-vias between balls on a 2-layer board. From 0.8mm pitch down, you need a 4-layer minimum and dog-bone (via offset to pad with short trace) routing. From 0.65mm and below, microvias and HDI become mandatory: blind/buried vias laser-drilled into the pad. Outer-ball-only routing is the standard first-pass escape; inner balls drop to inner layers via dog-bones or via-in-pad.
Pad Sizing Conventions (IPC-7351 Density Levels)TAB. 1Q-5
Density LevelToe / Heel ExtensionUse Case
Level A — Most (largest pads)Larger pad past leadHand soldering, harsh environment, low-quantity prototype
Level B — NominalStandard reflow-friendlyMost commercial reflow assembly; the default
Level C — Least (smallest pads)Minimal extensionHigh-density designs, BGA escape, mobile devices
Most design tools (KiCad, Altium, Eagle) ship with Level B as default. For hand-solder prototypes, switching to Level A library variants gives you more pad to land on with the iron tip. The size differences are small (typically 5-15% larger pads) but the practical difference at the bench is real.
Section 1R Electrical & Electronics

Bus Routing Reference

Impedance targets, length limits, and length-matching tolerances for common high-speed and high-current buses. Use these as routing rules of thumb; specific protocol specs (USB-IF, JEDEC, HDMI Forum) take precedence for compliant designs. For impedance math see 1L; for connector footprints and pin assignments, see 1C Pinouts.
USB 2.0 (Full / High Speed)TAB. 1R-1
ParameterValueNotes
Differential impedance90 Ω ±15%Edge-coupled microstrip or stripline
Max trace length~150 mm (6 in)Beyond this needs equalization or repeater
Length matching (D+ ↔ D−)±0.15 mm (±6 mil)Skew < 100 ps
Length to common-mode choke< 15 mm from connectorFor ESD/EMI on the host side
Reference planeSolid ground; no splits under pairCritical for return-current continuity
StubsNone toleratedNo daisy-chain, no test points dangling
Via countMinimize; 0 ideal, 2 maxEach via adds ~30 ps skew and impedance discontinuity
USB 3.x SuperSpeed (5/10/20 Gbps)TAB. 1R-2
ParameterValueNotes
Differential impedance85-100 Ω (typ. 90 Ω)Tighter tolerance than USB 2.0
Max trace length~150 mm (USB 3.0), ~100 mm (3.1/3.2)Higher speeds = shorter loss budget
Length matching±0.13 mm (±5 mil)Within each diff pair
Intra-pair couplingTight (S ≈ W ≈ H)Keep pair geometry constant for full length
AC coupling caps100 nF (0402 GRM type, very low ESL)Required on TX side of each lane
Via stubsForbidden; back-drill or skip-layer routeAny back-drill must remove stub to < 10 mil
Reference planeSolid GND adjacent layerNo splits, no other signals near
HDMI 1.4 / 2.0 / 2.1 (TMDS Lanes)TAB. 1R-3
ParameterValueNotes
Differential impedance100 Ω ±10%Per TMDS lane
Max trace length~150 mm at 1080p, ~100 mm at 4K, ~75 mm at 8KLoss-budget driven
Length matching (within pair)±0.13 mm (±5 mil)Within each lane
Length matching (lane-to-lane)±1.27 mm (±50 mil)Between TMDS lanes
Clock-to-data skew±10 milHDMI clock lane to data lanes
Via budget≤ 2 per laneEach via degrades signal integrity
Reference planeSolid GND under entire lane groupAvoid plane splits and slots
Gigabit Ethernet (1000BASE-T)TAB. 1R-4
ParameterValueNotes
Differential impedance100 Ω ±10%Four bidirectional pairs (MDI0-3)
Max trace length~100 mm (PHY to magnetics)Plus 100 m over the Cat-5e+ cable
Length matching (intra-pair)±1.27 mm (±50 mil)1000BASE-T is more forgiving than serial protocols
Length matching (pair-to-pair)Loose; not criticalEach pair is independent
Magnetics placement< 25 mm from RJ45Between PHY and RJ45; isolation barrier required
PHY-side termination49.9 Ω center-tap to chassisBob Smith termination via 1 kΩ + 1nF to chassis ground
Chassis groundSeparate from logic GND, joined at one pointCommon-mode noise mitigation
DDR3 / DDR4 SDRAMTAB. 1R-5
ParameterDDR3DDR4
Single-ended impedance40 Ω ±10%40 Ω ±10%
Differential impedance (DQS, CK)80 Ω80 Ω
TopologyFly-by (one trace through all chips)Fly-by
Address/command skew±100 mil to longest DQS±50 mil
DQ-to-DQS skew (within byte)±25 mil±10 mil
TerminationVTT termination at end of fly-byOn-die termination (ODT); no VTT
Stackup minimum4 layer for single chip, 6 for arrays6 layer minimum
Voltage1.5 V (1.35 V LP)1.2 V (1.05 V LP)
DDR memory routing is the deepest end of mainstream PCB design. Plan on at least 6 layers, controlled impedance from the fab, and a tool with constraint-driven length matching (KiCad has it; expect to spend real time on it). For one chip on a known-good reference design, you can copy the schematic + layout from the SoC vendor's eval board and skip a lot of this. For multi-chip arrays, you need to read the JEDEC docs.
LVDS / SerDes LanesTAB. 1R-6
ParameterValueNotes
Differential impedance100 Ω ±10%Common to FPD-Link, V-by-One, JESD204B
Max trace length~250 mm (low-speed), ~100 mm (multi-Gbps)Speed-dependent; check standard
Intra-pair skew±5 mil typicalTighter for higher speeds
Termination100 Ω at receiverSingle resistor across pair; close to RX
AC coupling100 nF (0402 GRM)Required for JESD204B; optional for FPD-Link
Common-mode chokesNot on the signal pathUse ESD only at connector; never inline
I²C / SPI / CAN (Lower-Speed)TAB. 1R-7
BusTopologyTerminationLength / Capacitance Limit
I²C Standard (100 kHz)Multi-drop, open-drainPullup to Vcc (4.7-10 kΩ)~400 pF total bus capacitance
I²C Fast (400 kHz)Multi-drop, open-drainPullup to Vcc (2.2-4.7 kΩ)~400 pF total
I²C Fast+ (1 MHz)Multi-drop, open-drainPullup (1-2.2 kΩ) or active~550 pF total
SPI (any speed)Master + multi-slave (CS each)Source-series 22-33 Ω if > 25 MHzSpeed-limited by capacitance & ringing
CAN 2.0 (1 Mbps)Bus (linear topology)120 Ω at each end~40 m at 1 Mbps, ~500 m at 125 kbps
RS-485Bus (linear)120 Ω at each end + bias resistors~1200 m at 100 kbps
RS-232Point-to-pointNone~15 m at 19.2 kbps
I²C bus capacitance is the most common gotcha: each device contributes ~10 pF, plus 1-2 pF/cm of trace. A 400 kHz bus with 20 devices on a 30 cm trace network is already near the limit. If you have to go longer or faster, use an I²C buffer (PCA9517, P82B96) or lower the pullups. SPI doesn't have a hard length limit but ringing kills it; 22-33 Ω source-series resistors near the MCU are cheap insurance above 25 MHz.
Section 1S Electrical & Electronics

Pull-up & Pull-down Resistors

Sizing rules and gotchas for pull-up and pull-down resistors. I²C bus pull-up calculator (capacitance, speed, and supply voltage to maximum resistance), typical values by signal type, and notes on internal MCU pull-ups, MOSFET gate handling, and reset/boot configuration. For I²C bus topology see 1I; for SPI and slower bus rules see 1R.
I²C Pull-up Resistor CalculatorTOOL 1S-1
Max Pull-up (rise time)—
Min Pull-up (sink current)—
Recommended Value—
Sink Current @ V_OL—
Assessment—
Rmax from rise time: tr = 0.8473 × R × C, solving R = tr / (0.8473 × C). Rmin from sink current: R = (Vcc − VOL) / IOL, with VOL = 0.4V typical. The bus speed sets the maximum allowed rise time per the I²C spec. Higher capacitance forces lower R (faster pull-up) which forces higher sink current. For 100 pF bus at 400 kHz: 2.2-4.7 kΩ is typical. For long busses or many devices, use an active buffer rather than fighting the math.
Typical Pull-up / Pull-down Values by Signal TypeTAB. 1S-1
Signal TypeTypical ValueRangeNotes
I²C bus pull-up (3.3V)4.7 kΩ2.2-10 kΩLower for fast-mode or high capacitance buses
I²C bus pull-up (5V)10 kΩ4.7-10 kΩHigher Vcc needs higher R to keep sink current in spec
GPIO input bias (unused)10-100 kΩ10 kΩ-1 MΩPrevent floating; balance leakage vs noise immunity
Reset / NRST line10 kΩ4.7-100 kΩPair with a 100nF cap to ground for clean POR
MCU boot / config strap10 kΩ4.7-47 kΩBoot mode pins on ESP32, STM32; must dominate strap state
MOSFET gate pull-down (logic-level)10-100 kΩ1 kΩ-1 MΩEnsures OFF state when driver is high-Z (power-up, reset)
MOSFET gate pull-down (high-side)10-100 kΩ10-470 kΩTied to source, not GND; varies with topology
SPI CS (chip select) idle10 kΩ pull-up—Prevents bus contention if MCU is in reset / unconfigured
USB D+ (full-speed detect)1.5 kΩ to 3.3V—Specific spec value; not a typical pull-up
UART RX (idle protection)100 kΩ to Vcc10-470 kΩOptional; keeps line idle-high when transmitter disconnects
Button input (to ground)10 kΩ pull-up1-100 kΩOften handled by MCU internal pull-up; add external for noise
JTAG / SWD lines10-100 kΩ—TMS, TDI typically pull-up; TCK pull-down; check JEDEC
Internal MCU Pull-up / Pull-down ReferenceTAB. 1S-2
MCU FamilyInternal Pull-upNotes
AVR (ATmega, ATtiny)~20-50 kΩPull-up only (no internal pull-down on most parts)
STM32 (most)30-50 kΩBoth pull-up and pull-down available per pin
ESP32 / ESP32-S3~45 kΩBoth directions; not on strap pins during boot
RP2040 (Pi Pico)~50-80 kΩBoth directions; pad current can be configured
SAM D / E (Atmel)~40 kΩBoth directions; per-pin configurable
PIC (mid-range)~50-200 kΩPull-up only on most parts; weak (high R)
MSP430~20-50 kΩBoth directions
Internal pull-ups are convenient but weak: they take 20-50 µs to charge a typical bus, which is too slow for fast I²C or high-capacitance loads. Internal pull-ups are fine for GPIO buttons and unused pins, but always use external resistors for I²C bus and any signal where rise time matters. Internal pull-ups are also typically disabled during reset, which is why straps need external resistors that "win" against the floating-input state.
Gotchas & Design NotesTAB. 1S-3
GotchaWhat HappensFix
Pull-up too weak on I²CSlow rise time; data corruption at fast speedsDrop R; or use buffer for very long buses
Pull-up too strong on I²CExcess sink current required; weak devices failCheck V_OL spec of weakest device on bus
Floating MOSFET gateRandom turn-on during MCU boot or reset10-100 kΩ pull-down (logic-level) or pull-up (high-side)
Reset line bouncingMultiple resets per power-up; brown-out misbehavior10 kΩ pull-up + 100 nF cap; consider supervisor IC
Strap pin contestedMCU boots into wrong mode (USB, UART, flash)Strap resistor must dominate; check internal pull direction
GPIO leakage on tight pull-upVoltage divider effect; pin reads incorrectlyPull-up < 100 kΩ for noisy environments; check I_leak spec
Open-drain to higher VccPull-up clamped by IC ESD diode to its own VccUse proper level shifter (BSS138 + 10 kΩ trick or dedicated IC)
Series + pull-up mismatchRC divider creates slow signal; signal misreadSource-series < 10% of pull-up value
Section 1T Electrical & Electronics

Connector Reference

Quick reference for common connector pinouts and footprints: USB family, Ethernet RJ45, SD/microSD, JST family, common pin headers, programming headers (JTAG, SWD, UART, ICSP), and power barrel jacks. For high-speed routing rules of buses using these connectors see 1R; for MCU pin-side pinouts see 1C.
USB Connector FamilyTAB. 1T-1
ConnectorPinsPinoutFootprint Notes
USB-A (host)41: VBUS (+5V), 2: D−, 3: D+, 4: GNDThrough-hole standard; 4 signal + 2 shield tabs
USB-B (device, classic)4Same as USB-ALarger device-side connector; older printers, audio
Mini-USB51: VBUS, 2: D−, 3: D+, 4: ID, 5: GNDMostly retired; ID pin for OTG
Micro-USB B5Same as Mini-USBMostly replaced by USB-C; still common in low-cost devices
USB-C (basic 2.0 only)6 used of 24VBUS × 2, GND × 2, CC1, CC2, D+, D−5.1 kΩ to GND on CC1/CC2 for device; 56 kΩ to VBUS for host
USB-C (full SuperSpeed)244× SuperSpeed pairs, 2× USB 2.0, CC1/CC2, SBU1/SBU2, VBUS × 4, GND × 4Reversible; CC pins set orientation; flat C-style ESD/EMI design required
USB-C minimum-viable design (USB 2.0 only, no Power Delivery): connect VBUS to power, D+ and D− to USB 2.0 pair, CC1 and CC2 each through a 5.1 kΩ resistor to GND. Without the CC resistors a host won't enumerate the device. For PD or DisplayPort Alt Mode you need a USB-C PD controller IC; this is not a DIY-friendly area. SuperSpeed traces are routed only on inner pairs and must be impedance-controlled (90 Ω diff).
Ethernet RJ45 (T568A / T568B)TAB. 1T-2
PinT568AT568B10/100 BASE-T1000 BASE-T
1White/GreenWhite/OrangeTX+BI_DA+
2GreenOrangeTX−BI_DA−
3White/OrangeWhite/GreenRX+BI_DB+
4BlueBlueunusedBI_DC+
5White/BlueWhite/BlueunusedBI_DC−
6OrangeGreenRX−BI_DB−
7White/BrownWhite/BrownunusedBI_DD+
8BrownBrownunusedBI_DD−
T568B is the dominant standard in commercial wiring; T568A is the older residential standard. They are pin-compatible (same physical jack) but the color order on the paired wires differs. For PCB designs you connect via a magnetics-integrated RJ45 (HanRun, Bel, Pulse) or use discrete magnetics + a plain RJ45. The four diff pairs go to the PHY in pin-order; PHY datasheet maps MDI0-3 to BI_DA-D pairs. PoE-capable jacks have center taps brought out for 48V power injection (Mode A or Mode B).
SD Card / microSD PinoutTAB. 1T-3
microSD PinSPI ModeSD 1-bitSD 4-bit
1CS (chip select)DAT3DAT3
2MOSI (data in)CMDCMD
3VSS (GND)VSSVSS
4VDD (2.7-3.6 V)VDDVDD
5CLK (SCK)CLKCLK
6VSS (GND)VSSVSS
7MISO (data out)DAT0DAT0
8(reserved)(unused)DAT1
9(reserved)(unused)DAT2
For most microcontroller projects, SD cards run in SPI mode: only CS, MOSI, MISO, CLK, VDD, VSS are connected. Add a 10 kΩ pull-up on each data line (CMD, DAT0-3, or CS/MOSI/MISO in SPI mode). The card runs on 2.7-3.6V; 5V MCUs need level shifting on every signal line. Full SD 4-bit mode is faster but requires more complex host controller support; SPI mode is simpler and works on any MCU with an SPI peripheral.
JST Connector FamilyTAB. 1T-4
SeriesPitchCurrent/PinTypical Use
SH1.00 mm1 ATiny; common on dev boards (Pixhawk, Sparkfun Qwiic alternative)
ZH1.50 mm1 ACompact; battery balance leads on small RC packs
PH2.00 mm2 AThe hobby workhorse; battery connectors, LCD modules, sensors
XH2.50 mm3 AMid-size; common on LiPo balance leads, fans
EH2.50 mm3 ALocking variant of XH
VH3.96 mm10 APower; older equipment
NH5.00 mm10 AHigh-current power; appliance internals
GH (top-entry)1.25 mm1 ALocking version of SH; resists vibration
JST PH 2.0mm is so common it has become the de facto standard for hobby/dev-board battery connections (Adafruit, Sparkfun, and many LiPo cells use it natively). Watch polarity: there is no universal convention for which pin is positive. Battery packs from one vendor may have opposite polarity from another with the same connector. Always verify before plugging in or you get a short across the cell. JST connectors are crimp, not solder; you need a proper crimper for clean assembly.
Pin Header StandardsTAB. 1T-5
PitchCommon NameDrillUse
2.54 mm (0.100")Standard / 0.1" / breadboard0.9-1.0 mmThe most common pin header; breadboards, Dupont jumpers, ICSP
2.00 mm2 mm header0.7-0.8 mmCompact; many sensors, LCD modules, BLE/Wi-Fi modules
1.27 mm (0.050")Sub-D / Picoblade-style0.5 mmJTAG, SWD, programming headers, compact debug
1.00 mm1 mm header0.4 mmHigh-density board-to-board, dev kit add-ons
0.50 mm0.5 mm FFC/FPCSMD onlyFlex cables, cameras, displays; not a typical pin header
Programming & Debug HeadersTAB. 1T-6
HeaderPitchPinsLayout
ARM JTAG (20-pin)2.54 mm202×10 row; TCK TMS TDO TDI nTRST nSRST Vcc GND × multiple
ARM SWD (Cortex Debug, 10-pin)1.27 mm102×5 row; Vtref SWDIO SWCLK SWO nRESET GND × 4 + key
ARM SWD (compact, 4-pin)2.54 mm4-5Linear or 2×2: Vcc GND SWDIO SWCLK (nRST optional)
AVR ISP (6-pin)2.54 mm62×3 row; MISO Vcc SCK MOSI RESET GND
AVR ISP (10-pin, legacy)2.54 mm102×5 row; older Atmel programmers (STK500)
UART (FTDI-style)2.54 mm6Linear: GND CTS Vcc TX RX DTR (FTDI cable convention)
UART (Sparkfun)2.54 mm6Linear: DTR RX TX Vcc CTS GND (note reversed)
PIC ICSP (5-pin)2.54 mm5-6Linear: MCLR Vdd GND ICSPDAT ICSPCLK (+ LVP/AUX)
The ARM Cortex Debug 10-pin (1.27mm) header is the modern standard, supported by ST-Link, J-Link, and Black Magic Probe. It uses keyed shrouds so you can't plug in backwards. For space-constrained designs, a 4-pin SWD pinout (Vcc, GND, SWDIO, SWCLK) is sufficient for programming and debugging most ARM Cortex-M parts. UART pinouts vary frustratingly by manufacturer: always check before connecting a FTDI cable; many a dead board has come from swapped TX/RX or reversed Vcc/GND.
Power Barrel JacksTAB. 1T-7
Outer × InnerVoltageTypical Use
5.5 × 2.1 mm5-24 V DCMost hobby boards, Arduino-style; the universal default
5.5 × 2.5 mm5-24 V DCSlightly larger inner; "wrong" plug fits but loose; check before use
3.5 × 1.3 mm3-12 V DCSmall electronics, USB hubs, small adapters
4.0 × 1.7 mm5-19 V DCSome routers, audio gear, older Sony devices
6.3 × 3.0 mm12-24 V DC, >3 ALarger laptops, mid-power gear
7.4 × 5.0 mm19-20 V DC, >5 AHP / Dell laptops; high-current
Center-positive is the modern convention for almost all DC barrel-jack equipment; if a device wants center-negative it will be marked. The schematic symbol shows the polarity as concentric circles with a dash on whichever lead matches the center. Wrong polarity will damage anything without reverse-protection. Adding a reverse-biased Schottky from Vin to GND with a series fuse, or a MOSFET-based ideal diode (LTC4359 or similar), is cheap insurance.
Section 1U Electrical & Electronics

EMI Mitigation Reference

Working reference for reducing radiated and conducted emissions: common-mode chokes, ferrite beads, board-level shielding, and the slot-antenna geometry to avoid. Covers when each technique applies, typical component values, and PCB layout patterns. For controlled-impedance routing and reference planes see 1L; for connector ESD considerations see 1T.
Common EMI Sources & MitigationsTAB. 1U-1
SourceMechanismPrimary Mitigation
Switching regulatorFast dI/dt at switch node creates broadband noise to ~300 MHzMinimize hot loop; input cap right at IC pin; spread-spectrum mode if available
Digital clock harmonicsSquare waves emit harmonics up to GHz rangeSeries source termination (22-33 Ω); spread-spectrum clock; route on inner layer
Cable-conducted noiseCommon-mode noise rides on power and signal cablesCommon-mode chokes at I/O; ferrite beads on power feeds
Ground bounceHigh dI/dt in return path raises GND momentarily above 0VSolid GND plane; decoupling caps near every IC; star or fishbone return for sensitive analog
Slot in reference planeForces return current to detour; trace becomes a loop antennaNo splits under high-speed signals; bridge with stitching caps if unavoidable
Crystal & oscillator radiationResonant circuit radiates harmonics at clock frequencyGround guard ring; ground pour under crystal; metal can if needed
High-speed pair stubsStubs ring at λ/4 frequencyBack-drill or route on inner layer with no stub; or use short pads
Floating heatsinkActs as patch antenna for switch-node noise capacitively coupled through TIMTie heatsink to GND; use grounded TIM if isolated; insulated TIM for clearance
Common-Mode Choke ReferenceTAB. 1U-2
ApplicationTypical Impedance @ 100 MHzCurrent RatingCommon Part Numbers
USB 2.0 data lines90 Ω @ 100 MHz300 mAMurata DLW21SN900 series, TDK ACM2012
USB 3.0 SuperSpeed90 Ω @ 100 MHz (low DCR)300-500 mAMurata DLP11SN, Würth WE-CNSW HF
CAN bus51-100 Ω @ 100 MHz100-200 mAWürth 744232101, TDK ACT45B
Ethernet (10/100, signal)Built into magnetics—Part of integrated magnetics jack
AC power input10 mH (line current)1-10 AWürth 744843, TDK B82731-M, Schaffner FN
DC power input (low V)100-1000 µH1-5 AWürth 744226, Bourns SRF series
Audio signal lines90-600 Ω @ 100 MHz200 mAMurata DLW31S, TDK ACM3225
A common-mode choke is two windings on a shared core: differential currents (signal) cancel in the core, common-mode currents (noise) see the full inductance. Placement: at the I/O connector entry, not in the middle of the cable run. The choke must be the closest component to the connector pad on the inside; ESD diodes and decoupling caps go between the choke and the system. Wrong polarity (winding sense reversed) makes it useless; check the dot or marking on the package against the schematic.
Ferrite Bead SelectionTAB. 1U-3
Bead ClassPeak Impedance RangeUse CaseWatch Out For
Low impedance (60 Ω @ 100 MHz)10-100 Ω broadbandSignal lines, low-current bias, audioLow effect on EMI; mainly for damping resonance
Mid impedance (120-220 Ω)100-300 Ω @ 100 MHzGeneral logic supply rails (1.8V, 3.3V, 5V)The typical "throw a bead at it" choice
High impedance (600-1000 Ω)600-1500 Ω @ 100 MHzSensitive analog supply, ADC/DAC VDDADCR can be 0.5-2 Ω; voltage drop matters at high I
Ultra-high impedance (2200+ Ω)2200-3000 Ω @ 100 MHzWireless module RF supply (Wi-Fi, BT, cellular)High DCR; designed for low-current applications
High-current beads100-600 Ω @ 100 MHzPower rails at 1-6 A; large package (1206, 1812)Lower peak impedance for higher current rating
A ferrite bead is a lossy series impedance: at low frequency it's nearly a wire (low DCR), at the bead's peak frequency it dissipates EMI as heat. Caveats: (1) Beads + capacitors can resonate and amplify noise at certain frequencies; verify with measurement, not assumption. (2) Beads on switching regulator outputs disrupt the feedback loop if placed inside the loop; always place them after the output sense point. (3) High-impedance beads have meaningful DCR; check the voltage drop at full current. (4) Murata, Würth, and TDK all publish impedance-vs-frequency curves; pick a bead whose peak aligns with the noise you actually have.
Shielding ReferenceTAB. 1U-4
Shielding MethodFrequency RangeUse CaseNotes
PCB shield can (board-mount)30 MHz to 6 GHzRF modules (Wi-Fi, BT, cellular, GPS), sensitive analogSolderable nickel-silver or steel can; needs grounded fence ring of vias on PCB
Conductive gasket (chassis)30 MHz to 18 GHzEnclosure seams; lid-to-body interfacesFingerstock, conductive elastomer, or copper mesh; needs clean conductive contact
Conductive paint / spray30 MHz to 1 GHzPlastic enclosures (consumer electronics)Nickel or copper paint inside enclosure; lower effectiveness than metal
Faraday cage (full enclosure)DC to 40 GHzTest setups, sensitive instrumentation, EMC compliance labsContinuous metal box with grounded seams; bonding straps where panels meet
Shielded cable10 kHz to ~1 GHzSensor leads, audio, USB, videoSingle-end-grounded shield for analog low-frequency; both-end for RF
Ground pour / fence vias100 MHz+Inside PCB between sensitive and noisy circuitsStitching vias spaced < λ/10 of highest frequency of interest
Shielding effectiveness (SE) depends on aperture size, not just material. A 1 GHz frequency has λ = 300 mm; effective shielding requires apertures smaller than λ/20 (15 mm) for > 20 dB attenuation. Any unsealed seam, vent hole, or cable pass-through is a potential antenna. Cable-pass-through is the most common shielding failure: a shielded box with an unfiltered cable exiting is barely better than no shield. Use feedthrough capacitors, conductive bushings, or pi-filters at every cable port.
Slot Antennas to AvoidTAB. 1U-5
Layout MistakeWhy It RadiatesFix
Plane split under high-speed traceReturn current diverts around split; loop area increases; loop radiatesDon't split planes under signals. If unavoidable, bridge with 100 nF stitching caps every 10 mm.
Connector cut-out in ground planeSlot in plane resonates at λ/2 of slot lengthBring the GND plane right up to the connector pads; minimize keepout area
Long power-rail islandPower island forms a patch antenna when not stitched to groundStitching caps (100 nF) every 10-15 mm between rail and GND
Cutout for through-hole connector pinsPin field creates an effective slot when not stitchedAdd GND vias around the pin field to maintain plane continuity
Crystal cutout in groundCrystal sits over a slot; near-field couples to anything nearbySolid GND plane under crystal; ground guard ring around crystal body
Long unstitched edge of boardEdge of board radiates if signals run near itStitch GND vias along edges every λ/20 of highest frequency
Asymmetric return pathSignal on outer layer with reference plane two layers downReference plane directly under signal, every time, no exceptions
Most "mysterious" EMI failures trace back to one of these patterns. The standard diagnostic: get a near-field probe (or a piece of wire on a scope), sweep over the board, and look for the loudest spot. It will almost always be at a slot or split. The fix is geometric, not component-level: rearrange the layout so return current flows in the smallest possible loop directly under the signal.
Field Reduction Checklist (Pre-EMC)TAB. 1U-6
StageAction
1. LayoutSolid GND plane on layer 2 (4-layer min) with no splits under signals; reference plane directly below every signal
2. Power entryCommon-mode choke + bulk cap + ceramic cap at every external power input
3. I/OCommon-mode choke + ESD diodes + ferrite bead on every cable interface
4. Switching regHot loop < 25 mm² area; input cap right at IC; ground polygon shield
5. Decoupling100 nF per IC power pin, < 3 mm from pin, ground via right next to cap pad
6. CrystalGround pour under crystal, guard ring of vias, ground caps tied directly to MCU GND pin
7. High-speedSource termination (22-33 Ω) on clocks; differential routing for SerDes; impedance matched
8. ConnectorsPad-side GND continuous to body of connector; shielded I/O grounded at connector shell
9. MechanicalConductive gasket between lid and body; cable shields bonded to chassis at entry
10. Pre-scanNear-field probe sweep of the board before EMC chamber; fix the loud spots first
The cheap insurance order: layout first (free), then bulk caps and ferrite beads (cents), then common-mode chokes (dollar each), then shielding (tens of dollars), then redesign (weeks). Most EMC failures are caught at step 1 if the layout is done right; the rest of the list is for when step 1 was rushed or constrained. If you have any choice, do EMC pre-compliance scans at a local lab or buy a near-field probe ($50-200 used) before committing to a chamber-quality test ($5000+).
Section 2F Mechanical

Fastener Torque Reference

Recommended torque values for standard metric (ISO classes 8.8, 10.9, 12.9) and imperial (SAE grades 5 and 8) fasteners. Dry, lubricated, and threadlocker conditions all change these values; values shown are for dry, clean threads.
Unit ConverterTOOL 2F-1
1 N·m = 0.7376 ft·lb = 8.851 in·lb = 10.197 kgf·cm.
Metric Fasteners (ISO Class 8.8, 10.9, 12.9)TAB. 2F-1
SizePitch (mm)Hex (mm)8.8 (N·m)10.9 (N·m)12.9 (N·m)
8.8 = medium-carbon steel (≈ Grade 5), 10.9 = alloy steel (≈ Grade 8), 12.9 = high-tensile alloy. Dry threads, K = 0.20. Reduce by ~25% for lubricated threads or with anti-seize.
Imperial Fasteners (SAE Grade 5 & 8)TAB. 2F-2
SizeTPIHex (in)Grade 5 (ft·lb)Grade 8 (ft·lb)
Grade 5 = medium carbon (≈ Class 8.8), Grade 8 = alloy steel (≈ Class 10.9). Dry threads. Values shown are for coarse (UNC) threads.
Threaded Insert ReferenceTAB. 2F-3
TypeInstall MethodPilot HoleUse
Heat-set (brass)Soldering iron at 200-250°C~0.1 mm under insert OD3D printed plastic; the standard for printed enclosures
Press-in (knurled)Press or hammer~0.1-0.2 mm under insert ODSoft wood, plywood, soft plastics
Expansion (split-collar)Tighten screw; collar expandsInsert OD nominalRemovable; concrete, hollow walls (drywall anchors)
Self-tappingScrew insert in directly~0.5 mm under thread ODHardwood, metal, dense plastic; one-shot install
Helicoil (wire insert)STI tap then install with toolPer Helicoil tap chartRepair stripped threads in metal; aerospace strength
Time-Sert / KeensertSTI tap then installPer insert specPermanent repair; stronger than Helicoil; locks via key
PEM nut / clinch nutPress in sheet metalRound hole at insert specSheet metal > 1 mm; appliances, electronics enclosures
Rivet nut (rivnut)Pull tool deforms back collarRound hole at body ODSheet metal where back is inaccessible; auto, agriculture
For heat-set inserts in PLA / PETG / ABS: use a temperature-controlled iron at 200-220°C with a specialized tip (McMaster 92395A, or 3D-printed-friendly Ruthex). Lower the insert slowly so the plastic melts around the knurls; do not force it. The plastic must flow into the knurls without distorting the surrounding wall. For tight fits, raise the temperature 10-20°C. For PEEK and other engineering plastics, ultrasonic insertion is preferred over thermal. Helicoil installation requires a STI (Special Thread Insert) tap one size larger than the bolt; check the Helicoil chart before drilling.
Rivet ReferenceTAB. 2F-4
TypePilot HoleGrip RangeUse
Blind (pop) rivet, aluminum~0.1 mm over body ODStamped on rivetSheet metal, light fab; one-side access
Blind (pop) rivet, steel~0.1 mm over body ODStamped on rivetHeavier sheet metal, structural fab
Blind (pop) rivet, stainless~0.1 mm over body ODStamped on rivetMarine, food-service, outdoor
Closed-end blind rivet~0.1 mm over body ODStamped on rivetSealing; weather and water resistance
Multi-grip blind rivet~0.1 mm over body ODWide range, one rivetVariable material thickness; reduce inventory SKUs
Structural blind (CherryMAX, Avdel)Per specPer specAircraft, structural; near-flush after install
Solid rivet (steel/aluminum/copper)~0.05 mm over body ODSet with bucking barAircraft skins, traditional structures; both-side access
Self-piercing rivet (SPR)None (rivet pierces sheet)Per specAutomotive body assembly; no pre-drilling
Tubular rivet~0.05 mm over body ODVariousLeather, fabric, light fab; clinch by deforming hollow end
Pop rivet sizes (US)3/32, 1/8, 5/32, 3/16, 1/4 inch body diameter
Pop rivet sizes (metric)2.4, 3.2, 4.0, 4.8, 6.4 mm body diameter
Blind rivets are limited by their shear strength: aluminum at ~50 lb/3.2mm, steel at ~150 lb, stainless at ~200 lb (per rivet). For structural loads use solid rivets or specialty blind rivets like CherryMAX. The grip range is the total material thickness the rivet can clamp; pick the rivet whose range includes your stackup. Multi-grip rivets cost more but reduce SKU count for varied work. Always drill the hole 0.1 mm (a few thousandths) larger than the rivet body so it slips in cleanly; tight holes prevent the rivet from setting properly.
Section 2G Mechanical

Beam Deflection

Maximum deflection for standard beam configurations under static loading. Useful for enclosure brackets, mounting rails, shelves, and quick "will this bend too much" sanity checks.
Deflection CalculatorTOOL 2G-1
Second Moment of Area I—
Max Deflection δ—
Max Bending Stress σ—
Deflection per length—
Stiffness assessment—
For static loads only. Buckling, fatigue, and dynamic loading are not considered here. The deflection/length ratio gives a quick stiffness check: 1/360 is typical floor limit, 1/240 is loose, 1/150+ feels rubbery.
Section 2H Mechanical

Bearing & Shaft Fits (ISO 286)

Reference for common hole-basis fits per ISO 286. Hole tolerance is H7 in all standard combinations; shaft tolerance varies by fit class. Tolerance values shown in micrometers (µm) for the indicated nominal-size range.
Fit LookupTOOL 2H-1
Hole tolerance (µm)—
Shaft tolerance (µm)—
Max material clearance—
Min material clearance—
Fit type—
Positive values = clearance, negative = interference. ISO 286 tolerance values from the standard for nominal-size brackets (≤3, >3-6, >6-10, >10-18, >18-30, >30-50, >50-80, >80-120, >120-180, >180-250). For interference fits, calculate press-on force and check the shaft and bore yield stress before assembly.
Press & Shrink Fit CalculatorTOOL 2H-2
Interface Pressure—
Hoop Stress (Bore)—
Press-On Force—
Bore Heat to Install (ΔT)—
Shaft Cool to Install (ΔT)—
Assessment—
Lamé equations for thick-walled cylinders. Interface pressure: P = δ × E / (D × [(D₂² + D²) / (D₂² − D²) + ν]) for an internal solid shaft and external hollow bore. Press-on force F = π × D × L × P × µ, with µ ≈ 0.15 for steel-on-steel dry. Shrink fit installation: heat bore by ΔT = δ / (α × D) for a clearance equal to half the interference; oil-quench cooling shaft works similarly. Thermal installation gives zero scuffing; cold press-fit galls if alignment is off or if friction generates enough heat to deform locally.
Section 2I Mechanical

Material Properties Reference

Density, modulus, strength, thermal expansion, and machinability for the materials you actually use at the bench. Filterable by category. Beam-deflection (2G) reads its E and yield values from this same database.
FilterTOOL 2I-1
Property KeyTAB. 2I-1
SymbolPropertyUnitWhat it tells you
EYoung's modulus (elastic)GPaStiffness in tension and bending. Higher means less deflection per load.
σ_yYield strengthMPaStress where permanent deformation begins. Stay well below.
σ_uUltimate tensile strengthMPaMaximum stress before fracture in tension.
ρDensityg/cm³Mass per volume. Multiply by part volume for weight.
αCoefficient of thermal expansionµm/m·KLength change per degree. Critical for press fits and dissimilar materials.
kThermal conductivityW/m·KHeat transfer rate. Matters for heatsinks and bearing surfaces.
Mach.Machinability rating%Relative to free-machining brass C360 = 100. Higher is easier.
Steel & Alloy Selection GuideTAB. 2I-2
SteelCarbon %Yield (MPa)Hardenable?Use
1018 (low-carbon)0.18~370Case-hardenable onlyGeneral shafts, brackets, weldable; the default mild steel
1045 (medium-carbon)0.45~500Yes (water/oil quench)Shafts, gears, axles; stronger than 1018 with light heat treat
1095 (high-carbon)0.95~525 ann.Yes (high HRC achievable)Knives, springs, simple cutting tools; takes a fine edge
4140 (alloy)0.40 Cr-Mo~655 ann.Yes (oil quench)Heavy shafts, gears, automotive axles; deeper hardenability than 1045
4340 (alloy)0.40 Ni-Cr-Mo~745 ann.Yes (oil quench)Aircraft landing gear, heavy automotive; tough, deep hardenable
12L14 (free-machining)0.15 + Pb~415No (lead impedes HT)Lathe production parts, fittings; cuts like butter, not for HT
A2 (tool steel, air-hardening)1.0 + Cr-Mo-V62 HRC achievableYes (air quench)Cold-work dies, blanking punches; minimal distortion
D2 (tool steel, high-Cr)1.5 + 12 Cr-V62 HRC achievableYes (air quench)Long-run punches, slitting blades; wear-resistant
O1 (tool steel, oil-hardening)0.9 + Mn-W-Cr62 HRC achievableYes (oil quench)Knives, small punches, gauges; classic shop knife steel
W1 (water-hardening)1.066 HRC achievableYes (water quench)Simple cutting tools, files; the original carbon tool steel
S7 (shock-resistant)0.5 + Cr-Mo58 HRC achievableYes (air quench)Chisels, jackhammer bits; tough rather than hard
M2 (HSS)0.85 + 6W-5Mo-4Cr-2V65 HRC achievableYes (oil quench)Drill bits, end mills, lathe tools; retains hardness at red heat
304 stainless<0.08 + 18Cr-8Ni~205No (austenitic)Food, marine, general corrosion resistance; weldable
316 stainless<0.08 + 16Cr-10Ni-2Mo~205No (austenitic)Salt-water marine, medical; better corrosion than 304
17-4 PH stainless<0.07 + 17Cr-4Ni precip.~1100Yes (age hardening)Aerospace shafts, valve stems; corrosion + high strength
Plain-carbon steels (10xx series) are cheap and weldable; alloy steels (41xx, 43xx) trade weldability for hardenability and through-thickness strength. Tool steels (A, D, O, W, S, M) are for cutting and forming hard things; pick the family by quench medium (W = water, O = oil, A = air) and end use. Stainless: 304 for general, 316 for salty environments, 17-4 PH when you need stainless that's also strong. The 12L14 trick: it cuts beautifully on a lathe because lead lubricates the chip; never use it in food contact or weld it. For knife-maker steels add: 52100 (bearing steel, fine grain), 80CrV2 (similar to 1080 + Cr), 1080/1084 (simple high-carbon).
Engineering Plastics Selection GuideTAB. 2I-3
PlasticModulus (GPa)Yield (MPa)Max Service TempUse
Polypropylene (PP)1.535100°CLiving hinges, chemical-resistant containers, automotive trim
Polyethylene HDPE1.025120°CCutting boards, chemical tanks, gears, low-friction slides
UHMW polyethylene0.72290°CConveyor wear strips, low-friction guides, marine slides
ABS2.34085°CConsumer enclosures, LEGO; tough, paintable
Acetal / Delrin (POM)2.86590°CGears, bushings, precision parts; rigid, low friction
Nylon (PA6)2.445100°CGears, bearings, structural; absorbs moisture, dimensional drift
Nylon (PA66)2.855120°CHigher strength than PA6; automotive gears, brackets
Polycarbonate (PC)2.465115°CImpact-resistant lenses, safety glass, eyewear; clear
Acrylic (PMMA)3.37075°CLaser-cut signs, displays, optical-grade clear
PTFE / Teflon0.415260°CSeals, chemical tubing, low-friction bushings; very expensive
PEEK3.6100260°CAerospace, medical implants, high-temp gears; very expensive
PEI / Ultem3.085170°CAerospace, flame-retardant electronics; cheaper than PEEK
Phenolic / G-10 / FR-422240130°CElectrical insulation, structural composites; PCB substrate
Engineering plastics fall into three tiers by cost and performance: commodity (PP, PE, ABS, PMMA) at $2-5/lb; engineering (POM/Delrin, Nylon, PC, PETG) at $5-15/lb; high-performance (PEEK, PEI, PTFE) at $30-100+/lb. For machined parts, Delrin is the workhorse: rigid, dimensionally stable, easy to cut and tap. For sliding wear, UHMW or Nylon for cost or Delrin/PEEK for strength. Nylon absorbs moisture (up to 8% by weight), which changes dimensions and softens it; not for precision parts in humid environments. PTFE is the lowest-friction plastic but flows under load (creep); don't use it in compression-loaded fits.
Section 2J Mechanical

Sheet Metal Bending

K-factor lookup, bend allowance and deduction calculator, minimum bend radius reference, and standard hem dimensions. Bend allowance assumes air bending or 90-degree die forming; press-brake numbers may differ slightly.
See also
For planning blank layouts on stock sheet, use the companion tool Cut List (FI 008) ↗. Bend allowance from this section determines blank size; Cut List packs the blanks onto your stock sheet with minimum waste.
Bend Allowance CalculatorTOOL 2J-1
Effective K-factor—
Neutral axis offset—
Bend Allowance (BA)—
Bend Deduction (BD)—
Flat Length (L1 + L2 − BD)—
BA = (π/180) × A × (R + K·t). BD = 2(R+t) × tan(A/2) − BA. K-factor is the ratio of neutral-axis location to thickness; tighter bends and stiffer materials push K higher.
Minimum Bend Radius (multiples of thickness)TAB. 2J-1
MaterialAnnealed1/4 Hard1/2 HardFull Hard
Values shown as multiples of material thickness (×t). Bending tighter than these causes cracking on the outer fiber. Bend across the grain when possible; bends parallel to the grain need a larger radius.
Hem and Tab DimensionsTAB. 2J-2
FeatureRecommendedNotes
Closed hem (flat)4×t minimum lengthFolds material back on itself; press to close.
Open hem (teardrop)4×t minimum, ID ≈ tLeaves a small bead inside; less stress.
Tab base width≥ 2×tNarrower tabs distort during forming.
Tab length≤ 5×t unsupportedLonger tabs need bracing or a second weld.
Hole edge clearance≥ 2×t from bend lineCloser holes distort during forming.
Slot for flush mountWidth = bolt OD + 0.5 mmAdjustment slots; longer than the bolt diameter.
Section 2K Mechanical

Geometry & Trig Solver

Right triangles, oblique triangles, arc and chord relationships, and a bolt-circle generator that lays out X / Y coordinates for any pitch circle.
Right TriangleTOOL 2K-1
Enter any 2 of the 5 values. Leave the others blank.
a—
b—
c (hypotenuse)—
Angle A—
Angle B—
Area—
Oblique Triangle (Law of Sines and Cosines)TOOL 2K-2
Enter any 3 of the 6 values, including at least one side. SSA cases may have two valid solutions; the smaller-angle solution is shown.
a—
b—
c—
Angle A—
Angle B—
Angle C—
Area (Heron)—
Bolt Circle LayoutTOOL 2K-3
Arc, Chord, SagittaTOOL 2K-4
Enter any 2 of (radius, chord, sagitta, arc angle).
Radius R—
Chord c—
Sagitta h—
Angle θ—
Arc length s—
Section 2L Mechanical

Helical Compression Spring Design

Spring rate k from wire and coil geometry, peak stress using the Wahl correction factor, and material allowable-stress lookup. Enter what you know; the rest is solved.
Spring CalculatorTOOL 2L-1
Spring Index C = D/d—
Wahl Factor K_w—
Spring Rate k—
Deflection under F—
Stress under F (τ)—
Allowable Stress (τ_allow)—
Solid Length L_s—
Total Coils N_t—
—
Spring rate: k = G·d⁴ / (8·D³·N_a). Stress at force F: τ = K_w · (8·F·D) / (π·d³). Wahl factor: K_w = (4C−1)/(4C−4) + 0.615/C. G = 79.3 GPa for steel, 41 GPa for phosphor bronze. Spring index C should land between 4 and 12 for good manufacturability and stability.
Wire Material ReferenceTAB. 2L-1
MaterialUTS (small d)τ_allow fractionG modulusTypical use
Music wire (A228)~2000 MPa0.45 σ_u79.3 GPaHighest quality; precision springs.
Hard-drawn (A227)~1700 MPa0.45 σ_u79.3 GPaLower-cost general purpose.
Chrome-vanadium (A232)~1900 MPa0.45 σ_u79.3 GPaHigher fatigue resistance; valve springs.
Stainless 302 (A313)~1850 MPa0.35 σ_u69 GPaCorrosion resistance.
Phosphor bronze~800 MPa0.40 σ_u41 GPaElectrical contacts; mildly corrosive environments.
Section 2M Mechanical

Wood Reference

Dimensional lumber and sheet stock sizes (nominal vs actual), pilot-hole sizing by species and screw gauge, Janka hardness, shrinkage values, and species notes for furniture and cabinet work.
Pilot Hole SelectorTOOL 2M-1
Pilot drill (thread engagement)—
Clearance drill (free pass)—
Countersink ⌀ for flat head—
Pilot the entire screw length in hardwoods to prevent splitting. In softwood, pilot only the thread length and use a clearance hole for the shank.
Softwood Lumber DimensionsTAB. 2M-1
NominalActual (in)Actual (mm)
Softwood is sold by nominal dimension; actual is smaller after planing and drying. Standard since 1964; older lumber may be closer to nominal.
Hardwood Lumber Thickness (Quarters)TAB. 2M-2
QuarterRough (in)S2S (in)S2S (mm)
Hardwood is sold rough by the quarter-inch. S2S = surfaced two sides; loses about 1/16" per face.
Plywood ThicknessTAB. 2M-3
NominalSoftwood plywoodHardwood / Baltic BirchNotes
1/4"0.21" (5.2 mm)6 mm (Baltic), 5.2 mm (HW)Cabinet backs, drawer bottoms.
3/8"0.34" (8.7 mm)9 mm (Baltic)Occasional back panels.
1/2"0.46" (11.6 mm)12 mm (Baltic), 11.6 mm (HW)Light cabinetry, drawer sides.
5/8"0.59" (15.1 mm)15 mm (Baltic)Sub-floor.
3/4"0.71" (18.3 mm)18 mm (Baltic), 18.3 mm (HW)Cabinet boxes, shelves, jigs.
Baltic Birch is sold metric and is true to thickness within a fraction of a mm. Hardwood ply varies more by mill.
Species: Janka Hardness and MovementTAB. 2M-4
Janka hardness is the load needed to push an 11.28 mm steel ball halfway into the wood (lbf). Shrinkage values are radial / tangential / volumetric percentages from green to oven-dry. Tangential shrinkage exceeds radial in nearly every species, which drives cupping in flat-sawn boards.
Section 2N Mechanical

Hardness and Surface Finish

Hardness conversion between Rockwell C / B, Vickers, Brinell, and equivalent tensile strength. Surface roughness (Ra) reference for typical fabrication processes. Blacksmith heat-color reference (forging temperature by eye) and tempering oxide-color reference (heat-treat color by eye on bright steel).
Hardness ConverterTOOL 2N-1
Conversions are empirical and approximate; accurate to within ±2 points across the practical range for steel. Soft non-ferrous metals don't follow these curves — use direct measurement.
Rockwell C (HRC)—
Rockwell B (HRB)—
Vickers (HV)—
Brinell (HB)—
Tensile (approx.)—
Common Steel Heat Treatments and HardnessTAB. 2N-1
StateHRCHRBNotes
1018 (cold-drawn)—71Soft; machinable, weldable.
4140 annealed—92Machinable; baseline before heat treatment.
4140 normalized20-2297After heating to about 870°C and air cool.
4140 quench & temper28-40—Common shaft and gear condition.
A2 tool steel (HT)58-62—Air-hardening; punches, dies.
D2 tool steel (HT)58-62—High-Cr; long-wear tooling.
O1 tool steel (HT)58-64—Oil-quench; precision tooling.
M2 HSS (HT)62-66—Cutting tools.
52100 bearing steel60-66—Ball and roller bearings.
304 stainless (annealed)—80Work-hardens with cold forming.
17-4PH H90040-45—Precipitation-hardened stainless.
Surface Roughness (Ra) by ProcessTAB. 2N-2
ProcessTypical Ra (µm)Typical Ra (µin)Where used
Sand cast12.5 - 25500 - 1000As-cast surface; not for mating.
Hot rolled6.3 - 12.5250 - 500Structural steel mill finish.
Forged3.2 - 12.5125 - 500As-forged; usually machined after.
Saw cut6.3 - 12.5250 - 500Bandsaw, cold saw.
Drilled hole3.2 - 6.3125 - 250Standard twist drill in steel.
End milled, rough3.2 - 6.3125 - 250Heavy roughing pass.
End milled, finish0.8 - 3.232 - 125Light finishing pass, sharp tool.
Turned, rough1.6 - 6.363 - 250Typical lathe roughing.
Turned, finish0.4 - 1.616 - 63Finish pass with sharp tool.
Reamed0.8 - 3.232 - 125Final hole finish for fit.
Grinding, rough0.4 - 1.616 - 63Cylindrical or surface grinding.
Grinding, finish0.1 - 0.44 - 16Fine grinding; bearing surfaces.
Honing0.05 - 0.42 - 16Bores, hydraulic cylinders.
Lapping0.025 - 0.41 - 16Gauge blocks, optics.
Polished0.01 - 0.10.4 - 4Optical, mirror finish.
Ra = arithmetic average roughness. Convert: 1 µm = 39.4 µin. For matched surfaces, finish requirements step down with precision: bearing journals at 0.2-0.4 µm, sliding fits at 0.8-1.6, general machined at 1.6-6.3.
Forging Heat ColorsTAB. 2N-3
Color (Steel)Temperature (°C)Temperature (°F)Working Range
Black heat (no glow)400752Too cold to work; brittle range for many steels
Faint red (visible in dark)480-530895-985Just visible glow; still too cold to forge
Blood red560-6001040-1110Below forging temperature for steel
Dark red630-6601165-1220Approaching critical for some carbon steels
Cherry red680-7201255-1330Hardening range for some tool steels (A2, O1)
Bright cherry red740-7701365-1420Hardening range for high-carbon (1095, W1)
Dark orange780-8101435-1490Low-temp forging range; tough steels
Orange830-8601525-1580General forging temperature for mild steel
Light orange / orange-yellow880-9201615-1690Easy forging range; most carbon and tool steels
Yellow950-10001740-1830Easy work; high carbon at upper end of safe range
Light yellow1050-11001920-2010Forge-welding range; risk of burning carbon steel
White (welding heat)1150-13002100-2370Forge-welding wrought iron and low-carbon steels
Sparking white1400+2550+Burning: carbon spitting as sparks; steel ruined
Read these in even, low light; sunlight makes everything look colder than it is and most forge work in a sunlit shop runs hot. Color shifts roughly 100°C between dimly-lit and brightly-lit readings. Critical temperatures vary by alloy: simple carbon steels (1075, 1095, W1) have Ac1 around 720-740°C and harden from bright cherry; tool steels (A2, D2, O1) harden from a darker color (cherry red, 760-820°C); stainless and high-alloy tool steels are bright yellow and above. Always verify against the steel's data sheet rather than color alone.
Tempering Colors (Oxide Colors on Bright Steel)TAB. 2N-4
Oxide ColorTemperature (°C)Temperature (°F)Typical Use
Faint pale straw200390Scrapers, razors, scribes (hard, brittle)
Pale straw220430Engraving tools, light scribes
Light straw230445Drills, lathe tools for hard materials
Dark straw240465Reamers, taps, end mills
Brown / dark yellow250480Knife blades (kitchen, EDC); razors over-tempered
Brown-purple260500Axes, chisels, woodworking edge tools
Purple270520Cold chisels, punches, screwdrivers
Dark purple / violet280535Hammers, springs (lower end), masonry chisels
Blue290555Springs, saws, screwdrivers; "spring blue"
Light blue310590Hand saws, wood saws, springs (upper end)
Grey-blue320-340610-645End of useful tempering range; structural
These colors appear on bright (sanded, polished) carbon-steel surfaces as a thin oxide layer forms during tempering. Sand the steel to a clean finish after quench, then heat slowly and watch the colors progress from straw at the heat source outward. For knives and edge tools, the color you want is at the cutting edge, not at the spine or tang. Stainless and high-alloy steels do not show tempering colors reliably; use an oven and a thermocouple. Always temper twice (or three times for some tool steels) at the target temperature, with full cool between cycles.
Surface Finish Symbols (Drawing Notation)TAB. 2N-5
SymbolMeaningNotes
∨Surface roughness specified (basic)Plain triangle; specify Ra value above
∇Material removal requiredTriangle with horizontal bar; machining mandatory
⌀Material removal prohibitedTriangle with circle; surface must remain as-cast/forged
Ra value aboveMaximum arithmetic average roughnesse.g., "3.2" means Ra ≤ 3.2 µm
Ra range (above)Min and max Rae.g., "0.8 / 3.2" means 0.8 ≤ Ra ≤ 3.2
"All over" circle in symbolApplies to all surfaces of the partSaves repeating on each face; ASME Y14.36 standard
Production process letterMandatory process"=" parallel, "⊥" perpendicular, "X" crossed, "M" multidirectional
Lay Direction SymbolMeaning
=Lay parallel to the edge of the surface
⊥Lay perpendicular to the edge of the surface
XLay crossed in two directions
MLay is multi-directional (random)
CCircular (concentric) lay around the center
RRadial lay relative to the center
PParticulate / non-directional
Surface finish symbols (ASME Y14.36 / ISO 1302) extend the basic triangle with additional fields: the Ra value above, the lay direction below, the production method along the top-right leg, and sampling/cutoff length parameters below the lay symbol. The position of values around the triangle is significant; mis-placed values are ambiguous. For most general-purpose mechanical drawings, "Ra 1.6" or "Ra 3.2" with no other specification is enough. Reserve fancier callouts for sealing surfaces, bearing journals, and optical surfaces.
Section 2O Mechanical

Pulleys, Belts, and Gears

Timing belt selection, pulley geometry, belt length calculation, and gear-ratio calculations for motion control.
Belt Length CalculatorTOOL 2O-1
Pitch Diameter (drive)—
Pitch Diameter (driven)—
Speed Ratio (drive : driven)—
Belt Length L—
Belt Teeth (approx.)—
Wrap angle (drive)—
Belt length: L = 2C + (π/2)(PD1+PD2) + (PD1−PD2)²/(4C). Round up to the next available standard belt length; take up slack at the tensioner or with a slotted motor mount.
Gear Ratio CalculatorTOOL 2O-2
Gear Ratio (output / input)—
Pinion PD—
Gear PD—
Center Distance C—
Output Speed—
Output Torque (ideal)—
Metric: PD = N × m. Imperial: PD = N / DP. Center distance C = (N1+N2)·m / 2 for metric, (N1+N2) / (2·DP) for imperial. Output torque = input × ratio (ideal; real gears lose about 2-5% per mesh).
Timing Belt PitchesTAB. 2O-1
DesignationPitchUse
GT2 / 2GT2 mm3D printers, light positioning. Common 6-9 mm widths.
GT3 / 3M3 mmHigher torque than GT2; still hobby and positioning.
GT5 / 5M / HTD 5M5 mmCNC routers, robotics; widely available.
HTD 8M8 mmHigher-torque industrial drives.
HTD 14M14 mmVery high torque; large machinery.
MXL2.032 mmVintage / inch-pitch small belts.
XL5.08 mmOffice machines, light power transmission.
L9.525 mmGeneral industrial.
H12.7 mmHeavier industrial.
GT-series (curvilinear tooth) has higher load capacity than older trapezoidal types (MXL, XL, L, H). HTD profile is similar to GT but slightly older; widely interchangeable with same-pitch GT pulleys.
Roller Chain & Sprocket ReferenceTAB. 2O-3
ANSI SizePitch (mm)Pitch (in)Working Load (kN)Typical Use
#256.351/4~0.4Small office machines, sewing
#359.5253/8~0.9Conveyors, small power equipment
#4012.71/2~1.4Mid-power conveyors, agricultural
#5015.8755/8~2.2Industrial conveyors, light machinery
#6019.053/4~3.2Industrial drives, motorcycles
#8025.41~5.5Heavy industrial, mining, ag
#10031.751-1/4~8.4Large industrial, heavy machinery
#12038.11-1/2~12.1Industrial cranes, heavy lifting
428H (motorcycle)12.71/2~22 (heavy-duty)Motorcycles, ATVs; "H" denotes heavy
520 / 525 / 53015.8755/828-34Mid to large motorcycles
Bicycle (1/2 × 11/128)12.71/2~9Single-speed bicycle
Bicycle (3/32" derailleur)12.71/2~7-9Multi-speed bicycle (7-12 speed)
Chain length (in pitches) = 2C + (T1 + T2)/2 + (T1 − T2)² / (4π² × C), where C is center distance in pitches and T1, T2 are tooth counts. Always round up to an even number of pitches; odd-pitch chains need an offset link, which is the weakest link. Sprocket center distance should be 30-50 pitches for smooth operation, never less than 20 (high tension and wear) or more than 80 (sag and whip). Use chain guides for long runs. Lubricate; chain efficiency drops from 97% wet to 85% dry.
Shaft Coupling SelectionTAB. 2O-4
Coupling TypeMisalignment ToleranceTorque CapacityUse
Rigid (sleeve, clamp)None; both shafts must be coaxialHighPrecision shafts on machined frames; CNC ballscrews
Jaw (spider, Lovejoy)Small (≤ 1° angular, 0.25 mm radial)Medium-highPumps, compressors, general industrial
Beam (helical-cut)Small (≤ 1.5° angular)Low-mediumEncoders, stepper-to-leadscrew on CNC, light loads
BellowsSmall (≤ 1.5° angular, low radial)MediumServo motors to precision screws; zero backlash
Disc (single or double)Small angular; near-zero backlashHighHigh-speed machinery, turbines
Oldham (cross-disc)Significant radial offset; no angularMediumWhere shaft centers can't be aligned
Gear (toothed)Small angular, small axialVery highIndustrial, mining; needs lubrication
U-joint (universal)Up to 30° angularHighDrive shafts; constant velocity needs paired joints
CV jointUp to 50° angular, constant speedHighAutomotive front-wheel drive, robotics
MagneticNone mechanical (across barrier)Low-mediumVacuum chambers, sealed pumps, magnetic stirrers
Rigid couplings require alignment tolerance better than 0.05 mm or they will transmit vibration and load the shaft bearings. Beam and bellows couplings tolerate small misalignment by flexing; this absorbs vibration but limits torque. Jaw couplings (Lovejoy / spider) are the workhorse for general industrial work: simple, replaceable elastomer spider absorbs shock, easy to disassemble. For precision applications (encoders, servo to leadscrew), bellows or beam couplings give zero backlash with some torque capacity. Magnetic couplings transmit torque through a wall; the strongest commercial units handle a few N·m and are common in chemistry rotary stages.
Section 2P Mechanical

Welding Reference

Electrode and filler-wire selection for SMAW (stick), GMAW (MIG), FCAW (flux-core), and GTAW (TIG). Gas-mix reference and a heat-input calculator.
Heat Input CalculatorTOOL 2P-1
Heat Input (kJ/mm)—
Heat Input (kJ/in)—
Arc Efficiency (η)—
Net Heat (η × Q)—
—
Q (J/mm) = (V × I × 60) / travel speed (mm/min). Divide by 1000 for kJ/mm. Typical ranges: GTAW 0.2-1.0, SMAW 0.5-2.0, GMAW 0.4-1.5, FCAW 0.6-2.0 kJ/mm. Too much heat input coarsens the grain and warps the work; too little leaves cold lap or porosity.
SMAW Electrodes (Stick)TAB. 2P-1
GMAW Wires (MIG)TAB. 2P-2
GTAW Filler (TIG)TAB. 2P-3
Shielding Gas MixesTAB. 2P-4
GasProcessUse
75/25 Ar/CO₂ (C25)GMAWStandard for mild steel; balanced penetration and spatter.
90/10 Ar/CO₂GMAWLess spatter; sheet metal and finish welds.
100% CO₂GMAW, FCAWDeepest penetration on mild steel; more spatter.
98/2 Ar/O₂GMAWStainless steel; small oxygen for arc stability.
Tri-mix (90 He / 7.5 Ar / 2.5 CO₂)GMAWStainless steel; better profile than 98/2.
100% ArGTAW, GMAW (alum.)TIG anything; MIG aluminum.
75/25 Ar/HeGTAW, GMAW (alum.)Aluminum thicker than 6 mm; helium adds heat.
100% HeGMAW (thick alum.)Very thick aluminum; runs hot.
Welding Symbol Reference (ANSI / AWS A2.4)FIG. 2P-1
A welding symbol has a horizontal reference line and an arrow pointing to the joint. Symbols below the line apply to the arrow side of the joint; symbols above the line apply to the other side. A tail at the end carries optional process notes ("GMAW", "MIG ER70S-6", etc.). A circle at the arrow junction means "weld all around". A flag means "weld in the field" (not in shop). Numbers before the symbol = leg or throat dimension; numbers after = pitch and length for intermittent welds (e.g., "6-150" = 6 mm leg, 150 mm spacing). The supplementary contour symbols (flush, convex, concave) and finish letters (G = grind, M = machine, C = chip, R = roll, H = hammer) modify the weld face after completion.
Section 2Q Mechanical

Pressure and Pneumatic

Pneumatic cylinder force, thin-wall and thick-wall hoop stress, and reference pressure ratings for tubing and fittings. Useful for actuated fixtures, shop air systems, and small fluid vessels.
Cylinder ForceTOOL 2Q-1
Piston Area (push)—
Annular Area (pull)—
Push Force (extend)—
Pull Force (retract)—
Push Force (lbf)—
Pull Force (lbf)—
F = P × A. Push acts on full piston area; pull acts on the annular area (piston minus rod). Shop air is typically 6-7 bar (90-100 psi). 1 bar = 14.5 psi, and 1 mm² × 1 bar = 0.1 N.
Thin- and Thick-Wall Hoop StressTOOL 2Q-2
r / t ratio—
Hoop Stress (thin-wall)—
Hoop Stress (thick, Lamé)—
Longitudinal Stress—
Safety Factor vs Yield—
—
Thin-wall (r/t ≥ 10): σ_h = P·r / t, σ_long = P·r / (2t). Thick-wall (Lamé): σ_max = P · (r_o² + r_i²) / (r_o² − r_i²). Hoop stress is twice longitudinal — pressure vessels rupture longitudinally. ASME BPVC requires SF ≥ 4 against UTS for boilers; SF ≥ 2 against yield for shop work is a reasonable minimum.
Pneumatic Tubing Pressure RatingsTAB. 2Q-1
Tubing1/8" OD1/4" OD3/8" OD1/2" ODNotes
Polyurethane (PU)200 psi150 psi120 psi100 psiFlexible, common for shop air.
Nylon300 psi200 psi175 psi150 psiStiffer than PU; better at heat.
LDPE (poly)150 psi100 psi85 psi60 psiCheap, light-duty.
HDPE200 psi150 psi125 psi100 psiBetter than LDPE for shop use.
PTFE600 psi400 psi300 psi250 psiHigh temp, chemical resistance.
Ratings at 20°C. De-rate by 50% at 60°C, 75% at 80°C. Burst pressure is typically 3-4× rated working pressure; do not approach burst even briefly.
Common Fitting TypesTAB. 2Q-2
FittingTypical RatingUse
Push-to-connect (PTC)150 psiMost shop pneumatic; quick assembly.
Compression (instrument)3000+ psiSwagelok-style; hydraulic and gas instrumentation.
Barb + clamp100-150 psiLow-pressure shop air, water.
NPT threaded300+ psiTapered pipe thread; requires PTFE tape.
BSPP / BSPT300+ psiBritish pipe thread; common in Europe.
JIC 37° flare3000+ psiAerospace and hydraulic; metal-to-metal seal.
SAE 45° flare1000+ psiRefrigeration, automotive.
O-Ring Sizing (AS568 Standard Dash Numbers)TAB. 2Q-3
Dash NumberID (mm)Cross-Section W (mm)OD (mm)Typical Use
-0041.781.785.34Miniature fluid systems; small dispensers
-0106.071.789.63Small valves; fuel injectors
-01412.421.7815.98Common -1xx range; pumps, valves
-1109.192.6214.43-1xx with thicker cross-section; pneumatic cylinders
-11619.182.6224.42Mid-size cylinders; common shop pneumatic
-21018.643.5325.70-2xx heavier; hydraulic cylinders
-21831.343.5338.40Mid hydraulic; large pneumatic
-32536.175.3346.83-3xx heavy hydraulic; thick cross-section
-33048.905.3359.56Large hydraulic
-425114.306.99128.27Very large; industrial process equipment
ApplicationSqueeze (%)Groove DepthGroove Width
Static, axial (face seal)20-30%0.70 × W1.30 × W
Static, radial (boss seal)15-25%0.75 × W1.30 × W
Dynamic, reciprocating (cylinder)10-15%0.85 × W1.40 × W
Dynamic, rotating5-10%0.90 × W1.40 × W
O-ring material selection: Nitrile (Buna-N) for most fluid power applications (-40 to 110°C, oil-resistant). Viton (FKM) for high-temperature and chemical resistance (-20 to 230°C). EPDM for hot water, steam, brake fluid (do not use with oil). Silicone for dry heat and FDA food contact. PTFE for chemical extremes; PTFE doesn't compress like rubber, so it's used as a "spring-energized" seal with a metal or elastomer behind it. Always size the groove for the application: static seals tolerate high squeeze (good sealing), dynamic seals need low squeeze (low friction).
Fluid Power Schematic SymbolsFIG. 2Q-1
ISO 1219-1 fluid power symbols. Solid triangles point in the direction of flow (filled = hydraulic; open = pneumatic, though common practice uses the same symbol for both). Valves are drawn with a box per "position": a 4/3 valve has 4 ports and 3 positions, drawn as three side-by-side boxes with arrows. Lines into the central box show the neutral (de-energized) state. Pumps and motors use a circle with triangle(s) inside; one triangle = unidirectional, two = bidirectional, additional arrows = variable displacement.
Tubing & Pipe Color CodesTAB. 2Q-4
StandardColorContents
NFPA 99 / ISO 32 (medical gas)GreenOxygen (O₂)
NFPA 99 / ISO 32YellowMedical air
NFPA 99 / ISO 32BrownHelium (He)
NFPA 99 / ISO 32BlackNitrogen (N₂)
NFPA 99 / ISO 32Light blueNitrous oxide (N₂O)
NFPA 99 / ISO 32GreyCarbon dioxide (CO₂)
NFPA 99 / ISO 32WhiteVacuum / suction
CGA (compressed gas cylinders, US)GreenOxygen
CGA (compressed gas cylinders)BrownAcetylene
CGAAluminum / greyArgon
CGAYellow / brownHydrogen
ASME A13.1 (industrial pipes)Yellow (black text)Hazardous fluids (flammable, toxic)
ASME A13.1Green (white text)Water (potable, cooling, boiler feed)
ASME A13.1Blue (white text)Compressed air
ASME A13.1Red (white text)Fire suppression (sprinklers, hydrants)
ASME A13.1Orange (black text)Toxic / corrosive fluids
Refrigerant (color band on can)Light blue (R-134a)HFC-134a refrigerant
RefrigerantPink (R-410A)HFC blend (AC standard)
RefrigerantSilver-grey (R-22)HCFC (legacy AC; phased out)
Always verify against local code — these are the most common standards but vary by region. Medical gas color codes are life-safety-critical; in the US, the NFPA 99 (and matching CGA V-1 cylinder color) standard is mandatory for hospitals. Industrial gas cylinders should never be relied on by color alone; always read the label. The ASME A13.1 color/text combination for pipes is what you'll see in commercial and industrial buildings; the color is the background, the lettering on top is white or black per contrast.
Section 2R Mechanical

Leather Reference

Weight and thickness conversion, saddle-stitch thread length, stitches per inch, needle and thread pairing, tannage characteristics, hide yields, and hardware sizing. Centered on vegetable-tanned and chrome-tanned cowhide work; the same conventions apply to most flat-goods and small-leather work.
Weight ↔ Thickness ConverterTOOL 2R-1
Weight—
Thickness (mm)—
Thickness (in)—
Typical Use—
Convention: 1 oz of leather = 1/64 inch (~0.40 mm) of thickness. Tanneries usually grade in a one-ounce range (5/6 oz, 7/8 oz, etc.) because thickness varies across a hide. Belly is thinner than back; shoulder is between.
Leather Weight ReferenceTAB. 2R-1
WeightThicknessmmTypical Application
1-2 oz1/64-1/32"0.4-0.8Linings, garment, glove leather, bookbinding inlay.
2-3 oz1/32-3/64"0.8-1.2Wallet interiors, light garment, soft pouches.
3-4 oz3/64-1/16"1.2-1.6Watch straps, journal covers, card holders, bag linings.
4-5 oz1/16-5/64"1.6-2.0Bifold wallets (folded), light bag bodies, dog collars.
5-6 oz5/64-3/32"2.0-2.4Bag bodies, light belts, knife sheath bodies, guitar straps.
6-7 oz3/32-7/64"2.4-2.8Standard belts, holsters, sheaths, heavier bag bodies.
7-8 oz7/64-1/8"2.8-3.2Heavy belts, holsters, work-belt construction.
8-10 oz1/8-5/32"3.2-4.0Tooled holsters, knife sheaths, heavy straps, dog harness.
10-12 oz5/32-3/16"4.0-4.8Saddle skirting, armor, structural straps.
12-14 oz3/16-7/32"4.8-5.6Heavy saddle, harness, very stiff structural work.
For folded items (wallets, sheaths), choose a weight that gives the final folded stack the stiffness you want. A two-piece bifold in 4 oz × 4 oz behaves differently than a single 8 oz layer at the same total thickness.
Saddle-Stitch Thread LengthTOOL 2R-2
Quick (×4 rule)—
Detailed Estimate—
Number of Stitches—
Recommended Cut—
Quick rule: cut thread = seam × 4. The detailed estimate accounts for two needles each traveling one stitch length per stitch plus penetrating the leather stack twice, then adds tails and 10% waste. Always round up. Running short mid-seam is worse than wasting a foot of thread.
Pricking Iron Spacing & SPITAB. 2R-2
Spacing (mm)SPI (≈)Spacing (in, ≈)Common Use
2.0012.70.079Very fine; small leather goods, edge stitch on thin work.
2.4510.40.096Fine; wallets, watch straps, card holders. Common French maker spacing.
2.709.40.106Standard fine; wallets, small bags, fine bridle work.
3.008.50.118Medium-fine; bags, briefcases, larger flat goods.
3.387.50.133Medium; bags, holsters, larger items.
3.856.60.151Medium-coarse; belts, holsters, sheaths.
4.505.60.177Coarse; heavy belts, saddle skirting borders.
5.005.10.197Very coarse; heavy saddle and harness work.
European makers list pricking irons in millimeters (Vergez Blanchard, KS Blade, Crimson Hides); American chisels often list teeth per inch. SPI = 25.4 / spacing_mm. Mark with pricking iron, pierce with awl, stitch with two harness needles.
Harness Needle & Thread PairingTAB. 2R-3
Needle SizeEye WidthThread DiameterRitza 25 TigerApplication
000 (heaviest)1.2 mm1.0-1.2 mm1.2 mmHeavy saddle, harness, very coarse work.
001.0 mm0.8-1.0 mm1.0 mmBelts, holsters, heavy bags.
00.9 mm0.6-0.8 mm0.8 mmStandard; bags, briefcases, holsters.
10.8 mm0.5-0.7 mm0.6 mmWallets, watch straps, medium flat goods.
20.7 mm0.4-0.6 mm0.6 mmFine wallet stitching, small leather goods.
30.6 mm0.4 mm0.4 mmVery fine work, decorative stitching.
4 (finest)0.5 mm≤0.4 mm—Specialist fine work.
Sizing is John James convention. The eye must accept the thread without splitting it, but oversize needles tear unnecessarily large holes. For Ritza 25 (Tiger thread): match thread diameter to needle size and the leather will close cleanly around the stitch.
Tannage ReferenceTAB. 2R-4
TannageProcessCharacteristicsTypical Use
VegetableTannins from oak, chestnut, mimosa. Months in pits or weeks in drums.Stiff, body, takes water and dye well, tools, carves, burnishes. Ages to dark patina with use and UV.Belts, holsters, saddles, tooled work, sheaths, structural goods.
ChromeChromium(III) salts. ~1 day in drums.Soft, supple, drapes, water-resistant, colorfast, dimensionally stable. Doesn't tool, doesn't burnish.Garment leather, shoe uppers, upholstery, fashion bags, gloves.
Oil (chamois)Fish or animal oils, fully impregnated.Very soft, supple, water-resistant, weatherproof. Yellow-tan color.Boot leather, work gloves, traditional outdoor and military.
CombinationChrome-tanned then re-tanned with veg.Hybrid: more body and tooling response than chrome, more supple than veg. Edges burnish moderately.Bags, belts, modern flat goods (Buttero, Pueblo are well-known examples).
Bark (pit-tanned veg)Slowest veg tannage; 6-18 months.Densest body, finest grain, premium price. Often single-source bark (oak).High-end saddles, bridles, museum-grade restoration.
Brain-tannedTraditional method; brain emulsion, smoke-finished.Very soft, breathable, smoke-cured for water resistance. Tan to brown color.Traditional buckskin, moccasins, regalia.
Veg-tan smells of bark and is firm; chrome-tan smells faintly chemical and bends like fabric. Edge behavior is the quickest tell: veg burnishes glassy with water and friction; chrome refuses to burnish and must be edge-painted.
Hide YieldsTAB. 2R-5
HideSide (sq ft)Full (sq ft)Notes
Cowhide18-3040-55The standard reference. Sides and double-shoulders are most commonly stocked.
Bull hide25-3550-65Thicker and larger than steer. Heavy structural work.
Calfskin—6-15Fine grain, premium small-goods leather. Sold whole.
Kid / Goatskin—5-9Strong for weight, distinctive pebble grain. Sold whole.
Deerskin—7-12Very stretchy, soft. Gloves, moccasins, garment.
Pigskin—8-12Three-dot pore pattern. Linings, gloves, suede.
Kangaroo—5-9Highest strength-to-weight of common leathers. Boots, whips, premium straps.
Lambskin—5-9Very soft, garment grade. Light gloves, jackets.
Horsehide / Shell Cordovan12-1825-35Shell is the dense fiber layer of the rump, ~3-5 sq ft per side. Premium wallet and shoe material.
Allow 15-25% waste for layout once you account for belly thinning, brands, scars, and grain direction. Cutting plans that align grain with stress (along the spine, along strap length) reduce stretch and improve longevity.
Hardware & Rivets Quick ReferenceTAB. 2R-6
HardwareSizesLeather RangeNotes
Double-cap rivets, small6-7 mm post2-4 ozLight wallets, small flat goods. Stack height 2-3 mm.
Double-cap rivets, medium8-9 mm post4-7 ozBags, belts, holsters. Most-used size.
Double-cap rivets, large10-12 mm post7-10 ozHeavy belts, structural bag joins.
Copper rivets & burrs #99/16" post8-12 ozTraditional heavy work. Set with burr, peen and trim.
Copper rivets & burrs #121" post12+ ozSaddle and harness. Largest common copper rivet.
Chicago screws1/8-3/8" post4-12 ozRemovable; bridle/strap-end fasteners, replaceable belt buckles.
Line 20 snap12.5 mm cap3-6 ozWallets, bag closures, light strap.
Line 24 snap15 mm cap4-8 ozStandard bag and strap snap.
Eyelet #0-#51/8 - 1/2" ID2-8 ozLace holes, drawstrings. Set with anvil and concave punch.
Sam Browne stud10 mm head6-10 ozSlotted closure on belts, holster retention.
Post length should equal the stack thickness plus 1.5-2.5 mm for the cap to seat. Too short pulls through; too long buckles sideways. Solid brass and copper resist corrosion; nickel plate is the budget standard.
Edge Finishing MethodsTAB. 2R-7
MethodLeatherProcessLook
Burnishing (water)Veg-tan onlyBevel, sand to 400, dampen, slick with wood/canvas/bone folder.Smooth, slightly glossy, natural color.
Burnishing (gum trag)Veg-tanAs above, with gum tragacanth as a slip.Higher polish than water alone.
Saddle soap burnishVeg-tanApply saddle soap, slick with canvas. Old-school technique.Soft polish, traditional saddle look.
Beeswax finishVeg-tanBurnish first, then rub beeswax in and re-slick.Sealed, slightly waxy hand.
Edge paint (acrylic)Chrome, combo, vegBevel, sand, paint in 2-4 coats with sanding between, heat-set.Hard glass-like edge, any color. Fenice, Giardini, Vernis 911.
Edge paint (slicked)Combo, vegPaint, then dry-slick with hot iron edge-creaser.Rounded glossy edge.
Edge progression: skive any liner overhang, bevel both faces (#1 for small goods, #2 for belts), sand 220 → 320 → 400 → 600, then finish. Burnishing only works on leathers with collagen still bound by tannins (veg-tan); chrome leather is fundamentally smooth-edged and must be coated.
Section 2S Mechanical

Ceramics Reference

Pyrometric cone temperatures, shrinkage math, clay body characteristics, firing schedules, glaze chemistry basics, coloring oxides, atmosphere effects, and clay-state workability. Centered on wheel-thrown and hand-built work in earthenware, stoneware, and porcelain bodies.
Cone ↔ Temperature ConverterTOOL 2S-1
Cone—
Temperature (°C)—
Temperature (°F)—
Firing Category—
Cones measure heatwork (time × temperature), not just temperature, so the same cone tips at a lower temperature when fired slower. Values are Orton self-supporting cone end-points; large and small cones differ slightly. Use cone numbers as the firing target, not raw thermocouple readings.
Pyrometric Cone ReferenceTAB. 2S-1
ConeSlow °CSlow °FFast °CFast °FTypical Use
Slow ramp: 60°C/hr (108°F/hr) over final 200°C. Fast ramp: 150°C/hr (270°F/hr). Numbers with a leading zero get colder as the digit increases (022 is coldest); numbers without zero get hotter (cone 14 is hottest in this set). Reference: Orton Ceramic Foundation self-supporting cone chart.
Shrinkage CalculatorTOOL 2S-2
Wet Clay Size—
Fired Size—
Shrinkage—
Volume Shrinkage—
