Hyperfocal: H = f² / (N × c), where N is aperture and c is circle of confusion. Focus at hyperfocal and everything from H/2 to infinity is acceptably sharp. For product shots of small parts, focus stack instead — DoF at f/16 macro is still only a few millimeters deep.
Color Temperature ReferenceTAB. 4E-1
SourceKelvinNotes
Candle, oil lamp1800-1900Very warm; orange-red cast.
Incandescent (60W)2700Tungsten warm white; common home lighting.
Tungsten / halogen3200Studio tungsten standard; warm white.
"Warm white" LED2700-3000Common indoor LED.
Sunrise / sunset golden hour3000-4000Warm, low-angle sun.
Cool white fluorescent4000-4500Office lighting; can have green spike.
Direct sun (noon)5200-5500Daylight balance reference.
Flash, electronic5500-6000Balanced for daylight film/digital.
Overcast sky6500-7500Diffused daylight; blue cast.
Open shade7500-8500Sky as light source only.
North blue sky9000-12000Coldest natural light.
Setting custom white balance from a gray card eliminates surprises. Mixing color temperatures in one scene (window light + tungsten lamp) usually looks broken; gel the small light to match the big one. For product shots, single-temperature daylight LED panels (5000-5600K) are the cheapest path to consistent color.
Lens Focal Length ReferenceTAB. 4E-2
RangeFull FrameAPS-C eq.Use
Ultra-wide14-24 mm9-16Architecture, environment, shop wide-shots. Distorts perspective.
Wide24-35 mm16-23Tight interiors, group shots, environmental portraits.
Normal35-50 mm23-35Documentary, walk-around. 50 mm matches human perspective.
Short tele85-105 mm55-70Portraits, product, flattering perspective compression.
Tele135-200 mm90-135Tight portrait, detail isolation, sports.
Long tele300 mm +200 +Wildlife, distant subjects.
Macro (1:1)90-105 mm typical—Workshop detail, jewelry, engraving, finish texture.
For shop photography: a 50 mm or short macro covers most documentation; 24-70 mm zoom is the workhorse. Avoid wide-angle for finished pieces — barrel distortion bows straight lines. The "reciprocal rule" of thumb: minimum hand-held shutter speed ≈ 1 / focal length × crop factor (so 50 mm on APS-C needs 1/80 sec minimum without stabilization).
Lighting Setup ReferenceTAB. 4E-3
SetupLightsUse
Single key1One large softbox at 45°. Honest, moody.
Two-light (key + fill)2Key plus reflector or weaker side light. Standard product.
Three-point3Key + fill + rim/hair. Standard portrait, video interview.
Clamshell2Light above + reflector below. Beauty, flat-lay, jewelry.
Light tent2-4Diffused all around. Small products, no reflections.
Window + reflector1 (free)North window + white card opposite. Best free studio.
Hard back-light only1Translucent subjects, glass, vapor, smoke.
Distance: as the subject-to-light distance shrinks, the source effectively grows in size relative to the subject, producing softer light. Inverse square: doubling the distance gives 1/4 the light. For documentation, soft + slightly directional reads as honest; flat-on hard light reads as security camera.
Print Resolution & DPI CalculatorTOOL 4E-3
Megapixels—
Print Size (inches)—
Print Size (cm)—
Standard Print Match—
Aspect Ratio—
Print Size = Pixels / DPI. The classic 300 DPI standard comes from offset printing (line screen × 2); modern inkjet printers vary in actual resolved detail but 300 DPI is the safe target for prints viewed in hand. Above 16×20 prints, viewers are usually further away and 240 or 200 DPI is indistinguishable from 300. Billboards typically print at 50-100 DPI because the viewer is across a parking lot. For screen display, "DPI" in image metadata is meaningless; what matters is pixel dimensions matching the display.
Color Space ReferenceTAB. 4E-4
Color SpaceGamut CoverageUse
sRGB~35% visible spectrumWeb, social media, standard monitors; the safe default
Adobe RGB (1998)~50% visible spectrumPrint workflow; wider in green-cyan; pro photo editing
Display P3~45% visible spectrumApple devices, modern displays; ~25% wider than sRGB
DCI-P3~45% visible spectrumCinema projection; similar to Display P3 but with different white point
ProPhoto RGB~90% visible spectrumMaster archival; extends beyond actual displayable colors; for RAW editing only
Rec. 709~35% (similar to sRGB)HDTV / web video standard
Rec. 2020~75% visible spectrum4K / 8K UHD; HDR video; aspirational, few devices fully display it
Use CaseRecommended Workflow
Web, social mediaExport as sRGB; embed profile; saves color-managed and unmanaged browsers
Inkjet / lab printSoft-proof in Adobe RGB or print profile; export with embedded profile
Editing master (RAW)ProPhoto RGB working space; 16-bit; preserve all RAW data for re-export
Web videoRec. 709 (HD) or Rec. 2020 (4K HDR); embed proper transfer function
Apple device deliveryDisplay P3; better gradients on modern iPhones, iPads, Macs
Cinema / DCPDCI-P3 or DCI-XYZ; specific to digital cinema package format
Monitor calibration is separate from color space choice: a monitor calibrated to sRGB shows sRGB content faithfully, but cannot display Adobe RGB content correctly (the wider colors get clipped). For Adobe RGB or P3 work, you need a wide-gamut monitor that covers the target space. Hardware calibration with a colorimeter (X-Rite i1, Datacolor Spyder) is the only way to verify; software calibration is approximate. For print, soft-proofing in Photoshop using the actual paper / printer profile previews the gamut compression before printing; without this you'll see color shifts on the final print.
Light Modifier ReferenceTAB. 4E-5
ModifierLight QualityNotes
Octabox / softbox (small, 24-36")Soft, directionalPortrait main light at 1-2 m; controlled spill
Octabox / softbox (large, 48-72")Very soft, wraps subjectBeauty, fashion; placed close to subject
Strip box (12×36" or larger)Soft, very directionalEdge light for product, fashion; window-shape catchlight
Shoot-through umbrellaSoft, broad spillCheap and effective; floods small studio with soft light
Reflective umbrella (white)Medium soft, containedLess spill than shoot-through; classic for old-school portraits
Reflective umbrella (silver)Slightly harder, more specularPunchier light; good for fashion, beauty
Beauty dish (16-22")Hard center, soft edgesFashion / portrait; defined catchlight with shadow control
Standard reflector (with grid)Hard, controlled spillHair light, accent light; grid controls beam angle
SnootHard, narrow spotEyes, hair, single-feature lighting
Bare bulb / pop-upVery hard, omnidirectionalDocumentary, news; harsh; rarely flattering
Scrim / silk (in front)Diffuses any sourceSun control, hard-light softening; 1/2 to 1 stop loss
Flag / black foamCuts light, adds shadowSubtractive; sometimes the answer is more shadow, not more light
Effective light source size is what matters, not modifier size: a 36" softbox at 3 m from a person looks the same size as a 12" softbox at 1 m, and gives the same softness. Closer + smaller = same soft light as farther + bigger. As distance shrinks, fall-off increases dramatically (inverse square); shadows on the far side of the face become very dark. Hard light shows skin texture and creates strong shadow shapes; soft light is more forgiving and is the default for portrait, beauty, and product. The "right" modifier depends on intent: editorial fashion benefits from harder light; corporate headshots benefit from softer light.
Tripod & Support Load RatingsTAB. 4E-6
Camera ClassTypical Weight (kg)Tripod Rating Needed
Phone / pocket camera0.3-0.53-5 kg (mini tripod ok)
Mirrorless body + small lens0.8-1.55-8 kg
DSLR + zoom lens1.5-2.58-12 kg
Full-frame + 70-200mm f/2.82.5-3.512-15 kg
Full-frame + 400mm or larger4-715-25 kg + gimbal head
Cinema camera + matte box + follow focus5-1015-30 kg, fluid head
Studio camera + monitor + microphone3-615-20 kg
Head TypeBest ForNotes
Ball headPhotography (still); landscape, hikingFast composition; choose 30-45 mm ball for full-frame
Fluid headVideo / cinema; smooth pan and tiltRated by counterbalance and drag steps; pricier
Gimbal headLong telephoto; wildlife; sportsBalances heavy lens around its center of mass
Three-way (pan-tilt)Studio still life; macro; architectureIndependent axes; precise but slow
Geared headArchitecture; tabletop; technicalKnob-driven precision; very slow but very accurate
Quick-release plate standardArca-Swiss (universal pro), Manfrotto RC2 / 200PL (common), Peak Design (small camera)
Rated load ≠ safe working load. Reputable tripod brands (Gitzo, RRS, Manfrotto, Benro, FLM, Sirui) state honest ratings; use 50-60% of the stated max for vibration-free work, especially with long lenses or in wind. For tabletop and macro, the lightest tripod that holds steady is best, since extra mass causes vibration after touching the rig. Center column extension significantly reduces stability; for serious work, the column stays retracted. Carbon fiber legs damp vibration faster than aluminum; aluminum is cheaper and tougher. Spike vs rubber feet: spikes for outdoor (turf, gravel), rubber for indoor (wood, stone).
Section 4F Shop & Studio

Project Videography

Frame rates and resolution, the 180° shutter rule, codec selection, storage requirements, and B-roll shot list categories. Oriented to process documentation, build videos, and short-form social.
Frame Rate & Shutter CalculatorTOOL 4F-1
Shutter Speed (sec)—
Shutter Speed (fraction)—
Frame Duration—
Motion Blur Character—
180° rule: shutter speed = 1 / (2 × fps). Gives natural motion blur ("cinematic"). Halving to 90° (1 / 4·fps) gives sharper, more staccato motion — Saving Private Ryan opening. Doubling to 360° (1 / fps) gives heavy blur, dream / drug effect. For sports and how-to detail, faster shutter (lower angle) reads more clearly.
Storage CalculatorTOOL 4F-2
File Size—
Minutes per Card—
Cards for Duration—
Size (GB) = bitrate (Mbps) × duration (min) × 60 / 8000. Always shoot to faster storage than the bitrate requires — V60, V90, CFexpress for 4K and above. Carry at least 1.5× the expected footage in cards or drives; corruption and bad takes happen.
Resolution ReferenceTAB. 4F-1
NamePixelsAspectNotes
SD / DVD720×480 / 720×5764:3 or 16:9Legacy. Don't shoot here.
HD (720p)1280×72016:9Web compressed; legacy YouTube.
Full HD (1080p)1920×108016:9Still the social and broadcast workhorse.
UHD / 4K3840×216016:9Consumer 4K. 4× pixels of 1080p.
DCI 4K4096×216017:9Cinema 4K standard.
6K6048×4032 typical3:2 to 17:9Reframe room for 4K delivery.
8K UHD7680×432016:9Future-proofing; big files.
Vertical (social)1080×1920 / 2160×38409:16Reels, TikTok, Shorts.
Square (social)1080×1080 / 2160×21601:1Instagram feed.
Anamorphic 2.39:13840×1606 typical2.39:1Cinematic wide; letterboxed delivery.
Codec ReferenceTAB. 4F-2
CodecUseBitrate (1080p / 4K)Notes
H.264 / AVCWeb delivery, social, consumer~10 / ~50 MbpsUniversal compatibility. Lossy.
H.265 / HEVCModern delivery, 4K+~5 / ~25 Mbps~50% smaller than H.264 at same quality.
AV1Streaming (YouTube, Netflix)~4 / ~20 MbpsRoyalty-free; better than HEVC; slower encode.
ProRes 422 LTLight edit codec~100 / ~400 MbpsApple intermediate; lossy but visually transparent.
ProRes 422 (standard)Edit / delivery~150 / ~600 MbpsWorkhorse intermediate.
ProRes 422 HQHigh-quality edit~220 / ~880 MbpsHeavier; for grade-and-go.
ProRes 4444 XQVFX, mastering~500 / ~2000 MbpsWith alpha; very heavy.
DNxHD / DNxHRAvid; alt to ProResvariesCross-platform intermediate.
CinemaDNG / RAWMastering, color2-6 GbpsPer-frame raw sensor data. Huge files, full latitude.
Shoot in the highest-quality format your camera offers if you plan to color-grade; transcode to ProRes / DNx for editing if your computer struggles with H.265. For YouTube delivery, export H.264 or H.265 at 50-80 Mbps 4K and let the platform re-encode.
B-Roll Shot ListTAB. 4F-3
CategoryExamples
EstablishingWide of the shop, exterior of the building, sign, tool wall, work in progress on the bench.
Tool revealHands picking up the tool, light catching the edge, sliding into shot, slow rotation.
Detail / textureMacro of grain, weld pool, blade meeting material, sawdust falling, glue squeeze-out.
ActionStroke through the cut, hammer strike, drill bit boring, plane shaving curling.
HandsClose on grip, fingers measuring, pencil marking, brush sweeping.
Eye-line / faceMaker's focused expression. Even if not on-camera, it sells the moment.
TransitionsWhip pans, in-out of focus, hand pass over lens, match cuts (same shape in / out).
RevealSlow tilt up the finished piece, walk-around 360°, on/off of the dust sheet.
Process / timeTime-lapses of glue-up, finish drying, kiln firing, project taking shape.
EnvironmentLight through a window, sawdust in sunbeam, kettle, radio, cat in the shop.
Shoot more B-roll than you think you need; in the edit, the cuts you have are the cuts you can use. For any process step you'll feature, capture wide, medium, and close coverage of the same moment from slightly different angles — gives the editor cuts.
Greenscreen / Chroma Key ReferenceTAB. 4F-4
ParameterTargetNotes
Background colorPure chroma green (RGB ~0,177,64)Standard chroma green has minimal skin-tone overlap; blue for skin tones with green clothing
Subject distance from screen≥ 2 m (6+ ft)Reduces green spill on subject; allows separate background lighting
Background light evenness±0.5 stop across the screenMeasure with meter or grey card; uneven green = jagged key edges
Background light angle45° from each side, softTwo large softboxes or fluorescent banks; avoid hot spots
Subject lightingIndependent of background; match plateLight subject for the composite scene, not for the screen
Key/fill ratio (subject)2:1 to 4:1Lower ratio = flatter, easier to key; higher = more dimensional
Camera settingsLow ISO, lowest noise, log or flat profileNoise destroys key edges; log gives more grading latitude
Shutter angle / speed180° (typical) for natural motion blurFaster shutter = sharper edges but more strobing artifacts
Wardrobe / propsAvoid green or anything reflectiveEven tiny green reflections on shiny props will key out
Common ProblemCauseFix
Green spill on subjectSubject too close to screen, or background brighter than subject lightingMove subject forward; reduce background light; use spill suppression in keyer
Jagged key edgesUneven screen lighting, low resolution, or compressed codecRe-light screen evenly; shoot ProRes or DNxHR, not h.264 highly compressed
Loss of fine hair detailHard keyer settings or screen too closeUse a soft-edged key with despill; pull a separate detail matte for hair
Edges look "cut out"No edge blur or motion match between subject and plateAdd edge blur and grain matching plate; light-wrap subject to background colors
Color shift in keyed subjectGreen spill or keyer aggressive on green tonesDespill plugin (handles automatically); avoid yellow-greens in subject
For consistent keying, record in a 4:2:2 or 4:4:4 chroma subsampled codec (ProRes 422 HQ, ProRes 4444, DNxHR HQ); 4:2:0 (h.264, most camera default) throws away half the chroma data, which is exactly where the keyer looks. For DIY studios, a 6×9 ft fabric green screen with two softboxes or LED panels handles most one-person work. Color grade the subject to match the plate (look at shadow / midtone / highlight color before keying). The "light wrap" technique pulls some of the background color onto the subject's edges, making compositing seamless.
Section 4G Shop & Studio

Audio Recording

Microphone types and patterns, signal levels and gain staging, sample rate and bit depth, frequency ranges, and basic acoustic treatment. Oriented to voiceover, interview, and shop-process recording for video.
Microphone TypesTAB. 4G-1
TypePowerStrengthsUse
Dynamic (moving coil)noneRugged, loud sources, rejects ambientShop voiceover, podcast, drums, loud guitar amps. SM58, SM7B.
Condenser (large diaphragm)phantom 48VSensitive, detailed, full rangeStudio voice, vocals, acoustic instruments. AT2020, Neumann U87.
Condenser (small diaphragm)phantom 48VDetailed, transient responseAcoustic guitar, cymbals, room mics. KM84, SE7.
Ribbonnone (some active w/ phantom)Smooth, vintage, sensitive to handlingVoiceover (warm), brass, guitar cabs. Royer R-121.
Shotgun (line)phantom or batteryTight forward pattern, rejects sidesField, video on-camera, interview boom. Sennheiser MKH 416.
Lavalierbattery or phantomClose to source, small, hiddenInterview, talking-head video, wearer-mounted.
USB condenserUSB busPlug-and-play, decent qualityPodcast, simple voiceover, no interface needed.
Boundary / PZMphantomMounts to a surface, picks up areaConference, room ambience, sound design.
Pickup Pattern ReferenceTAB. 4G-2
PatternPickupUse
Omnidirectional360° equalRoom ambience, conference, lav (clothing-noise tolerant).
Cardioidfront, rejects rearMost-common voice and instrument mic pattern.
Supercardioidtighter front, small rear lobeLive stage, tighter than cardioid but small rear pickup.
Hypercardioidnarrower front, larger rear lobeLoud stage environments where back rejection is critical.
Shotgun (line)very tight forwardField video boom, sound design from distance.
Figure-8 / bidirectionalfront and back, dead sidesTwo-person interview face-to-face, M/S stereo recording.
Variableswitch on bodyStudio versatility; switch between omni, cardioid, fig-8.
Cardioid is the right answer 80% of the time. Switch to shotgun for noisy shop environments (rejects more), or to dynamic + cardioid for very loud sources. Avoid omni in any environment with noticeable noise floor.
Sample Rate & Bit DepthTAB. 4G-3
FormatSample RateBit DepthUse
CD audio44.1 kHz16-bitMusic delivery standard.
Video standard48 kHz16 or 24-bitAll video deliverables; matches broadcast.
High-res audio96 kHz24-bitMusic production headroom; rarely audible after delivery.
Ultra high-res192 kHz24-bitSound design, time-stretch source, mastering.
32-bit float48 / 96 kHz32-bit floatField recording where levels are unpredictable; effectively no clipping.
For video: record 48 kHz / 24-bit; bit depth gives headroom against clipping. Higher sample rates beyond 96 kHz are largely engineering pride — humans top out at 20 kHz, and the Nyquist limit of 48 kHz handles that with margin. 32-bit float is a real game-changer for run-and-gun: the noise floor is effectively below digital silence, so you can record a quiet sigh and a hammer strike on the same take.
Signal Level ReferenceTAB. 4G-4
LevelVoltageUse
Mic level~1 mV to 100 mVMicrophone output. Needs preamp.
Instrument level (Hi-Z)~100 mVPassive guitar/bass pickup. DI box or hi-Z input.
Line level (consumer)~316 mV (-10 dBV)CD player, prosumer gear, RCA jacks.
Line level (pro)~1.23 V (+4 dBu)Mixers, processors, mastering gear, XLR balanced.
Speaker levelseveral volts to 50+ VAmplifier output. Never feed into a line input.
Phantom power+48 V DCSupplied by mixer / interface to condenser mics.
Match levels at every stage. Plugging an SM58 (mic level) into a line input gives faint, hissy audio. Plugging a synth (line level) into a mic input clips the preamp. If your interface has a "+4 / -10" switch per input, check it. Phantom power is safe to most mics but damages some ribbons — confirm before flipping the switch.
RT60 Reverb EstimatorTOOL 4G-1
RT60 (Sabine)—
Character—
Volume (cu ft)—
Sabine formula: RT60 = 0.161 × V / A, where V is room volume in m³ and A is total absorption in sabins (m² of perfectly absorbent equivalent surface). Most untreated rooms have 0.5-1.5 sec RT60 — voice recordings sound cavernous. Add 6-10 m² sabin of treatment to bring a small workshop room down to a clean voice-over range (0.3-0.5 sec).
Absorption ReferenceTAB. 4G-5
Materialα (500 Hz)Notes
Concrete, smooth0.02Untreated shop floor. Effectively reflective.
Glass window0.10Mostly reflective; treat or curtain.
Painted drywall0.05Untreated wall.
Wood paneling0.10-0.15Slight absorption.
Curtains (medium velour)0.50Hung with airspace behind doubles effectiveness.
Carpet on concrete0.30Standard residential carpet.
2" rigid fiberglass / mineral wool panel0.85-0.95Acoustic treatment standard. 24×48" panel = ~7 sabin.
4" rigid panel0.95-1.0Better low-frequency absorption.
Open bookshelf, full of books0.20-0.40Cheap diffuser and absorber; depends on titles.
Heavy upholstered furniture0.30-0.50Helps; a couch is treatment.
α is the absorption coefficient at 500 Hz (the band most relevant to speech). Total absorption in a room = sum of (area × α) for every surface. For voiceover work, target an RT60 of 0.2-0.4 sec; for music, 0.5-0.8 sec depending on style. Egg crates, foam pyramids, and "studio foam" treat high frequencies but do almost nothing below 500 Hz — they make a bright room muddier, not more controlled.
Audio Cable ReferenceTAB. 4G-6
ConnectorPinoutBalanced?Use
XLR (3-pin)1: shield/ground, 2: signal +, 3: signal −YesMicrophones, balanced line; AES/EBU digital on same connector
TRS 1/4" (Tip-Ring-Sleeve)Tip: signal +, Ring: signal −, Sleeve: groundYes (when wired balanced)Balanced line, headphones (stereo), insert send/return
TS 1/4" (Tip-Sleeve)Tip: signal, Sleeve: groundNoInstrument cable (guitar), unbalanced line
TRRS 3.5mmTip: L, Ring1: R, Ring2: ground, Sleeve: mic (CTIA) OR ground+mic swapped (OMTP)NoSmartphone headsets; CTIA is most common (Apple, Android post-2012)
TRS 3.5mm (mini stereo)Tip: L, Ring: R, Sleeve: groundNo (consumer); Yes when wired balancedHeadphones, line out, camera audio
RCA (phono)Center pin: signal, outer shell: groundNoConsumer line level (-10 dBV); SPDIF digital on coax
Speakon (NL2, NL4, NL8)1+/1− = main pair; 2+/2− = bi-amp or second cab—Speaker cables; locking; replaces 1/4" TS for power amp output
Bantam / TT (Tiny Telephone)Tip: +, Ring: −, Sleeve: groundYesPatch bays in pro studios; smaller than 1/4" TRS
XLR (5-pin)Used for stereo mics, DMX lighting, or AES3-idVariesStereo condenser mics; DMX-512 light control
Balanced vs unbalanced: balanced uses two signal wires carrying opposite polarity (differential). At the receiver, the two are subtracted; common-mode noise picked up along the cable cancels. Unbalanced uses one signal wire and shield as return; any noise on the shield gets added to the signal. Always prefer balanced for runs longer than a few feet, especially in studios with lighting dimmers or computer power supplies nearby. Phantom power (+48V) travels on balanced lines via pins 2 and 3 of XLR; ribbon mics can be damaged by phantom power, so verify before connecting.
Studio Acoustic Treatment ReferenceTAB. 4G-7
TreatmentMaterial / ConstructionEffective RangePlacement
Broadband absorber (2" rigid fiberglass)Owens Corning 703 / Roxul Safe'n'Sound, fabric-wrapped frame250 Hz - 4 kHzFirst reflection points; clouds; rear walls
Thick broadband (4" rigid fiberglass)Same as 2" but doubled125 Hz - 4 kHzSame as 2" but more low-end control
Corner bass trapStacked rigid fiberglass or specialty (Auralex, GIK)40-200 HzCorners (where bass nodes peak); floor-to-ceiling
Diffuser (QRD or skyline)Mathematically designed scattering surface500 Hz - 5 kHzRear wall in control room; scatter instead of absorb
Resonator panel (Helmholtz)Sealed cavity with tuned port or slats50-300 Hz (narrow)Targeting specific room modes; specialty use
Open-cell foam (pyramid, wedge)Polyurethane foam, various profiles500 Hz - 4 kHzQuick high-frequency taming; does NOT help bass
Heavy curtains / drapesVelvet, theatrical-weight fabric, gathered500 Hz - 4 kHzWindows; reflection control; temporary studios
Treatment StageOrder of Operations
1. Corners (bass)Stack thick broadband or dedicated bass traps in every corner including ceiling-wall edges
2. First reflection points2" absorbers at the mirror reflection point of your monitors at the listening position
3. Ceiling cloud4" absorber overhead, between monitors and listening position
4. Rear wall2-4" absorber or diffuser depending on room size (small rooms absorb; larger diffuse)
5. SymmetryMirror-image treatment on both side walls to keep stereo image accurate
6. MeasurementREW (Room EQ Wizard) + UMIK-1 measurement mic; measure before and after
Don't over-treat. A "dead" room sounds claustrophobic and is exhausting to work in. Target RT60 of 0.2-0.4 sec for control rooms, 0.3-0.5 sec for live recording rooms. Foam panels are the most common purchase and the least effective for the spend; rigid fiberglass with fabric covering costs about the same and works 4× better at low frequencies. Corner traps are the single highest-impact investment in any room under about 25 m². Above that, room modes become wide and less problematic, but flutter echo (parallel walls) still needs attention.
Loudness Standards (LUFS)TAB. 4G-8
Platform / StandardIntegrated LUFS TargetTrue Peak LimitNotes
Spotify−14 LUFS−1 dBTPNormalizes to this; mixes louder get turned down
Apple Music (Sound Check)−16 LUFS−1 dBTPDefault normalization target
YouTube−14 LUFS−1 dBTPNormalization since 2015; reduces mastering compression race
Amazon Music−14 LUFS−2 dBTPSimilar to Spotify / YouTube
Tidal−14 LUFS−1 dBTPStreaming standard
SoundCloud−14 LUFS (post-2023)−1 dBTPAdded normalization later than competitors
Broadcast TV (US, ATSC A/85)−24 LUFS−2 dBTPCALM Act compliance
Broadcast TV (EU, EBU R128)−23 LUFS−1 dBTPEuropean broadcast standard
Cinema (theatrical)−27 LUFS (varies)−2 dBTP or per specVery wide dynamic range; specific to mix room calibration
Podcast (general)−16 to −19 LUFS−1 dBTPLooser standard; voice clarity over dynamics
Voiceover (commercial)−16 to −20 LUFS−2 dBTPPer agency spec; mono single speaker
Audiobook (Audible)−18 to −23 LUFS−3 dBTPRMS −18 to −23 dB also specified
LUFS (Loudness Units Full Scale) is a perceptual loudness measure that better matches human hearing than peak or RMS. Integrated LUFS is the average over a track; short-term and momentary LUFS show the variation. Streaming platforms normalize loudness on playback; mastering louder than the target gets turned down (so loud masters don't sound louder than quiet ones on streaming, just more compressed). For music, master to the louder end (−10 to −14 LUFS); platforms will turn you down but dynamics-rich masters benefit from the conservative limiting. For voice / podcast, −16 LUFS with good limiting is the sweet spot. Use a loudness meter (iZotope Insight, Youlean, Waves WLM); peak meters alone are not enough.
Studio Cable Management & Color CodesTAB. 4G-9
Cable CategoryCommon ConventionNotes
Power (AC mains)Black jacket; orange when isolation circuitRun separately from audio; cross at 90° if must
Audio mic (XLR)Black or color-coded by channel (red/right, white/left common)Coiled in over-under, never tight; tagged with channel
Audio line (TRS)Black or grey; sometimes color-coded by signal flowPatch bay cables often distinctively colored (orange, yellow)
SpeakerBlack or labeled "speaker only"; thick conductorNever substitute mic cable; impedance and current mismatch
Network / Cat6Blue (general), yellow (PoE), red (uplink) — common but not standardRun separate from audio when possible
Video (HDMI, SDI)Black usually; some studios color-code HDMI by signal pathLengths matter: HDMI > 25 ft needs active cable or fiber
USB / ThunderboltVaries; label by deviceTag both ends; length impacts power delivery and bandwidth
DMX (lighting)Yellow or color-coded; XLR-5 connectorDo not use audio XLR for DMX runs; impedance differs
PracticeNotes
Color-code by destinationPick a color per output device (camera A red, camera B blue, etc.) and label every cable
Label both endsHeat-shrink or printed labels; survives strain and abrasion better than tape
Length stampingPrint cable length on label; saves time when picking from a box
Over-under coilAlternating direction prevents cable memory and tangles; standard pro practice
Velcro ties, not zip tiesZip ties damage cables and don't allow adjustment
Separate power from signalRun AC and signal cables in different bundles; cross at 90° to minimize hum
Service loop at gearLeave 8-18" slack at each connection; allows movement without strain on connectors
There is no universal color standard for studio cabling; the convention is whatever your studio adopts and labels clearly. The most-used convention is left = white, right = red (matching speaker labeling). For multichannel setups, pick a color for each input and stick to it across the entire signal chain. Test cables on a continuity tester (Behringer CT100, Hosa CTL-300) before installation; intermittent cables in walls and floor boxes are the worst troubleshooting nightmare. For permanent installs, use solid-core stranded cable rated for the application (Belden 1192A for portable, 1813A for installed) and proper strain relief.
Section 4H Shop & Studio

Parts Sourcing

A search launcher for the vendors I reach for most often. Type a query once, click whichever vendor is most likely to stock the part. Search opens on the vendor's site in a new tab. The Almanac itself stays offline and does not log or transmit queries; vendor sites follow their own policies once you arrive. Covers Digi-Key, Mouser, LCSC, Newark, Arrow on the electronics side; McMaster-Carr, Grainger, MSC, Fastenal on the industrial side; SparkFun, Adafruit for hobby breakouts; Amazon as a generalist fallback.
Search QueryTOOL 4H-1
Examples:
Pressing a vendor button below opens that vendor's search in a new tab. Different vendors index parts differently: Digi-Key parses values out of free-text (try "4.7k 0805 1%"), McMaster prefers descriptive phrases ("socket head cap screw 1/4-20 stainless"), LCSC matches generic part numbers tightly. Try the same query at two or three vendors when in doubt.
ElectronicsTAB. 4H-1
Digi-Key and Mouser are the two big US authorized distributors with the deepest catalogs and most reliable lead times. LCSC pairs with JLCPCB for cheap China-direct sourcing and is the budget option for hobby work. Newark and Arrow round out the authorized-distributor set when one of the others is short.
Industrial & MechanicalTAB. 4H-2
McMaster-Carr is the gold standard for same-day shipping on shop hardware, raw stock, and obscure mechanical parts. Grainger and MSC are deeper on cutting tools, industrial supply, and bulk fasteners. Fastenal has local branches across the US for picking up fasteners and rod stock in person.
Hobby & MakerTAB. 4H-3
For dev boards, breakouts, and learn-focused kits. SparkFun and Adafruit both stock items oriented to maker-scale projects with documentation; Adafruit leans more education-and-tutorial, SparkFun more breakout-and-sensor catalog.
Generalist FallbackTAB. 4H-4
When you cannot find it elsewhere, or need it tomorrow. Caveat emptor on counterfeits for branded electronic parts; verify by datasheet against package markings on arrival.
Section 4I Shop & Studio

Optics

A working reference for focal length, sensor size, field of view, and projection math. Useful next to 4E Photography (which holds the depth-of-field calculator and exposure tools) for camera setup, and on its own for projector throw distance, machine vision lens selection, and macro work. Covers full-frame, APS-C, Micro Four Thirds, 1-inch, smartphone, and Raspberry Pi sensors.
Lens & Optics FundamentalsTAB. 4I-1
ConceptFormula / DefinitionNotes
Focal length (f)distance from lens to sensor when focused at infinityDetermines field of view and magnification
F-number (f/N)N = f / aperture diameterf/2.8 is wide (more light); f/16 is narrow (more DOF)
Field of view (FOV)2 × atan(sensor / 2f)Diagonal, horizontal, or vertical depending on which sensor dimension
Crop factor43.27 / sensor diagonal (mm)Relative to 35mm full-frame; smaller sensor = larger crop
Effective focal lengthactual f × crop factor50mm lens on APS-C "looks like" 75-80mm on full-frame
Depth of field (DOF)region of acceptable sharpnessIncreases with smaller aperture, shorter f, farther subject
Hyperfocal distanceH = f² / (N × c)Focus here for max DOF; c = circle of confusion
Magnification (m)image size / object size1:1 macro means subject same size on sensor as in real life
Thin lens equation1/f = 1/do + 1/diRelates focal length to object and image distance
Sensor SizesTAB. 4I-2
SensorDimensions (mm)Diagonal (mm)Crop Factor
Full-frame (135 / 35mm)36 × 2443.271.00×
APS-H (Canon)28.7 × 1934.51.25×
APS-C (Nikon, Sony, Fuji)23.6 × 15.628.31.53×
APS-C (Canon)22.3 × 14.926.81.61×
Micro Four Thirds17.3 × 1321.62.00×
1-inch (Sony RX, P&S)13.2 × 8.815.92.72×
2/3-inch8.8 × 6.611.03.93×
1/1.7-inch (high-end phone)7.6 × 5.79.54.55×
1/2.3-inch (compact / drone)6.17 × 4.557.665.64×
1/3-inch (RPi HQ Camera)6.29 × 4.717.855.51×
1/4-inch (RPi v2 / IMX219)3.68 × 2.764.609.40×
Crop factor lets you compare lens behavior across sensor sizes. A 25mm lens on Micro Four Thirds frames the same scene as a 50mm lens on full-frame. Smaller sensors give more DOF at any given aperture, which is why phones can fake "portrait mode" with software but real bokeh comes from large sensors.
Field of View CalculatorTOOL 4I-1
Horizontal FOV—
Vertical FOV—
Diagonal FOV—
Frame Width @ distance—
Frame Height @ distance—
Effective Focal Length—
FOV = 2 × atan(sensor / 2f). For a 50mm lens on full-frame, horizontal FOV is about 40°. The "frame width at distance" is what you'd actually capture at that subject distance, useful for sizing a backdrop, framing a product shot, or setting up a copy stand.
Projection & MagnificationTOOL 4I-2
Image Distance—
Magnification—
Image Size on Sensor—
Thin lens equation: 1/f = 1/do + 1/di. For everyday photography (objects at meters, focal lengths in millimeters) the image distance is approximately equal to the focal length. For projection or macro work where object distance approaches focal length, the math becomes important.
Common Lens CategoriesTAB. 4I-3
CategoryFocal Range (full-frame eq.)Typical Use
Ultra-wide / fisheye8-20 mmReal estate interiors, landscape, astrophotography, action cams
Wide24-35 mmLandscape, environmental portrait, walk-around, video B-roll
Normal40-58 mmDocumentary, street, "natural" perspective; 50mm is the classic
Short telephoto / portrait70-135 mmPortrait (flattering compression), product, headshots
Telephoto150-300 mmSports, wildlife, tight isolated subjects
Super-telephoto300-800 mm+Distant wildlife, aviation, astrophotography
Macro50-200 mm (1:1 capable)Small object detail, product, scientific work
Tilt-shift17-90 mmArchitecture (perspective correction), product, miniature-look
Focal range is reported in full-frame equivalent. For shop photography of small projects and parts, a 50-100mm prime or short macro on whatever sensor you have hits the sweet spot for working distance and detail.
Section 4J Shop & Studio

Shop Infrastructure

Reference for the systems that make a shop work: dust collection sizing (CFM per tool, ductwork), compressed air requirements, shop lighting (lumens per area, color temperature), basic shop electrical, and tool restoration (rust removal methods). For shop safety see 4D; for fixturing and benches see 4A and 4B.
Dust Collection CFM ReferenceTAB. 4J-1
ToolRequired CFMPort Diameter (in)Notes
Table saw (cabinet)350-4004Most of the CFM is at the dust shroud below blade; over-blade pickup adds 100 CFM
Table saw (contractor)300-4004Open underside is hard to capture; consider shroud upgrade
Jointer (6-8 inch)400-5004Heavy chip output; the most demanding hand-fed tool
Planer (15 inch)500-8005-6Highest chip volume; ribbons not dust; use bigger pipe
Band saw (14 inch)3504Below-table pickup, plus optional over-blade
Router table350-4504 + 2.5Two ports: above-fence and below-table
Drum sander400-6004Very fine dust; demands HEPA filter; high static pressure
Miter saw3504Hood enclosure with dust shroud improves capture significantly
Lathe (wood)300-5004Pickup near tool rest; varies with bowl vs spindle work
Random orbital sander100-1502.5 (or built-in)Shop vac territory; HEPA bag essential for fine dust
Hand-held router100-2002.5Shop vac with router accessory
Drill press100-2002.5Light dust; shroud at table level catches most
Duct Diameter (in)Max CFMMin Velocity RequiredNotes
3 inch~2504000 FPMBranch lines only; too restrictive for main
4 inch~4004000 FPMMost common tool port size; branch line
5 inch~6504000 FPMMain trunk for small shop; branch for large tools
6 inch~9004000 FPMMain trunk for medium shop
7 inch~12004000 FPMTrunk for large shop or multi-tool operation
8 inch~16004000 FPMIndustrial trunk
Minimum velocity 4000 FPM keeps chips moving (lower and they settle in the duct). Static pressure loss adds up fast: each 90° elbow ≈ 8 ft of straight duct, each flex hose foot ≈ 3 ft of rigid duct. A 2HP single-stage dust collector typically delivers ~600-800 CFM at the tool through 20 ft of 4" duct; a cyclone-equipped 3HP gives ~1000-1200 CFM through 6" main + 4" branches. For fine dust (drum sander, planer), add a separator and HEPA secondary filter. Below 5 microns the dust passes through standard bag filters and goes back into shop air.
Compressed Air RequirementsTAB. 4J-2
ToolSCFM @ 90 psiDuty CycleNotes
Brad nailer (18 ga)0.3~10% (intermittent)1 gal pancake compressor handles it
Finish nailer (15-16 ga)0.5~15%Trim work; small portable compressor OK
Framing nailer (round head)2.5~30%Higher demand; needs ≥ 3 gal tank
Pin nailer (23 ga)0.2~5%Minimal demand; portable compressor
Air stapler1-2~20%Upholstery, sheathing
Impact wrench (1/2 in)4-6~50%Requires good-sized compressor (≥ 5 gal)
Impact wrench (3/4 in)7-10~70%Heavy automotive; ≥ 60 gal stationary compressor
Air ratchet (3/8 in)3-5~50%Light assembly
Die grinder4-8~80%Continuous use; large compressor
Random orbital sander (air)8-12~100%Continuous duty; ≥ 80 gal compressor needed
HVLP spray gun9-13~100%Largest demand; dedicate compressor
Plasma cutter4-7ContinuousFilter and dryer essential
Sandblaster (small)7-10~100%Continuous; 60+ gal compressor
Adjust for duty cycle: a 2.5 SCFM nailer at 30% duty = 0.75 average SCFM. Continuous-duty tools (sanders, spray guns) need the full SCFM rating × 1.25 (margin). Pipe sizing: 3/8" line up to 25 ft for one tool, 1/2" for longer runs or multiple tools, 3/4" for main shop drops. Air dries by cooling; long lines with downward slopes and drip legs let condensation drop out before tools. For paint and plasma, a desiccant or refrigerated dryer is mandatory; water in the line means fish-eye finish or wandering plasma arc.
Shop Lighting ReferenceTAB. 4J-3
TaskLumens / sq ftLuxNotes
Storage areas, walkways5-1050-100Safe traffic; not for working
General shop lighting30-50300-500Comfortable for moving around and setup
Bench / hand work50-75500-750Layout, measuring, hand-tool use
Machine tool work75-100750-1000Reading scales, fine adjustment
Inspection / fine assembly100-2001000-2000Soldering, jewelry, watchmaking
Drawing / drafting100-1501000-1500Reading drawings, marking
Color-critical (paint, finishing)75-150750-1500Use 5000K full-spectrum CRI ≥ 90 fixtures
Light SourceLumens / WattColor Temp OptionsCRI
LED shop (4 ft strip)100-1303000K-6500K80-95
LED high-bay120-1504000K-5000K80-90
T8 fluorescent85-1003000K-6500K78-85
T5 fluorescent (HO)95-1053000K-6500K78-90
Halogen15-252900K-3200K100
Incandescent10-172700K100
Color temperature for shop work: 4000-5000K is the sweet spot (matches noon daylight, doesn't make warm wood look gray). For color-critical finishing work, use 5000K full-spectrum (D50) fixtures with CRI ≥ 90 and verify with a color checker; cheap LED panels often have spike-heavy spectra that misrepresent paint colors. For photography studios, mix 5500K (daylight balanced) fixtures or use bicolor LEDs adjustable from 3200K (tungsten) to 5600K. Avoid mixing color temperatures in a single workspace; the eye adapts to the dominant source and judges everything else incorrectly.
Shop Electrical ReferenceTAB. 4J-4
Tool / LoadVoltageTypical Amp DrawRecommended Circuit
Table saw (contractor / hybrid)120 V12-15 A20 A dedicated
Table saw (cabinet, 3-5 HP)240 V12-20 A30 A dedicated
Dust collector (2 HP)240 V10-12 A20 A dedicated
Dust collector (3 HP cyclone)240 V15-20 A30 A dedicated
Air compressor (5 HP)240 V15-20 A30 A dedicated
Welder (MIG 140 A)120 V20 A20 A dedicated (or 240V for higher amp)
Welder (MIG/TIG 250 A)240 V30-50 A50 A dedicated
CNC router / mill (small)120 V8-15 A20 A dedicated
CNC mill (industrial)240 V (3-phase)15-40 APer nameplate; service drop
Lighting + receptacle (general)120 V8-12 A15-20 A general-purpose
Battery charger station120 V3-8 A20 A general-purpose
GFCI protection is required by NEC for shop receptacles within 6 ft of sinks, on exterior walls, and (since 2017) in garages and workshops. AFCI is required for bedroom-like spaces; check local code for shop applicability. Dedicated circuits are necessary for any tool above 50% of breaker rating (a 20A circuit shouldn't run a 12A continuous tool plus anything else). Subpanels are smart for shops over 100 A demand: separate disconnect, clear labeling, ground-bond per code. For 240V receptacles, NEMA 6-20 (20A), 6-30 (30A), 6-50 (50A), and L6-30 (twist-lock 30A) are common; verify the plug type matches the tool before wiring. The NEC permits 240V single-phase to most shop equipment; 3-phase requires a phase converter or a 3-phase service drop.
Tool & Iron Restoration (Rust Removal)TAB. 4J-5
MethodSolutionTimeNotes
Electrolysis1 tbsp washing soda per gallon water + sacrificial steel anode2-24 hoursBest for heavy rust; preserves original surface; needs DC power supply 6-12V at a few amps
Citric acid bath1-2 tbsp per gallon hot water2-24 hoursFood-safe; mild; good for light rust; rinse and dry immediately to prevent flash rust
White vinegar soak5% acetic acid (straight from bottle)4-24 hoursCheap; aggressive; etches steel surface; rinse, neutralize with baking soda, dry, oil
Oxalic acid (wood bleach)1-2 tbsp per quart hot water15-30 minRemoves iron stains from wood (water damage); use gloves and ventilation
Phosphoric acid (Naval Jelly)Commercial gel, brush on5-30 minTargets rust; converts to phosphate coating; rinse thoroughly
Wire wheel / brushMechanical onlyMinutesFast for surface rust; doesn't reach pits; wear face protection
Evaporust (commercial)Proprietary chelating agent2-24 hoursReusable until exhausted; gentle; good for valuable tools
Molasses bath (slow method)1 part molasses to 8-10 parts water2-6 weeksPatient method; gentle bacterial action; smells terrible
Electrolysis is the standard for restoring old hand planes, vises, anvils: clean parts come out with intact surfaces (no etching), and the process is selective for iron oxide. After any acid bath, immediately rinse with water, neutralize with baking soda solution, dry thoroughly (compressed air or oven warm), and apply rust-preventive oil (3-in-1, light machine oil, or paste wax). Flash rust forms within minutes on bare iron in humid shops. For long-term storage, paste wax (Renaissance Wax for museum-quality) or a thin oil film prevents recurrence. Never use chlorine bleach on iron; it accelerates corrosion.
Section 5A Power & Motion

Motor Sizing

A working reference for selecting motors for shop projects and small machinery: DC brushed, BLDC, stepper, servo, and AC induction. Covers torque calculation, NEMA flange dimensions for stepper mounting, lead screw selection for linear motion, gear ratio math, and stepper microstepping. For pulley and belt drive math see 2O Pulleys, Belts, Gears.
Motor Type ComparisonFIG. 5A-1
DC brushed is the simplest and cheapest but the brushes wear. BLDC trades a more complex driver for long life and high efficiency. Steppers give precise position without feedback at the cost of low speed and possible missed steps. Servos add closed-loop feedback for precision under load. AC induction dominates above a few hundred watts where line power is available.
NEMA Flange DimensionsTAB. 5A-1
NEMA SizeFlange (mm)Bolt CircleShaft Ø (mm)Typical Holding TorqueUse
NEMA 820.4 × 20.416.0 mm42-3 N·cmTiny mechanisms, miniature actuators
NEMA 1128 × 2823.0 mm55-10 N·cmSmall camera sliders, lab automation
NEMA 1435 × 3526.0 mm510-20 N·cmLight 3D printers (Delta), small CNC
NEMA 1742.3 × 42.331.0 mm520-65 N·cmWorkhorse: 3D printers, small CNC, robotics
NEMA 2356.4 × 56.447.1 mm6.35 / 8100-300 N·cmMid CNC, lathes, hobby mills
NEMA 2460 × 6050.0 mm10150-400 N·cmMid-large CNC, industrial automation
NEMA 3486 × 8669.6 mm14400-1500 N·cmIndustrial CNC, large gantry systems
NEMA 42110 × 11089.0 mm192000+ N·cmLarge industrial; rarely seen in hobby work
NEMA frame size refers to the square flange dimension in tenths of an inch (NEMA 17 = 1.7 in = 42.3 mm). Mounting holes are at the bolt circle diameter. Shaft diameters are nominal; verify exact dimensions per manufacturer datasheet. Torque ratings vary widely with motor length and current; always check the datasheet for the specific part.
Torque CalculatorTOOL 5A-1
Static Torque—
With Safety Factor—
Required Motor Torque—
In oz·in—
Torque = force × moment arm. For a 5 kg load on a 50 mm arm: F = 49 N, torque = 2.45 N·m. Apply a safety factor (1.5-2× for steady loads, 2-3× for shock loads). Mechanical advantage (pulleys, levers, gears) reduces motor torque proportionally but reduces output speed by the same ratio.
Lead Screw CalculatorTOOL 5A-2
Required Motor RPM—
Required Motor Torque—
Mechanical Power—
Note—
Common lead screws: T8×2 (2mm/rev, slow precise), T8×8 (8mm/rev, faster). Acme threads run 30-40% efficient; ball screws 85-95%. For an 8mm lead at 20 mm/s, motor must spin 150 RPM. Backdrive resistance increases as lead decreases; below about 4mm lead per revolution, leadscrews self-lock under static load.
Gear Ratio CalculatorTOOL 5A-3
Output Speed—
Output Torque—
Input Power—
Output Power—
Ratio > 1 reduces speed and multiplies torque (reduction gear). Ratio = 10:1 means 10 input revolutions per 1 output revolution. Torque scales by ratio × efficiency. Spur gears: 95-98% per stage. Worm gears: 50-90% (high reduction in one stage, often self-locking). Planetary: 90-95% per stage.
Stepper Microstepping ReferenceTAB. 5A-2
MicrostepSteps / Rev (1.8° motor)ResolutionTrade-off
Full step2001.800°Maximum torque, most audible noise
Half step4000.900°~70% holding torque, quieter
1/4 step8000.450°Smoother, less torque ripple
1/8 step16000.225°Standard for 3D printer X/Y
1/16 step32000.1125°Common on Trinamic drivers
1/32 step64000.0563°Very smooth; diminishing returns past this
1/64 - 1/256 step12800-51200<0.03°Trinamic StealthChop; mostly for noise reduction
Standard stepper is 1.8° per full step (200 steps/rev). 0.9° motors exist (400 steps/rev) for finer resolution. Microstepping interpolates between full steps electrically; effective accuracy improvement maxes out around 1/8 to 1/16 because the rotor cogs into discrete detent positions regardless of driver subdivision. Above that, you're getting smoother motion and quieter operation, not more resolution.
Section 5B Power & Motion

Pneumatics & Hydraulics

Fitting standards, thread comparison, hose ratings, air consumption math, and pneumatic vs hydraulic system selection. For cylinder force and hoop stress math see 2Q Pressure & Pneumatic; this section focuses on fittings, flow, and fluid power system design.
Pressure Unit ConversionTOOL 5B-1
psi—
bar—
kPa—
MPa—
atm—
inHg / mmHg—
kgf/cm²—
Shop air systems run 90-120 psi (6-8 bar). Hydraulic systems typically run 1000-3000 psi (70-200 bar). 1 atm = 14.7 psi = 1.013 bar. Gauge pressure (psig, barg) is what the gauge reads; absolute pressure (psia) adds atmospheric. Most shop work uses gauge.
NPT vs BSP Thread ComparisonTAB. 5B-1
StandardRegionThread AngleFormSealNotes
NPTUSA, Canada60°TaperedThread interference + sealantNational Pipe Taper; needs tape or pipe dope
NPTFUSA60°Tapered (dry)Thread interference onlyDesigned to seal without tape; "dryseal"
BSPT (R)UK, EU, Asia55°TaperedThread interference + sealantBritish Standard Pipe Taper; not interchangeable with NPT
BSPP (G)UK, EU, Asia55°ParallelO-ring or bonded washerBritish Standard Pipe Parallel; thread does not seal
JIC 37°USA hydraulics60°Parallel + flareMetal-to-metal flareCommon in hydraulics; reusable, vibration-resistant
ORFS (O-ring Face Seal)USA hydraulics60°ParallelO-ring faceHigh-pressure hydraulics; very reliable
Metric (ISO 9974)Global60°ParallelO-ring or bonded washerEuropean hydraulics; M10×1, M14×1.5, etc.
NPT and BSPT look similar but the thread angles (60° vs 55°) and pitches do not match; mixing them strips threads. The most common mistake: trying to mate a BSP fitting from a European cylinder into a US NPT manifold. Buy NPT-BSP adapters or commit to one standard for the whole system.
Common Pipe & Fitting SizesTAB. 5B-2
Nominal SizeNPT Threads/inOD (in)OD (mm)Typical Use
1/8"270.40510.3Small instrumentation, gauges
1/4"180.54013.7Small shop air, pneumatic cylinders
3/8"180.67517.1Mid shop air, larger cylinders
1/2"140.84021.3Main shop air line, larger hydraulics
3/4"141.05026.7Large shop air drops, water
1"11.51.31533.4Compressor mains, industrial
Air Consumption CalculatorTOOL 5B-2
Cylinder Volume / Stroke—
Free Air per Cycle—
Consumption (SCFM)—
Consumption (L/min)—
Compressor sizing tip—
Free air is the air at atmospheric pressure that you must compress to fill the cylinder. Working pressure × volume ÷ atmospheric pressure = free air consumed per stroke. Size a compressor for at least 1.5× peak demand and add 20-30% for tools that run continuously. A typical 1/4 hp pancake compressor delivers about 1-2 SCFM continuously.
Pneumatic vs Hydraulic SelectionTAB. 5B-3
FactorPneumaticHydraulic
Working pressure6-10 bar (90-150 psi)70-350 bar (1000-5000 psi)
Force at given cylinder sizeLow (compressed air, low P)High (incompressible fluid, high P)
Speed controlFast, but bouncy (compressible)Slow but precise (incompressible)
CleanlinessClean exhaust (air)Risk of oil leaks; environmental concern
Temperature toleranceWide (cold air just feels nice)Limited by fluid viscosity range
CostLow; commodity componentsHigh; precision components
MaintenanceFilter, lubricator, dryer for moistureFluid changes, seal replacement, filtration
Best forLight fast cycling, clean environmentsHeavy force, slow precise positioning
Typical useAssembly fixturing, ejection, light pressMachine tools, lifts, forming presses
Fitting Type ReferenceTAB. 5B-4
FittingConnectionPressure RangeUse
Push-to-connect (push-in)Push tubing in; collet gripsto 10 bar (pneumatic)Festo, SMC; shop air, fast assembly
CompressionFerrule compresses on tubingto 350 barHydraulic, gas, instrumentation
Barb + clampTubing pushed over barb, clampedto 10 barLow-pressure water, vacuum, low-pressure pneumatic
Quick-disconnect (industrial)Push-and-twist couplerto 12 barShop air tools; many incompatible profiles (Industrial, Auto, ARO)
Flare (JIC, SAE)Conical flare, metal-to-metalto 700 barHydraulic systems, brake lines
O-ring face seal (ORFS)O-ring on flat faceto 420 barHigh-pressure hydraulics
Camlock / cam-and-grooveCam levers, gasket sealto 10 barTankers, large hoses, agricultural
Quick-disconnect couplers have multiple incompatible profiles even at the same nominal size; the Industrial (M-style), Automotive (T-style), and ARO profiles all look similar but do not mate. Pick one standard for your whole shop and stick to it.
Section 5C Power & Motion

Bearings

A reference for bearing type selection, common bore sizes (metric and inch), load rating fundamentals (dynamic C, static C0, L10 life), and designation systems. For shaft and bore tolerance classes for press and slip fits, see 2H Bearing & Shaft Fits. For shop hardware sizing in general, see 2F Fastener Torque.
Bearing Type ComparisonFIG. 5C-1
Deep groove ball bearings are the universal default: cheap, handle radial and modest axial load, fit most general-purpose rotation. Tapered roller bearings handle high combined loads and dominate automotive wheel hubs. Needle bearings give very high radial load capacity in a thin envelope. Thrust bearings handle axial-only loads. Plain bushings (sleeve bearings) handle slow rotation and high loads with no rolling elements at all.
Common Bore Sizes (Metric)TAB. 5C-1
DesignationBore (mm)OD (mm)Width (mm)Notes
6055145Small motors, 3D printer idlers
6066176Small CNC, 6mm shaft
6088227The universal hobby bearing: skateboards, 3D printers, fidget spinners
6099247Less common; specialty
600010268Light-duty; small electric motors
620010309Medium-duty; more capacity than 6000 at same bore
6300103511Heavy-duty; same bore, larger envelope
600112288Light-duty next-size-up
60021532915mm shaft; common stepper output
600317351017mm shaft
600420421220mm shaft; small machinery
600525471225mm shaft; medium machinery
600630551330mm shaft
600840681540mm shaft; mid-industrial
Designation system for deep groove ball bearings: the first digit indicates series (6 = single row), the second indicates duty (0 = light, 2 = medium, 3 = heavy), and the last two indicate bore. For 6004: bore = 04 × 5 = 20mm (the rule starts at 04 = 20mm; below that, 00 = 10mm, 01 = 12mm, 02 = 15mm, 03 = 17mm, then each step adds 5mm). Suffix codes: ZZ = double metal shield, 2RS = double rubber seal, C3 = looser internal clearance.
Common Bore Sizes (Inch)TAB. 5C-2
DesignationBore (in)OD (in)Width (in)Notes
R41/4 (.250)5/8 (.625).196R-series: inch dimensions; light duty
R63/8 (.375)7/8 (.875).218Common 3/8" shaft
R81/2 (.500)1-1/8 (1.125).250Very common; 1/2" shafts
R105/8 (.625)1-3/8 (1.375).2815/8" shaft
R123/4 (.750)1-5/8 (1.625).3123/4" shaft
R161 (1.000)2 (2.000).3751" shaft
L10 Bearing Life CalculatorTOOL 5C-1
Load Ratio (C / P)—
L10 Life (million revs)—
L10 Life (hours)—
L10 Life (years @ 8hr/day)—
Assessment—
L10 is the life that 90% of identical bearings will exceed under given load and speed. Formula: L10 = (C/P)^p × 10⁶ revolutions, where p = 3 for ball bearings and 10/3 for roller. C is from the bearing datasheet (dynamic load rating). Doubling the load drops life by a factor of 8 for ball bearings. A C/P ratio above 6 generally gives "infinite" practical life.
Bearing Seal & Suffix CodesTAB. 5C-3
SuffixMeaningUse
Openno shield or sealRequires external sealing; oil-bath applications
Z / ZZsingle / double metal shieldExcludes dust; allows oil mist lubrication
RS / 2RSsingle / double rubber contact sealPre-greased, sealed for life; most hobby use
RZ / 2RZsingle / double rubber non-contact sealBetter dust exclusion than Z, less friction than RS
C3looser internal clearanceFor applications with thermal expansion or interference fit
C2tighter internal clearancePrecision applications, less radial play
P5, P4, P2ABEC tolerance classes (precision)Higher number = looser; ABEC 7+ for precision spindles
For most shop builds, 2RS sealed bearings are the right default: greased for life, dust-proof, and run cool at moderate speeds. ZZ shielded bearings handle higher speeds because the shields don't contact the inner race (so no friction or heat from the seal). For high-RPM applications, open or ZZ with proper external sealing and forced lubrication outperforms 2RS.
Mounting & Fit GuidanceTAB. 5C-4
ConditionInner Race (Shaft)Outer Race (Housing)
Inner rotating, light loadk5, k6 (light interference)H7 (loose)
Inner rotating, normal loadm5, m6 (medium interference)H7
Inner rotating, heavy/shockn6, p6 (heavy interference)K7
Outer rotatingg6, h6 (loose)n6, p6 (interference)
Easy assembly, low loadj6 (transition)J7 (transition)
Rule of thumb: the rotating race gets the tighter fit. For a shaft that rotates in a stationary housing (the common case), the shaft is press-fit and the housing is slip-fit. This prevents the rotating race from spinning on its mating surface, which would gall and destroy both parts. See section 2H for the actual ISO 286 tolerance values for each class.
Section 5D Power & Motion

Mechanism Design

Reference for common motion-converting and motion-controlling mechanisms: four-bar linkages, slider-crank, cam profiles, Geneva drives, ratchets, detents, and latch types. Includes the Grashof condition for linkage classification, cam dwell-rise-fall-return terminology, and selection guidance. For motor sizing see 5A; for shaft couplings and drive trains see 2O Pulleys, Belts, Gears.
Mechanism Selection by Motion TypeTAB. 5D-1
Input → Output MotionMechanismTypical Use
Rotation → rotation (different speed)Gear train, belt drive, chain driveSpeed reduction, torque multiplication
Rotation → rotation (oscillating)Four-bar (crank-rocker)Windshield wipers, oscillating fans, treadle
Rotation → linear (reciprocating)Slider-crank, scotch yokePistons, sewing machine needle, jigsaws
Rotation → linear (continuous)Rack & pinion, lead screw, ball screwCNC axes, sliders, jackscrews
Rotation → intermittent rotationGeneva drive, ratchet, indexing camFilm projectors, indexing tables, watch movements
Continuous rotation → dwell + rise + fallCam & followerEngine valves, automation, packaging machines
Linear → linear (different ratio)Lever, hydraulic / pneumatic cylinder pairForce multiplication, mechanical advantage
Linear → rotationSlider-crank (reversed), rack & pinionEngine crankshafts, steering racks
Motion lock (one-way)Ratchet & pawl, sprag clutch, freewheelSocket wrenches, hoists, bicycles
Latching / hold positionDetent, over-center latch, cam lockDoor hardware, mode switches, toggle clamps
Four-Bar Linkage & Grashof ConditionTAB. 5D-2
Link Length RelationshipGrashof CaseResulting Motion
s + l < p + qGrashof (one link rotates fully)Crank-rocker, double-crank, or rocker-crank depending on which is fixed
s + l = p + qChange-point / parallelogramFolding mechanism; can lock or invert direction
s + l > p + qNon-Grashof (no full rotation)Double-rocker; both moving links oscillate only
s = shortest link, l = longest link, p and q = the other two. For a crank-rocker (most common four-bar): fix the link adjacent to s, and s becomes a continuously-rotating crank while the opposite link rocks. The transmission angle (between coupler and rocker) should stay within 40°-140° for smooth force transfer; outside this range the mechanism binds. Four-bar linkages are the most common non-trivial mechanism in mechanical design: clamps, lifting arms, pantographs, treadles, sewing machine feed dogs, and folding furniture all derive from this geometry.
Slider-Crank & Scotch YokeTAB. 5D-3
ParameterSlider-CrankScotch Yoke
Motion profileApproximately sinusoidal (with second-harmonic distortion)Pure sinusoidal motion
Stroke length2 × crank radius2 × crank radius
Side load on sliderSignificant (creates piston rub)None (yoke runs in straight slot)
Wear patternCylinder walls, piston skirtsSlot surfaces, crank pin
Typical useInternal combustion engines, compressors, pumpsTest rigs, sewing machines, valve actuators
Connecting rod length / crank radius3-5× crank radius (typical)not applicable
Slider-crank is by far the more common because the angled connecting rod offers some thrust relief and packaging advantages. The downside is that crank-radius / rod-length ratio determines the asymmetry between the upper and lower halves of the stroke (the "second-harmonic" content). For a 3:1 rod-to-crank ratio, the slider spends more time near top-dead-center than bottom-dead-center, useful for engines (better combustion dwell) but a problem for pumps wanting symmetric flow. Scotch yoke gives pure sinusoidal motion but trades it for accelerated wear on the slot.
Cam Profile ReferenceTAB. 5D-4
ProfileAccelerationUse
Constant velocity (straight rise)Infinite at start and end (impact)Almost never used; introduces shock
Constant acceleration (parabolic)Step change at midpoint (jerk)Low-speed indexing; some rotary actuators
Simple harmonic motion (SHM)Sinusoidal; finite jerkMedium-speed cams, valve trains (older)
CycloidalSmooth sine; finite jerkHigh-speed cams, modern automotive cams
Modified sinusoidal / 4-5-6-7 polynomialOptimized; smooth jerkHigh-speed packaging, racing engines
Modified trapezoidalBounded acceleration, finite jerkRobotics, indexing automation
A cam profile is a function of crank angle. Define dwell-rise-dwell-fall-dwell (DRDFD) angles, then pick a motion law for each rise and fall. Avoid constant velocity (infinite jerk = shock load). For mid-speed work, SHM is acceptable. For high-speed (above a few hundred RPM), cycloidal or modified-sine reduce vibration. Pressure angle (between cam normal and follower travel) should stay below 30° for translating followers and below 45° for swinging followers; higher pressure angles cause binding and side load.
Geneva Drive & Indexing MechanismsTAB. 5D-5
MechanismMotionUse
Geneva drive (external)Continuous input → N intermittent steps per revolutionFilm projectors (24 frames/sec), indexing tables, watch movements
Geneva drive (internal)Same, more compact for small NIndexing where dwell > motion time
Star wheel + crankContinuous → stepped rotationBottling machines, package conveyors
Indexing cam (rotary)Continuous input → stepped output via cam groovesHigher accuracy than Geneva; CNC tool changers
Ratchet & pawlOscillating input → unidirectional stepped outputSocket wrenches, hoists, manual ratchets
Sprag clutch / one-way bearingBidirectional input → one direction transmittedBicycle freewheels, alternator pulleys, indexers
Geneva drive geometry: N slots, drive pin enters slot perpendicular to slot axis at start of motion. Number of indexed positions = number of slots. Dwell-to-motion ratio = (N − 2) / 2. For N = 4: equal dwell and motion. For N = 6: 2:1 dwell to motion (slow steady indexing). The drive pin must enter and leave each slot tangentially or jamming occurs; the locking arc on the driver matches the slot diameter for solid hold during dwell.
Latch & Detent MechanismsTAB. 5D-6
MechanismHold ForceRelease MechanismTypical Use
Spring-loaded detent (ball + spring + notch)Adjustable, low to mediumOvercoming spring with applied forceMode selectors, indexable handles, hex bit holders
Plunger detentMediumPull or push the plungerPin locks, sliding doors, equipment safety pins
Over-center latch (toggle clamp)High; self-lockingReverse the handle past dead-centerToggle clamps, suitcase latches, machinery covers
Hook latch (cantilever snap)Medium; geometry-dependentDeflect cantilever past undercutPlastic enclosures, snap fit assemblies
Cam lock (rotary)Medium-highRotate key or knobCabinet locks, mailboxes, doors
Draw latch (over-center with tension)High; adjustableLift the handleRoad cases, electrical enclosures, tool boxes
Push-push latch (heart cam)MediumPush again to releasePen retraction, modular furniture, electronics covers
Detent force = (spring rate) × (deflection at notch entry). For a 1mm ball in a 0.8mm-deep V-notch with a 5 N/mm spring: detent force ≈ 5 × 0.4 = 2 N. Increase by deeper notch, stronger spring, or larger ball, but watch wear on the notch. Over-center latches give the strongest hold per actuation force because they trade displacement for force at dead-center; they also fail catastrophically if the geometry is wrong (no hold or no release). Plastic snap latches are sized for finite life: enclosures opened daily should use over-center geometry, not cantilever snaps.

GPL-3.0 · M.B. Parks
